**Chapter 7: The Watch That Rewound Time**

It was the first day of seventh grade, and everything about it felt wrong. The fluorescent lights in the school hallway buzzed like a swarm of angry bees, the lockers smelled like old pizza and regret, and the new kid in the class—me—was already regretting my decision to wear the smartwatch my parents had given me as a birthday gift.

The watch was sleek, black, and slightly too flashy for a middle schooler. It had all the usual features—step counter, heart rate monitor, and a voice assistant—but there was something different about it. Something… *wrong*. I’d noticed it the day before, when I accidentally tapped the wrong button and my backpack appeared to flicker for a second, like it was caught between two moments.

I had dismissed it as a glitch. But then, on Monday morning, I found myself standing in the hallway, watching a group of girls laugh and point at me. I recognized them from the previous school year—back when I was still the quiet kid in the back of the classroom, not the one who had *accidentally* caused a fire in the cafeteria. I had forgotten how much people had changed since then. Or maybe I had just forgotten how much I had changed.

That’s when I noticed the watch.

It had a strange glow, almost like it was alive. I tapped the screen, and suddenly, the world *snapped*. The girls vanished, replaced by a version of the hallway from yesterday. I was standing in the same spot, but the clock on the wall read 7:58 AM instead of 8:04. I had *rewound* time.

At first, I thought it was a prank. I tried it again—this time, I rewound just a few minutes, enough to catch a missed bus. It worked. I grinned, thinking I’d discovered a secret superpower. But the next time I used it, something felt… off.

I was late for gym class again. I rewound time by ten minutes, just to be safe. When I arrived, the teacher was already halfway through the warm-up. But when I looked in the mirror, something was different. My face looked older—like I’d aged a few years in a matter of seconds. My voice had deepened, and my voice recorder app on the watch was now showing my age as 14 instead of 13.

I panicked. I tried to reverse it again, but the watch didn’t respond. I tapped the screen, but it just blinked. I was stuck in this new timeline, one where I looked like I was *old enough* to be in eighth grade. And worse—people were treating me differently.

During lunch, I tried to sit with my old group of friends, but they looked at me like I was a stranger. “Wait, isn’t that… *you*?” one of them asked. “You’ve… changed.” Another one laughed. “You’re like, *so* much older. Are you even *allowed* to be here?”

I didn’t know what to say. I had *rewound* time, but I hadn’t realized it was doing more than just changing the present—it was *aging* me. Each time I used the watch, I was losing time, but not in the way I thought. It wasn’t just seconds or minutes—it was *years*.

**Chapter 1: The Time-Twisting Watch**

It was the first day of seventh grade, and 12-year-old Jake Mercer felt like he was already running behind. His backpack was heavier than usual, stuffed with books, a lunch, and a brand-new smartwatch that his parents had gifted him for his birthday. It was a sleek black device with a glowing blue screen, and it promised to track his steps, monitor his heart rate, and even help him with homework. But Jake didn’t care much about any of that. What mattered was that it looked cool, and it was the only thing in his life that felt slightly magical.

As he walked through the crowded hallway of Maplewood Middle School, Jake fumbled with the watch, trying to figure out how to turn it on. He pressed the button, and the screen flickered to life, displaying a simple message: **"Welcome, Jake Mercer."**

“Cool,” he muttered, glancing at the time—8:47 a.m. “I’m gonna be late.”

Then, out of nowhere, he saw something strange. A kid from his class, named Tommy, was walking toward him, holding a crumpled paper ball and looking like he was about to cry.

Jake hesitated. “Hey, Tommy, are you okay?”

Tommy looked up, eyes red and puffy. “My mom said I couldn’t bring my pet goldfish to school. She said it’s not allowed. I had to throw him away. I didn’t mean to!”

Jake felt a pang of sympathy. “I get it. My dog died last year. It was really hard.”

Tommy sniffled. “Can we… can we be friends? I feel like everyone hates me.”

Jake nodded. “Sure. I’m Jake. We can be best friends.”

That was the moment the watch decided to act.

Without thinking, Jake pressed a button on the side of the watch. The screen flashed red, and suddenly, everything around him slowed down—like someone had hit pause on the world. Tommy’s tears stopped mid-swell, his face frozen in a mix of sadness and confusion. The hallway around them seemed to stretch, time bending in ways Jake couldn’t explain.

Then, with a soft *click*, everything snapped back to normal. Tommy was standing there, looking confused. “Wait… did I just… cry?”

Jake blinked. “You were crying. What’s going on?”

Tommy shrugged. “I don’t know. Maybe I’m just weird.”

Jake didn’t say anything. He just stared at his watch, heart racing. He had no idea what just happened. He thought it was a glitch. Or maybe a bug in the software.

But the next day, something changed.

Jake woke up early, exhausted, with a headache he couldn’t explain. His body felt heavier, like he’d run a marathon. When he looked in the mirror, he noticed something strange—his face looked… older. His skin was a bit more textured, and his voice had a deeper tone. He was still 12, but something about him felt… different.

He tried to brush it off. Maybe it was just stress. But the changes kept coming.

Some stared at him like he was a weirdo. Others avoided him, whispering behind his back. “He’s acting weird,” one girl said. “He’s like… changing.”

Jake didn’t know what to think. He checked the watch again, hoping it would explain everything. But it just displayed the time, as if nothing had happened.

Then, the real disaster happened.

She was carrying a notebook, and her face was red with anger. She looked like she was about to yell at someone.

Jake didn’t think. He pressed the watch again, hoping to rewind the moment before she entered the room.

The world froze again. Sarah’s steps slowed, her expression locked in place. Jake felt a strange warmth spreading through his body, like something was draining out of him. He had the urge to scream, but he couldn’t. The world snapped back to normal, and Sarah was standing in the doorway, confused and angry.

“What did you do?” she demanded, eyes wide.

Jake didn’t answer. He just stared at his watch, now glowing a faint red. His hands were shaking. He felt… older. Much older.

Everything went black.

But for 13-year-old Jordan Reyes, the real surprise wasn’t the new school or the awkwardness of fitting in. It was the smartwatch.

It had been a gift from his dad, wrapped in a box labeled *“For the Future”*—a phrase that now felt ominously prophetic. The watch looked sleek, futuristic, and slightly too advanced for a middle schooler. It had all the usual features: heart rate monitor, step counter, and a weather forecast. But when Jordan tapped the screen and said, “Hey Watch, rewind,” something strange happened.

The world around him blurred, like a photo being taken in slow motion. His backpack tumbled to the ground, the floor seemed to ripple, and then—*snap*—he was back in the moment, standing exactly where he had been, but something was different. His hands felt lighter, and the sound of his own heartbeat seemed… louder.

At first, he thought it was a glitch. He tapped the screen again, “Hey Watch, rewind,” and it worked. He could undo things—missed assignments, awkward conversations, even the time he tripped over his own feet during gym class. It was like having a do-over button. Jordan became obsessed.

He started using it every chance he got. He rewound his mom’s scolding when she caught him sneaking chips from the pantry, rewound the moment he accidentally spilled his lunch on a new friend’s shirt, even rewound the time he said something stupid during a presentation. Each time, the watch responded with a soft *click*, and the world snapped back into place.

But then, something changed.

It started subtly. Jordan noticed that his voice was deeper than it used to be. His voice crackled when he spoke, like he was aging. His hands felt heavier, and he had to adjust his backpack straps more often. His reflection in the mirror looked… different. Not unattractive, just… older.

At first, he blamed puberty. He was 13, after all. But when his best friend, Mia, asked him, “Wait, are you *trying* to sound like a grown-up? You’ve gone from a kid to a *man* in a week,” he froze.

Jordan tried to ignore it. He told himself it was just stress. But the changes kept coming. His voice deepened more, his body felt more mature, and his face—his face—was starting to look like someone else’s. People started treating him differently. His teachers called him “Mr. Reyes” now. His classmates gave him strange looks. Even his mom seemed… distant, like she was watching him from across a chasm.

One afternoon, while trying to rewind a moment where he had accidentally knocked over a stack of books in the library, Jordan lost control. He tapped the screen too fast, and instead of rewinding a few seconds, he rewound *hours*. The library disappeared around him, replaced by a version of the school that looked… wrong. The walls were darker, the lights flickered, and the students looked older, worn out, like they’d been through something terrible.

The timeline had shifted.

Jordan stumbled through the warped hallway, trying to make sense of it. The students around him whispered, their eyes wide with fear. A teacher approached him, and when she spoke, her voice was thick with something unnatural. “You shouldn’t be here,” she said. “This isn’t your time.”

Before he could react, the world snapped again—this time, not back to the present, but to a moment *before* he had ever touched the watch. He was standing in the hallway of his old school, the one from last year, surrounded by students who looked like they hadn’t changed in months.

But something was off. His body felt… wrong. He looked down and saw that his face was still changing, like he was caught in the middle of a transformation. His hands trembled. The watch was still on his wrist, glowing faintly, as if it knew what it had done.

The timeline had shifted, and now it was up to Jordan to fix it.

But the only way to undo the damage was to rewind again—this time, all the way back to the beginning. And that meant using the watch one more time, even if it meant losing himself in the process.

With a deep breath, Jordan tapped the screen one last time.

“Hey Watch, rewind.”

She’d changed everything.

**Chapter 1: The Watch That Rewinds Time**

Lena Hart had always been an average middle schooler—average height, average grades, and average luck. That is, until the day she opened the box from her parents, who had gifted her a sleek, black smartwatch for her 12th birthday. It looked like any other smartwatch, with a glowing screen and a few buttons, but something about it felt… different. The moment she tapped the screen and said, “Hey Watch,” it responded with a soft chime and a cheerful “Happy Birthday, Lena!”

At first, she thought it was just a fancy gadget. But then, late one night, after a particularly frustrating math quiz, she tapped the screen again and muttered, “I wish I could do that over.” The watch blinked, and suddenly, the room around her shifted. The clock on the wall ticked backward. Her phone screen flickered, and before she could react, the world snapped back to normal—except it was *earlier* that day.

She blinked, confused. “Did I… time travel?” she whispered. She checked her phone—time had gone back *four minutes*. She laughed, half in disbelief, half in excitement. “This thing’s magic!”

Over the next few days, Lena experimented. She rewound time to catch the bus, to avoid embarrassment in class, and even to redo her homework. Each time, the watch responded with a soft hum, and time would rewind a few minutes. It was like having a second chance—until one day, she noticed something strange.

After rewinding to fix a spilled coffee on her shirt, she caught a glimpse of her reflection in the hallway mirror. Her face looked… different. Slightly more mature. Her voice had a deeper tone, and her skin had a faint sheen, like she’d been sunbathing all day. She frowned. “Did I… grow up?”

She dismissed it as a trick of the light, but the next day, it happened again. This time, she was caught by her best friend, Maya, who looked at her with a mix of confusion and concern. “Lena, are you… *different*?”

Lena laughed it off. “I just slept weird last night.” But deep down, she was scared. She started to notice more changes—her voice cracking when she sang, her voice getting deeper, her body growing a little faster than expected. She was *aging* every time she rewound time.

It wasn’t until she accidentally rewound during lunch that things got worse. She had just finished a sandwich when she saw a boy in the corner of the cafeteria—someone she barely knew—look up at her and say, “Hey, that’s not you.”

Lena froze. “What?”

The boy shrugged. “You used to be shorter. And your hair was longer. You’ve… changed.”

She didn’t know what to say. She tried to laugh it off, but the realization hit her: every time she rewound time, she was aging. And she wasn’t just aging—she was *accelerating* through puberty.

The next day, she tried to be careful. She rewound only when absolutely necessary. But it wasn’t enough. Her body kept changing, and people started treating her differently. Teachers gave her more respect, as if she were older. Boys started staring at her in a way that made her uncomfortable. Even her mom and dad seemed… distant, like they were looking at someone else when she walked into the room.

Then came the incident that changed everything.

It was during a school field trip to the science museum. Lena had been excited to see the planetarium, but when she got there, she realized she had forgotten her lunch. She tried to rewind time to fix it, but something went wrong. The watch glitched, and instead of going back a few minutes, it rewound *too far*—back to before the trip even started.

When she opened her eyes, she was standing in the school parking lot, but everything was *wrong*. The sky was darker, the trees looked twisted, and the students around her were dressed in outdated uniforms. The world had shifted. Time had been rewritten.

People looked at her in confusion. A group of kids ran past her, whispering. “Who is that?” one asked. “She looks… *off*.”

Lena realized what had happened. She had caused a *timeline shift*. The world wasn’t the same anymore. And now, she was stuck in a version of reality where she didn’t belong.

Panic set in. She tried to rewind again, but the watch didn’t respond. It was broken—or maybe it had been *used up*. She was running out of time.

Then, she saw it: a stray cat sitting near the school, watching her with an uncanny, knowing look. It had been there every time she rewound. And now, it seemed to understand.

Lena’s heart pounded. She remembered the first time she saw the cat—right after she first used the watch. It had stared at her like it knew something she didn’t. Now, it looked like it was *waiting* for her.

She had to fix this. She had to reverse the clock once more—not just for herself, but for the world she had accidentally broken.

I took a deep breath, stepped out of the bathroom, and walked toward my room. I had to fix things. Not with time, but with *me*. I had to learn from the mistakes I’d made. And maybe, just maybe, I could still make things right.

**Chapter 6: The Time-Track**

It started with a simple glitch.  

On the first day of seventh grade, Jamie Taylor had received a sleek, black smartwatch as a birthday gift from their older brother. It was supposed to be a cool gadget—tracking steps, heart rate, and even notifying them when they were running late. But when Jamie accidentally tapped the wrong button during lunch, something strange happened.  

The cafeteria around them blurred, like a scene from a movie on fast-forward. Then, just as suddenly as it had begun, everything snapped back. Jamie blinked, looking around at their classmates, who were still eating their sandwiches and laughing. No one seemed to notice anything was off.  

“Did I just… rewind time?” Jamie whispered, staring at the watch.  

Their brother had told them it was a “smartwatch with a twist.” Jamie had rolled their eyes at the time, but now, as they stared at the glowing screen, they couldn’t shake the feeling that something was very, very different.  

That night, Jamie tested it again—this time on purpose. They tapped the button once, and the room dissolved into a blur. When it stopped, they were back in their bedroom, the clock still showing the same time. But something felt… off.  

They looked in the mirror. Their face looked… younger. Not just a few minutes younger, but like they had skipped a few years. Their skin was smoother, their voice a little higher. It wasn’t a big change, but it was enough to make them pause.  

“Maybe I just slept too much,” Jamie thought, trying to convince themselves.  

But the next day, things got weirder.  

During lunch, Jamie noticed that their voice was cracking at odd moments—like when they laughed or tried to speak loudly. It wasn’t the typical puberty thing, though. It was happening randomly, as if time itself was messing with their body.  

At first, Jamie thought it was just nerves. But when their best friend, Mia, leaned in and said, “Hey, are you… *getting* a voice like a boy?” Jamie froze.  

“N-no, I’m not,” they stammered, but the words came out too high-pitched, like they were trying to mimic someone else.  

Mia shrugged. “Maybe you’re just stressed. You’ve been weird all week.”  

But Jamie knew it wasn’t stress. It was the watch.  

They started using it more often, trying to fix things—rewinding a bad moment in class, undoing a clumsy trip, even skipping a test they hadn’t studied for. Each time, they felt a little younger. Their body seemed to be reversing, like time was working backward on them.  

One afternoon, Jamie decided to test the limits. They were in the school hallway, about to be late for a class, when they tapped the button. The world twisted, and suddenly, they were back in the hallway—but it was *different*.  

The walls were a different color. The lockers had new stickers. The principal was wearing a different shirt.  

Jamie’s heart pounded. “I’ve changed the timeline.”  

It wasn’t just the environment. People were acting differently too. A teacher they had never seen before was standing by the door, and a classmate who had always been quiet was now shouting at someone.  

Jamie tried to rewind again, but the watch didn’t respond. The screen was black.  

Panic set in. “I’ve messed everything up,” they muttered.  

They ran through the hallway, trying to find someone who could help. But the school was unfamiliar. No one recognized them. They were a ghost in a world that no longer fit.  

Then, they saw it—someone from their old timeline.  

A boy from their class, Jake, was standing in front of the principal’s office, looking confused. Jamie’s stomach dropped.  

“Jake?” they called out.  

Jake turned. “Who the hell are you?”  

Jamie’s heart sank. This wasn’t their Jake. This Jake was older, with a scar on his cheek. And he looked *scared*.  

Jamie realized what had happened. By rewinding time, they had accidentally shifted the timeline, creating a parallel reality where things had gone wrong in a different way. The original timeline—the one they remembered—was gone.  

They were trapped in a version of their world that had been altered by their actions.  

Desperate, Jamie tried the watch again. It was still broken. But then, something strange happened.  

The screen flickered. A new option appeared: **"Reverse All."**  

Jamie hesitated, but there was no other choice. They tapped the button.  

The world exploded in light.  

When it faded, Jamie was back in their old classroom. The walls were the same color. The lockers had the same stickers. Jake was sitting at his desk, smiling at them like nothing had happened.  

But Jamie felt… older.  

Their voice was lower. Their skin was rougher. They were *older*.  

They looked in the mirror. Their reflection was… different. Not just older, but *changed*.  

“Wait,” they whispered. “Did I… age?”  

It wasn’t just the watch anymore. It had done something irreversible.

It came from the wrist of 13-year-old Mia Torres, who had just received her new smartwatch for her birthday. The watch was sleek, black, and covered in glowing blue icons. It looked high-tech, and her mom had insisted it was the “latest in safety and productivity.” Mia had rolled her eyes, but now, as she sat in the cafeteria at Creekwood Middle School, the watch was doing something strange.

The beeps were coming in bursts—three, then two, then five. She glanced at the screen, but nothing was displayed. Just a blank white space with the word “ERROR” blinking in red. She ignored it, stuffing a sandwich into her mouth and pretending to listen to her friend Lola’s latest drama about the school’s new principal.

That was until the watch started *rewinding*.

Write a chapter where a middle school student discovers their new smart watch has the ability to rewind time, but doesn't realize that each use is aging them until they notice physical changes (like accelerating through puberty) and that people are treating them differently. **Chapter 6: The Watch That Rewound Time**

It all started with a birthday gift—something simple, yet extraordinary. When 13-year-old Jake Mercer opened the sleek, black smartwatch on his wrist, he thought it was just another gadget from his tech-savvy older brother, Ethan. But as he tapped the screen, a soft chime echoed through the room, and the second hand on his watch began to move *backward*.

At first, he thought it was a glitch. He tapped it again, and the time reset to the moment he’d just been in. Confused but intrigued, Jake tried it again—this time, he rewound the clock by ten minutes. He watched as his mom walked into the kitchen, then back out, and when he blinked, she was gone. He’d just seen her moments ago.

He laughed it off. Maybe it was a cool feature. But as days passed, Jake began to notice strange things—his teacher, Ms. Patel, seemed to be repeating lessons. His best friend, Maya, looked like she’d seen something he hadn’t. Even his reflection in the mirror seemed… different. He’d catch himself looking older, but only for a second.

Then came the changes.

At first, they were subtle. His voice deepened a little. His voice cracks, which he thought was just puberty. But then, one morning, he woke up with a full beard. He panicked, staring at his face in the mirror. “What the—?” He tried to brush it off, but his mom noticed.

“Jake, are you sure you’re sleeping enough?” she asked, concern etched on her face.

“I’m fine,” he muttered, avoiding eye contact.

But the real problem wasn’t the beard. It was the way people were treating him. At school, kids who used to call him “kid” now referred to him as “dude.” His classmates whispered when he walked by, and even his teacher seemed… *older*. Ms. Patel, who had always been in her early thirties, now looked like she was in her forties. Jake realized with a sinking feeling that something was wrong.

He tried to figure it out. Maybe the watch was broken. Maybe it was a prank. But then, during lunch, he saw something that made his stomach drop.

A boy from his class—Liam—was arguing with another student, Kyle. It looked like a typical fight, but when Jake rewound the clock by five minutes, he saw Liam and Kyle arguing *again*—the same argument, the same words, the same expression. It was a loop.

Jake gasped. “Oh no.” He hadn’t just changed time—he’d *broken* it.

The consequences were immediate. The classroom became a mess. People were repeating the same actions, caught in loops of déjà vu. Teachers complained about the same lesson being taught over and over again. Some students began to act strangely, as if they were stuck in a dream. Even the principal, Mr. Rogers, looked confused and slightly panicked.

“Something’s wrong,” he said, pacing the hallway. “I’ve had this exact same conversation with the staff three times today.”

Jake knew he had to fix it. But how? The watch had stopped working after the first time he tried to rewind, and now it was just a lifeless black rectangle on his wrist. He had no idea how to reverse the changes—or if it was even possible.

Desperate, he tried one last time to rewind. He held his breath, pressed the button, and… nothing. The watch was silent. The time stood still. The classroom was frozen in a loop of repetition.

He felt his body start to change again. His skin was growing rougher, his bones aching. He was aging. Fast.

“Jake!” Maya appeared, her face pale. “You’re… you’re not the same. What happened?”

He tried to explain, but his voice cracked, and he could barely form words. “I… I messed up. I messed up time.”

She grabbed his hand. “We have to fix it. We have to go back.”

But how?

Then, as if the universe had finally decided to listen, Jake noticed something. The watch wasn’t broken. It was just *inactive*. He had used it too much, and it had run out of power. But if he could find a way to recharge it… maybe he could rewind everything again.

He ran to the school’s tech lab, where the principal’s assistant, Mr. Chen, was tinkering with a charging station. “Hey,” Jake said, breathless. “Can I charge my watch?”

Mr. Chen looked up, confused. “You’re not supposed to have that. It’s against school policy.”

“I don’t have a choice,” Jake said, holding up the watch. “I think I’ve messed up time.”

For a moment, Mr. Chen hesitated. Then he sighed. “Fine. But if this thing causes any more problems, I’m calling the police.”

Jake nodded and plugged the watch in. The screen flickered to life. A small red light blinked. He pressed the button. The second hand began to move backward.

Time rewound.

**Chapter 12: The Clockwork Curse**

The first time it happened, I thought it was a glitch.

It was the first day of school, and I had just received my new smartwatch as a birthday gift from my dad. It was sleek, black, and had all the latest features: heart rate monitor, step counter, even a voice assistant. I checked the time—**7:45 a.m.**—and then I noticed something odd. The screen flickered, and suddenly, the time changed to **7:44 a.m.**

I blinked. I pressed the screen again. Nothing. I looked around, half-expecting someone else to have noticed. But the hallway was empty, save for a few lockers and a janitor mopping with a long, slow sigh. I shrugged it off, thinking maybe I had hit a button by accident.

It happened again the next day. This time, I was in homeroom, and the clock above the door read **8:30 a.m.** I looked down at my watch. It said **8:29 a.m.** I frowned. I tapped the screen, and the time changed again—**8:28 a.m.** I couldn’t stop it. It was like the watch had a mind of its own.

At first, I figured it was just a weird feature. But then, during lunch, I noticed something strange. My voice had gotten deeper. Not just a little, but noticeably so. My voice was starting to sound like my older brother’s. I looked in the mirror and saw my face—still mine, but somehow… more mature. My skin had a slight sheen, like I hadn’t slept in days. I wasn’t sure what was going on, but I couldn’t stop the watch.

The third time it happened, I was in the hallway again, trying to figure out how to undo it. I hit the rewind button, and—*poof*—time reset. But the change didn’t. I was still older. I started to realize the truth. Every time I used the rewind function, I wasn’t just going back in time—I was aging. My body was changing faster than it should. My voice deepened, my face grew more defined, and I began to feel like I was… *growing up* way too fast.

I just nodded and tried to laugh it off.

The coach was different too. And—most terrifyingly—the lockers had shifted again, but this time, they weren’t just one position off. They were all out of order, like someone had scrambled them.

**Chapter 12: The Watch That Rewinds Time**

It started with a simple thing—a new smartwatch, sleek and silver, wrapped in bright blue packaging. Jake Mercer had been waiting for this for weeks. His parents had finally relented, agreeing to let him get the latest model after he promised to use it responsibly. He’d never been one for gadgets, but the watch looked cool, and it had all the bells and whistles: heart rate monitor, GPS, voice assistant, and… something else. A feature he didn’t notice until the first time he accidentally hit the wrong button.

It happened during gym class. Jake had been trying to beat his own time on the treadmill when he fumbled with the watch’s interface. A flash of light, a strange hum, and suddenly the world around him slowed down. He blinked, and the class had already moved on to the next activity. He’d been *back*—not just a few seconds earlier, but *hours*. His heart pounded. He checked the watch. It read 10:03 AM instead of 11:12. He laughed, thinking it was a glitch. But then he noticed something odd—his reflection in the mirror was different. His face looked… older. Not much, but enough to make him pause.

At first, he thought it was just stress. After all, he had a lot on his plate: middle school, group projects, and the constant pressure to fit in. But the next day, he noticed it again. His reflection in the bathroom mirror showed him with a few more freckles, a slightly deeper voice, and—most unnerving—his hands were starting to look more like those of a teenager. He shrugged it off, telling himself it was just puberty kicking in. But then he saw the photos.

The house was old, built in the 1950s, and filled with family heirlooms. Jake’s grandmother had left them a collection of old photos—black and white, sepia-toned, faded with time. He’d always found them interesting, but now they were changing. The first time he noticed, he was looking at a picture of his grandfather as a young man, standing in front of the house with a smile. The photo had been taken in 1973. Jake had always thought he looked older in that picture, but now, as he stared at it, he saw something strange: his grandfather’s face was *younger* than it had been before. And Jake—standing in the background—looked *taller*.

He gasped and stepped back. The photo hadn’t changed. It was still the same. But the effect was real. He looked down at his own hands and realized he was growing. Not just a little—he was *accelerating*.

At school, things started to get weird. His friends noticed. “Hey, Jake, you look… different,” said Mia, his best friend. “Like, *older*.” He tried to brush it off, but the comment stuck. Then came the whispers. “Is he sick?” “Does he have a tumor?” “He’s acting weird.” Jake tried to ignore them, but the stares and questions made him anxious. He started to feel like a ghost, like he didn’t belong in his own body.

The watch was the only thing that made sense. He spent hours staring at it, trying to figure out what was happening. The more he used it, the more he noticed the changes. Each rewind made him older. And each time he rewound, he got closer to something… *unwanted*. He had a moment where he accidentally rewound during lunch, and when he returned, he found himself in the middle of a class discussion. He wasn’t just *back*—he was *in the past*. The teacher, the students, even the clock on the wall—everything was different. The classroom was quieter, the desks were arranged differently, and his best friend Mia wasn’t even in the class anymore. She had been transferred to another school a year earlier.

Jake panicked. He tried to rewind again, but the watch didn’t respond. It was like it had broken. Or like it had *locked* him in this timeline. He was stuck. He had changed things. People he knew were gone. His life was unraveling.

Desperate, he tried to find a way to fix it. He searched the internet late at night, scrolling through forums and videos, hoping someone had had the same experience. Nothing. The watch had no manual, no instructions, no explanation. It just *worked*. And it had *cost* him.

Then, late one night, he found the photos again. The ones in the house. He had always thought they were just old family pictures, but now they were *changing*—not just in the way he had seen before. Some showed him as a baby, others as a toddler, and one, from the 1980s, showed a boy who looked *exactly* like him, standing in front of the house with his parents. But there was something else in that photo. A *shadow* behind him. A figure, tall and looming, watching.

Jake’s breath caught. He looked down at himself. He was *too tall* for that photo.

**Chapter 6: The Black Market Contract**

The air in the old biotech warehouse was thick with the smell of rust, mildew, and something faintly metallic—like blood, or maybe just the residue of too many experiments. The settlement of *New Greenhaven* clung to the edges of this crumbling structure, its makeshift tents and solar panels barely holding against the relentless wind. Here, in this forgotten corner of the old world, life was a delicate balance of survival and defiance.

I adjusted the gloves on my hands, fingers trembling slightly—not from fear, but from the strain of holding back. The lab was a patchwork of salvaged equipment: a repurposed MRI machine acting as a centrifuge, a jury-rigged PCR setup built from a defunct coffee machine and a salvaged DNA sequencer, and a fridge that only worked half the time. Every piece was a relic of a world that had once promised progress, now reduced to tools of desperation.

The contract had come in the form of a sealed data packet, encrypted and delivered by a courier who vanished into the night. It was simple: a request for a spliced gene sequence from a pre-collapse strain of *Oryza sativa*, the rice plant. The client wanted a crop that could survive in the acidic soil of the eastern wastes, where radiation had long since turned the land into a graveyard. The catch? The genetic material had to be viable for at least three generations—or they'd take it from us.

I stared at the screen, the code flashing like a dying heartbeat. The DNA sequence was fragmented, degraded beyond repair in most parts. Corporate genetic engineering had been designed with built-in obsolescence—engineered DNA that lasted only one generation to ensure repeat purchases. Now, in this new world, those same corporations were long gone, and the survivors had to piece together what little remained.

I exhaled slowly, then reached for the PCR machine. It was unstable—too many wires had been spliced together, and the heating element flickered like a dying star. But it was the only thing we had that could amplify the weak genetic fragments. I loaded the sample, a sliver of rice DNA I’d managed to salvage from a buried cache in the ruins of a lab outside the settlement. It was barely enough.

As the machine whirred to life, I leaned against the rusted counter, watching the glowing indicators flicker. The physical toll of this work was constant. My hands were scarred from repeated exposure to mutagenic chemicals, my skin patchy with the remnants of failed experiments. The untested genetic modifications I’d used as medicine had left me with a strange immunity to disease, but also an unpredictable sensitivity to radiation. Sometimes, I’d wake up with a fever that would vanish in hours, or find my fingers tingling with an energy that felt both foreign and familiar.

This was the cost of survival in a world where nature had been rewritten—and then abandoned.

The PCR cycle took nearly six hours. When it finished, I examined the results under the makeshift microscope. The DNA fragments had amplified, but they were still unstable. Too many mutations had crept in during the degradation process. I could splice them together, but it would take time, and the risk was high. If the resulting crop failed, we’d have nothing to eat for the next growing season.

I sat back, rubbing my temples. The contract wasn’t just a request—it was a demand. The client had no idea what they were asking for. In this world, even the promise of a single harvest could mean the difference between life and death.

I reached for the old journal I kept, filled with notes and failed experiments. One entry caught my eye: *“Tested modified gene sequence on self. Symptoms: fever, fatigue, increased cellular turnover. Possible enhancement in metabolic efficiency.”* I had once used a modified gene from a pre-collapse strain of *Escherichia coli* as a makeshift antibiotic. It had saved my life, but at a cost. It was both a curse and a gift.

I made the decision. I would splice the rice DNA with a modified gene from the *E. coli* strain, creating a hybrid that could withstand the harsh conditions and potentially boost the plant's growth rate. It was a gamble, but it was the only option.

The lab equipment groaned as I assembled the new setup. The PCR machine was barely holding together, and the genetic sequencer had started to short out. I had to work quickly, using a combination of old software and improvised code to align the sequences. My hands shook as I inserted the final primer.

The machine whirred to life again, and for a moment, I felt something I hadn’t in years—a flicker of hope.

When the cycle ended, I examined the results. The hybrid DNA was unstable, but it was stable enough. I could plant it and see what happened. I’d have to monitor the plants closely, but it was a start.

**Chapter 6: The Last Harvest**

The air in the ruins of Old Memphis smelled like rust and decay. The once-thriving city had long since collapsed under the weight of corporate greed and environmental collapse. Now, it was a graveyard of abandoned skyscrapers and rusted cars, where scavengers and biohackers like me fought for survival. My name is Jax, and I’m a biohacker in a world where DNA was engineered to die with its host—designed to expire after a single generation, ensuring that corporate profits never slowed.

It wasn’t just the war or the climate that brought society down—it was the genetic time bomb. Every organism, from crops to livestock, was engineered with a self-destruct sequence. The corporations called it “biological obsolescence.” It maximized their profits by forcing people to keep buying new seeds, new animals, new life. When the system collapsed, the world didn’t just die—it *rotted* from the inside out.

I live in a repurposed lab beneath the old city hall, a space I’ve jury-rigged from what’s left of the old biotech facilities. The walls are lined with salvaged equipment—old PCR machines, gene sequencers, and protein synthesizers, all jury-rigged to function with what little power I can siphon from the city’s decaying grid. The air is thick with the smell of ozone and old electronics, and the only light comes from a flickering holographic display that shows my latest genetic experiment.

Today, I’ve got a contract. A black market deal, brokered through a network of underground traders. Someone wants a strain of wheat that can survive in the post-collapse soil—something that doesn’t die after a year. The catch? The genetic material I need is from a pre-collapse seed bank, buried deep in the ruins of a now-abandoned agricultural research facility. It’s a dangerous mission, and the price is high—enough to buy me a month of food and power.

I take a small team of scavengers and a former lab technician, Kira, who’s more familiar with the ruins than I am. We move through the skeletal remains of the facility, dodging collapsing beams and the occasional stray drone. The seed bank is sealed with a biometric lock, long since fried by the collapse, but Kira finds a way in using a hacked fingerprint scanner from a nearby junkyard.

Inside, the air is thick with dust and the scent of old genetics. Shelves lined with glass vials hold samples of what once were the world’s most advanced crops—maize, wheat, soy, all engineered for maximum yield but with a built-in expiration. I pull out a vial of wheat DNA, its label faded but still legible: *Triticum aestivum, Genotype X-7, Shelf Life: 1 generation.*

I run a quick PCR test in my makeshift lab, and the results are grim—most of the DNA has degraded beyond repair. But there’s one sample that shows partial viability. It’s a strain of wheat that was modified for drought resistance, but the genetic code is fractured. It won’t grow on its own, not without modification.

Back in my lab, I set up a crude gene splicing rig, using a salvaged CRISPR module and a makeshift incubator built from a combination of old HVAC units and repurposed cooling systems. The process is slow, and the results are uncertain. I splice the surviving genetic code with a fragment of a more resilient strain I found in a nearby settlement, hoping to create something that can survive the poisoned soil and the erratic climate.

The work takes hours, and my hands are stained with old blood and biofluids. The genetic modifications are untested, and I know the risks. In the past, I’ve used unregulated gene edits as medicine—patching wounds with modified bacteria, or using synthetic proteins to fight infections. It’s risky, and it often leads to unintended side effects. My body is a patchwork of experimental modifications, some of which I’m not sure I can live with.

But today, it works. The wheat seed begins to sprout in the incubator, its roots twisting through the soil I’ve managed to scavenge and purify. It’s not perfect—it’s weak, and the growth rate is slow—but it’s alive. And that’s all I need.

The contract pays well, but it’s not just about the money. This wheat could mean the difference between survival and starvation for the settlement I’m trying to rebuild. We’re not just scavengers anymore—we’re trying to grow something new, something that doesn’t die with us.

As I sit in the dim light of my lab, watching the first green shoots pierce through the soil, I think about the corporations that designed all of this. They never thought about the long-term, only the profit. But now, in the ruins of their empire, we’re learning to survive not by following their rules, but by breaking them.

The world is broken, but it’s not beyond repair. And maybe, just maybe, we’re starting to build something better.

Above, the sun hung low, casting long shadows over the cluttered workbench where Elara Voss hunched over a cluster of humming, jury-rigged machines.

She had spent years scavenging, learning, and surviving in this world where genetic engineering had been the last great illusion. Once, corporations had promised a utopia of tailored crops, disease-free livestock, and perfect humans. But that was before the collapse. Before the engineered DNA started to decay, programmed to last only one generation—designed for profit, not survival.

Now, the remnants of that world were nothing more than junk. And Elara had become a master of salvaging what little still worked.

---

The contract had come in the form of a sealed data chip, embedded in a rusted cigarette lighter. She’d found it tucked in the pocket of a dead security guard near the old BioNova Research Facility. The message was simple: *“Need viable crop spores. Pay in tech. Meet at 03:00, Dock 7.”*

Elara had no idea who was behind it, but she knew one thing—food was the most valuable currency in this world. The settlement she and her group had carved out of the ruins of Old Phoenix was barely holding together. The soil was poisoned, the water unreliable, and the last of the genetically modified seeds had long since turned to dust.

She had spent the past week trying to extract usable genetic material from a salvaged PCR machine, one of the few still functional in the city. It was a relic—built for precision, now repurposed with scrap parts and duct tape. The machine’s cooling system had been jury-rigged using a refrigerator from a collapsed grocery store, and its amplification chambers were lined with salvaged silicon wafers and copper wiring.

The process was slow and dangerous. She had to extract DNA from a rotting sample of a once-viable wheat strain, then try to amplify it just long enough to create a spore. The degradation was relentless—every hour, the genetic code frayed like old rope. But Elara had learned to work with what she had.

---

The meeting was at Dock 7, a crumbling industrial complex where the water was still running and the air was thick with the stench of decay. Elara arrived just as the sun dipped below the horizon, casting the dock in eerie twilight. A figure waited by a rusted shipping container, cloaked in a patchwork coat of old lab coats and military surplus.

“Elara Voss?” the figure asked, voice muffled by a respirator.

“Yeah. You got the chip?”

The figure nodded, pulling a small pouch from their pocket. Inside were tiny, shimmering spores—somehow still viable. “This is from a strain called *Triticum Resilientis*, last seen in a lab near New Memphis. It’s supposed to resist both drought and radiation. You’re the only one I trust with it.”

Elara took the pouch carefully, her fingers brushing against the delicate grains. “How do you know I won’t use it to make a weapon?”

The figure smirked. “Because I’ve seen what you do. You’re not a killer. You’re a survivor.”

Elara didn’t reply. She had no need to. She knew the truth—the world had long since abandoned morality. What mattered was survival.

She tucked the spores into a vial, then pulled out a small handheld sequencer she’d built from a broken DNA analyzer and a salvaged microchip. She ran a quick scan, cross-referencing the genetic markers with her own database of degraded strains.

The results were promising. The spores had a 42% viability rate—better than anything she’d managed in weeks. But the data was incomplete. The genetic code had suffered significant fragmentation, and without a full sequence, there was no way to ensure it would grow properly.

Still, it was a start.

---

Back at the lab, Elara worked through the night, feeding the spores into the PCR machine, trying to amplify their genetic material. The machine whirred and buzzed, its lights flickering like a dying heartbeat. She had to inject stabilizing agents made from a mix of algae and synthetic polymers—something she had cobbled together from a collapsed aquaponics system.

The process was grueling. Every time the machine failed, she had to recalibrate, replace parts, and hope the next run would be better. Her hands were stained with chemicals, her eyes bloodshot from sleepless nights. But the physical toll was nothing compared to the risks.

She had once used a similar process to create a makeshift antibiotic from a degraded strain of *Streptomyces*. It had worked—at first. The infection had cleared, but within days, the patient had developed an aggressive mutation, their body rejecting the foreign DNA. Elara had watched them die, unable to save them. It had changed her. She now approached genetic modifications with extreme caution

**Chapter 8: The Last Harvest**

The air in *Green Haven* smelled of rust and decay. Once a thriving research facility, the old biotech lab now served as the heart of the settlement—its walls lined with scavenged equipment, its floors strewn with broken glass and half-erased lab notes. The sky above was a dull gray, perpetually choked with dust and smoke from distant fires. It was a world where survival depended on ingenuity, and where every breath was a gamble.

Jax Veyne stood hunched over a makeshift PCR machine, its components cobbled together from a salvaged lab kit and a repurposed toaster. The machine hummed weakly, its heating element flickering like a dying heartbeat. Jax’s hands, calloused and stained with old DNA samples, adjusted the temperature settings with a practiced eye. They had only one shot at this—*one cycle*—and it had to work.

The contract had come through a cryptic message on a dead satellite link: **"Seed the future. Pay in blood."** No name, no location, just a code—**VIRIDIS-7**. Jax knew what that meant. *VIRIDIS-7* was a prototype gene-editing tool, designed for corporate agriculture, capable of splicing resilient traits into crops that could survive in poisoned soil. But it had been abandoned years ago, deemed too unstable for mass use. The only reason it still existed was because someone had hidden it deep in the ruins of *New Eden Biotech*, where Jax and their crew had scoured for supplies months before.

Now, with the settlement’s last food stores nearly depleted, Jax had no choice but to take the job. The payment was vague—*a vial of something*—but the stakes were clear. If they failed, Green Haven would starve. If they succeeded, they might just have a chance to survive.

The lab was a patchwork of scavenged parts. A centrifuge made from an old car engine, a DNA sequencer powered by a solar panel and a dead laptop battery, and a makeshift incubator built from a refrigerator and a heat gun. Everything was jury-rigged, unreliable, and constantly failing. But Jax had learned to work with it. They’d spent years surviving in this world, where the only law was necessity.

The genetic material they had was barely viable. A few strands of rice DNA, harvested from a half-decayed seed, and a fragment of wheat from a corpse’s stomach. It wasn’t enough. Jax needed to amplify it, to make it strong enough to be spliced with the VIRIDIS-7 gene kit. But the PCR machine wasn’t working properly. The temperature fluctuated wildly, and the reagents had long since degraded.

“Come on,” Jax muttered, adjusting the heat element with a pair of pliers. “You’re not giving up on me now.”

The machine coughed to life, the temperature rising in uneven pulses. Jax watched the gel electrophoresis tray, waiting for the telltale bands of DNA to appear. If they could get a clean sample, they could splice it with the VIRIDIS-7 code and create something new—something *alive*.

Hours passed. The lab was cold, and Jax’s fingers were numb. They had to stay alert. The last time they’d used untested genetic modifications, it had nearly killed them. A failed splicing attempt had caused a rapid mutation in their blood cells, leading to a fever that had taken days to break. The body wasn’t meant to handle such instability. But there were *unexpected* upsides too. The mutation had given Jax an increased resistance to certain toxins, a side effect that had proven invaluable in the wastelands.

Now, they were gambling again.

The PCR machine finally spat out a faint band of DNA. It wasn’t perfect, but it was *something*. Jax grabbed the sample and rushed to the workstation, where the VIRIDIS-7 gene kit lay dormant in a rusted metal box. The kit was a mess—half the components were missing, and the instructions were faded. But Jax had studied enough corporate genetic engineering to know what to do.

They inserted the DNA into the gene splicer, a device that looked like a cross between a toaster and a soldering iron. The machine whirred to life, emitting a low hum as it began to weave the genetic code together. Jax watched closely, heart pounding. If this failed, there would be no second chance.

The splicing completed in a flash. The resulting strand was unstable, glowing faintly with a bioluminescent marker—*a sign of successful modification*. Jax exhaled, shaky with relief. They had done it.

Now came the hard part: testing it. They planted a few seeds in the makeshift greenhouse, a haphazard structure of salvaged plastic and old solar panels. The soil was poisoned, but the modified DNA was supposed to make the plants resilient. They waited, fingers crossed.

Days passed. The plants didn’t sprout. Jax checked the soil, adjusted the pH, tried another batch. Nothing.

Then, on the seventh day, a single green shoot broke through the dirt.

It was weak, fragile, but alive.

And if the spliced organism failed, it could contaminate the rest of the food supply—or worse, mutate into something unrecognizable.

The soil was a mix of ash, debris, and whatever had fallen from the sky over the years. She carefully dug a small hole and planted the seed mixture, covering it with a thin layer of ash to suppress the radiation.

She stepped back, watching as the first shoots began to emerge—slowly, uncertainly. The plants were twisted, their leaves a sickly green, but they were growing. It wasn’t the golden rice of old, but it was something. A step forward in a world that had been designed to take one step back.

It was a miracle and a curse. The same thing happened with the spliced crops: they grew, they thrived, but they mutated unpredictably.

Still, I had no choice. The contract was clear. The people in the greenhouse needed something—something that would last.

You don’t know what’s in there.”

Kael didn’t look up. “We don’t have time for caution, Tara. The last harvest failed. The soybeans turned to mold before they could even sprout. If we don’t get something viable soon, we’ll be eating insects and dust.”

Tara sighed, but she knew he was right. The world had collapsed in the early 2020s, not from war or climate, but from a quiet, insidious collapse—corporate genetic engineering designed with **built-in obsolescence**. Genovate’s patented seeds, designed for maximum profit, were engineered to last only one generation. Once the DNA degraded, the crops failed, and the system collapsed. Now, nearly a generation later, the world was starving, and the only hope lay in salvaging what little genetic material was left.

Kael reached for a **cracked canister**—one of the many empty containers once used by Genovate. It had no label, no expiration date, just the faint imprint of a logo that had long since faded. He had found it buried in the rubble of an abandoned lab, along with a handful of other similarly useless containers. But to Kael, they were treasure troves. He had spent weeks purifying the contents, hoping to extract something useful.

“This one’s got traces of a **modified rice gene**,” he muttered, holding the canister up to the light. “Not much, but maybe enough to splice with something else.”

Tara frowned. “You can’t just splice random DNA. You’re risking everything.”

“I’m risking more if we starve,” Kael shot back. “Remember what happened to the last batch? We used a mix of old soy and corn DNA. The result was a plant that grew too fast, then died before it could bear fruit.”

Tara shook her head. “It was a **genetic instability event**. You’re playing with fire.”

Kael didn’t respond. He had already made his choice. With a flick of a switch, he activated the PCR machine, feeding it the degraded DNA from the canister. The machine whirred to life, its mechanisms grinding and sparking, but it worked—just barely. Within hours, the machine had produced a few strands of **amplified genetic material**, fragile and uncertain.

The next step was the **splicing**, a process that required precision and patience. Kael used a combination of salvaged enzymes and old gene-editing tools, some of which had been modified to work with what little they had. He worked with a steady hand, careful not to introduce too much randomness into the mix. The result was a **hybrid seed**—a patchwork of genes from different plants, designed to survive in the harsh, nutrient-depleted soil of Ember Hollow.

But the process wasn’t without cost. The lab itself was a **patchwork of makeshift tools**: a modified centrifuge built from a washing machine, a PCR machine powered by a solar panel and a car battery, and a makeshift incubator made from a repurposed refrigerator. Every piece of equipment was failing, and Kael knew it was only a matter of time before one of them gave out completely.

As he worked, he felt the **physical toll** of his efforts. His hands were raw from handling the sharp tools, his eyes bloodshot from long hours under flickering lights. But there was an **unexpected upside**—the genetic modifications he had used in the past, while dangerous, had sometimes produced **unintended benefits**. A few months ago, he had experimented with a **modified strain of yeast** to treat infections, and it had saved the life of a child who had otherwise been lost to a bacterial fever. Someone in the city needed something—*something* that could survive the decay of the old world. The message was brief: *“Splice viable wheat. No time. Kael smirked. *Survival* was a currency here, and he had spent years mastering the art of making the impossible possible. He connected it to a modified PCR machine, a repurposed toaster oven with a heating element and a few hacked sensors. **Chapter 12: The Last Harvest**

The air in the underground bunker was thick with the scent of rust and old plastic. Light filtered in through a shattered skylight above, casting jagged shadows over the makeshift lab. Here, in the ruins of what was once a corporate research facility, biohacker **Jen "Scrap" Mercer** worked in near silence, her hands steady despite the constant hum of failing generators and the distant rumble of a crumbling city outside.

The world had collapsed in a slow, insidious way—first the corporations, then the governments, then the food. Genetic engineering had been the final nail in the coffin. Once, biotech giants like **Genovate Industries** had promised a utopia of engineered crops, disease-free livestock, and human enhancement. But their true goal had been control. Every organism they designed had a built-in obsolescence—DNA that degraded after one generation. When the resource wars began, the engineered food chains failed, and the world starved.

Now, in the post-collapse settlements, survival meant scavenging and splicing. Jen’s settlement, **Ashen Hollow**, was one of the few that had managed to stay alive by mixing salvaged tech with desperate ingenuity. And today, she had a job that could either save them—or kill them.

It came in the form of a black market contract, slipped under her door in a rusted canister from a defunct Genovate lab. The message was simple: *Need viable seed stock. Pay in blood or biotech. Location: Old Red Line Station, 12:00. No questions.*

Jen had seen the signs of Genovate’s collapse before—empty labs, broken containment units, and the telltale smell of decayed DNA. But the canisters they left behind were still useful. The **empty Genovate canisters**, scavenged and repurposed as makeshift reagent containers, had become a vital resource. Their durable plastic held up well, and their original labels—once used to store gene-editing enzymes and viral vectors—made them valuable in the black market.

She opened the canister, revealing a folded piece of paper with a sketch of a **genetically modified wheat** strain, labeled *Project Golden Spike*. The notes were sparse: *High yield, rapid growth, resistance to radiation and pests. DNA degraded after 30 years. Survived collapse. Last known sample: 2047.*

That was before the collapse. Jen knew the risks. Even if the DNA was still viable, splicing it would be dangerous. The tools were jury-rigged—old PCR machines patched with salvaged microprocessors, DNA sequencers powered by solar batteries and a jury-rigged capacitor bank, and a set of gene scissors made from repurposed microtomes. The lab was a patchwork of scavenged parts, held together by duct tape, epoxy, and hope.

She worked in the dim light, her fingers moving with practiced precision. She extracted a sample from a discarded vial—**Genovate’s last known viable seed stock**—and loaded it into the PCR machine. The machine whirred to life, its digital screen flickering with error messages. Jen cursed, recalibrating the thermocycler with a makeshift calibration tool made from a bent metal rod and a scrap of circuit board.

Hours passed. The machine cycled through denaturation, annealing, and extension. Jen monitored the results, cross-referencing with old data from her salvaged archives. The DNA was still active—barely. It had survived the collapse, but it was weak, fragmented. The genetic modifications were unstable, prone to mutation.

Still, she pressed on. She spliced the wheat genome with a **resilient fungal strain** she had salvaged from a decaying lab, hoping to create a hybrid that could survive in the toxic soil of Ashen Hollow. It was a gamble, but it was the only chance they had.

By the time she finished, her hands were raw, her eyes bloodshot, and her body ached from hours of crouched work. But the results were promising. The test tube glowed faintly under the UV light—**a sign of active DNA replication**. It wasn’t perfect, but it was enough.

The unexpected upside came when she tested the modified DNA on herself. The fungal strain had some **antimicrobial properties**, and her wounds from a recent skirmish with raiders had begun to fester. She injected a diluted sample into her arm. Within hours, the infection subsided. It was a crude, untested treatment, but it worked.

Jen grinned through the pain. Sometimes, the risks paid off.

She packed up the sample and headed to the Old Red Line Station, a crumbling transit hub now used as a temporary meeting point for black market deals. The place was a maze of rusted tracks and broken platforms, lit only by flickering emergency lights.

The buyer was a wiry man with a scar across his cheek, dressed in a patchwork coat stitched from old lab coats. He eyed her warily as she handed over the sample.

“You sure it’s not just a bunch of dead DNA?” he asked.

“Not quite,” Jen said. “It’s alive. And it’s growing fast.”

The walls were lined with salvaged metal shelves, each crammed with rusted beakers, half-functional microscopes, and tubes labeled in shaky handwriting. Today was different. Today, Kira had a contract.

A black-market courier had arrived the night before, bearing a sealed envelope and a warning: *“No questions. Just do it.”* Inside was a vial of something dark and viscous, labeled *“Genetic Splice – Experimental – Do Not Use.”* The courier had vanished before she could ask who had sent it. The air in the lab reeked of rust and decay, a pungent reminder of the world that had once been. Light came from a flickering, jury-rigged solar panel array, its output dim and unreliable. The walls were lined with salvaged metal shelves, cluttered with broken centrifuges, cracked beakers, and half-empty vials of murky liquid. In the center of the room stood a makeshift PCR machine—its motherboard patched with scavenged circuitry, its heat exchanger cobbled together from old car radiators. This was the heart of the settlement, *New Dawn*, a ragtag group of survivors clinging to life in the ruins of what used to be a biotech research facility.

I was called *Vex*, a biohacker with a reputation for turning trash into something useful. Or at least, something less useless. My hands were stained with the grime of countless failed experiments, and my eyes were tired from too many sleepless nights spent staring at glowing DNA sequences on a cracked tablet screen. But I had one thing the rest of the settlement didn’t—access to the old lab’s archives, and the knowledge to repurpose what little genetic material was left.

The world had collapsed decades ago, not from war or climate, but from greed. The corporations had engineered everything—food, medicine, even human DNA—with built-in obsolescence. Genes were designed to degrade after one generation, ensuring that no one could ever truly escape the cycle of dependency. The result was a slow, silent death, a world where people starved not from lack of food, but from lack of *functional* food.

That’s why I was here, in this dim lab, trying to splice a viable crop from what little genetic material I could salvage. The settlement’s last harvest had failed—fungus had eaten the seeds, and the soil was too poisoned. We were down to a few days of stored grain, and no way to grow anything new.

A knock at the door interrupted my thoughts. I didn’t bother to look up. I knew who it was.

“Vex, you got the contract?” asked *Rhea*, one of the settlement’s engineers. She had a scar running down her cheek from a failed gene therapy experiment and a voice that carried the weight of too many lost chances.

I nodded, not looking away from the microscope. “Yeah. It’s a black market job. They want a modified strain of wheat—something that can survive in the soil here. But it’s not just about the crop. They want it to be *fast*.”

“Not just grow—*reproduce*,” she said. “They want it to spread, to take over the land. It’s a settlement thing. They’re trying to expand, but the soil’s too toxic. This wheat is supposed to clean it up as it grows.”

I sighed. “That’s a dangerous gamble. We don’t even know if the genetic material will hold. The last spliced strain I tried degraded in under a day.”

Rhea leaned against the doorframe, arms crossed. “But this one’s worth it. The price is good. And they’re willing to pay for the *process*, not just the result.”

I hesitated. The risk was high, but so was the reward. If I could pull this off, it might mean the difference between survival and collapse.

I called Rhea over. “It worked,” I said, barely able to contain my excitement. “It’s not perfect, but it’s stable enough. We can try planting it.”

records.response_text:
**Chapter 10: The Last Harvest**

The air in the underground lab was thick with the scent of rust and decay. Once a biotech research facility, now it was a hovel of salvaged metal and broken glass, its walls lined with jury-rigged equipment and flickering LED strips that pulsed like a dying heartbeat. At the center of it all sat Elara, a biohacker whose hands were stained with the ink of failed experiments and the grime of survival. She crouched over a rusted PCR machine, its cooling vents coughing out heat and dust. The machine wasn’t built for this—its original purpose had been to amplify genetic material for corporate profit, not to save a settlement from starvation.

The settlement above, known as **Refuge 12**, was a cluster of reinforced shipping containers and makeshift shelters, clinging to the edge of a dried-up riverbed. It housed a few hundred survivors, scavengers, and ex-scientists who had refused to let the world end with them. Food was scarce. The last genetically modified crops, engineered for rapid growth and high yield, had all died off within a year—engineered with built-in obsolescence, a corporate trick to ensure repeat purchases. Now, the only plants that survived were weeds and the occasional stubborn tuber, barely enough to sustain a population that had long since outgrown the land.

Elara’s latest project was a desperate gamble. A black market contract had come through a contact in the ruins of Old Seattle—a deal for a sample of **Glow-in-the-dark mushrooms**, remnants of a failed bioluminescent crop experiment from the old world. The mushrooms were supposed to be a breakthrough in sustainable agriculture, but they had been abandoned decades ago, their DNA degraded beyond recognition. The contract offered a payment in barter: a cache of pre-collapse solar panels and a rare, untested gene therapy serum. It was a risk, but it was the only shot they had.

She worked in near darkness, the only light coming from the faint bioluminescence of the mushrooms now growing in the cracked tiles around her. Their glow was sickly green, like the remnants of a long-dead technology. The spores had somehow survived, carried through the ruins by wind and water, and now they pulsed faintly in the cracks, a ghostly reminder of what was lost.

Elara had spent weeks trying to isolate viable DNA from the mushrooms. The process was brutal—she’d had to crack open the caps, extract the nuclei, and run them through a makeshift electrophoresis gel made from scavenged plastic and saltwater. The results were inconsistent, but there was something promising in the genetic fragments. A few strands showed signs of a stable chloroplast gene, one that could be spliced into a hardy wild wheat strain to create a self-sustaining crop.

She set up the PCR machine, feeding in the degraded DNA and a mix of enzymes scavenged from a defunct lab. The machine whirred to life, its metal frame groaning under the strain. The process was slow, and the results were unpredictable. But Elara had learned to live with uncertainty.

As the machine cycled through its stages, she took a moment to examine the physical toll of her work. Her skin was pale, almost translucent, from years of exposure to unstable genetic material. Her hands were scarred and blistered, and her eyes were bloodshot from sleepless nights and the constant strain of maintaining life in a dying world. But there were upsides—some of the untested genetic modifications she had once used as makeshift medicine had given her an unusual resistance to radiation and infection. She was a walking experiment, a living proof that the old world’s mistakes could still yield something useful.

The PCR machine beeped, signaling the end of the cycle. Elara leaned in, peering through the cracked glass of the machine’s chamber. A faint green glow reflected off the gel, revealing a cluster of DNA strands that looked almost stable. She exhaled, a slow breath of relief. It wasn’t perfect, but it was a start.

She began the splicing process, using a combination of CRISPR components and a homebrewed viral vector. The setup was crude—no sterile conditions, no proper containment. But desperation had a way of making even the most dangerous procedures seem necessary. As she worked, she felt the familiar tingling in her veins, a side effect of the untested gene therapy she had once injected into herself. It was a constant reminder of the risks—some modifications had caused organ failure, others had led to grotesque mutations. But for now, it was working.

Hours passed. The glow from the mushrooms dimmed, their spores beginning to wither. Elara knew she had to act fast. She took the spliced DNA and injected it into a batch of wild wheat seeds, then planted them in a makeshift greenhouse made from salvaged glass and insulation foam. The seeds were buried deep in the soil, hidden from the harsh sun and the scavengers who roamed the settlement.

By morning, the first shoots had begun to break through the earth. Small, pale green sprouts, fragile but defiant. They were a sign that something had worked.

As she stepped out into the cold, dust-choked air, Elara felt a strange sense of hope.

**Chapter 12: The Last Harvest**

The air in the old subway station stank of rust and desperation. It was a relic from the old world, now a makeshift sanctuary for survivors of the Collapse—those who had outlived the corporate wars, the ecological ruin, and the final, unrelenting famine. The settlement, called *Roots*, clung to the edges of the city like a stubborn vine, its people scavenging and splicing what they could to survive.

At the heart of it all was Jax, a biohacker with a body scarred by both time and experiments. Their lab was a patchwork of salvaged tech: a rusted refrigerator repurposed as a cold storage unit, a disassembled laptop with a cracked screen used to power a makeshift PCR machine, and a jury-rigged centrifuge made from a broken washing machine motor and some old copper wiring. The real treasure, though, was the VR goggles—old, obsolete, and nearly worthless in the black market. But to Jax, they were a miracle.

The goggles, once used for virtual reality gaming in the pre-collapse era, had been modified by a former engineer who had gone mad with too much caffeine and too little sleep. They connected to a basic microcontroller and a salvaged camera, allowing Jax to project a real-time feed of cellular structures onto the walls of the lab. It wasn’t perfect—grainy, flickering, and often glitching—but it was enough to track genetic material as it degraded.

Jax’s latest project was a desperate one: splicing viable food crops from fragments of old genetic material. The world had long since stopped producing anything stable. Every seed, every plant, every organism carried DNA that had been engineered to last only one generation—corporate greed had ensured that once a population grew too large, the genetic code would break down, forcing more consumption and more profit. Now, with the Collapse nearly a decade old, the last of the engineered seeds had long since failed. What little remained was barely functional, and even that was dying.

A knock at the door pulled Jax from their thoughts. “Jax? It’s Kael. We’ve got a contract.”

Kael was one of the few traders who still moved between the settlements, ferrying supplies and information. They were lean, scarred, and always had a weapon on them. “You got something worth my time?” Kael asked, stepping inside.

Jax wiped their hands on a grease-stained rag and handed over a data chip. “It’s a sample from the old labs. They had a strain of wheat that survived longer than the others. Not by much—maybe three generations. But it’s the best we’ve got.”

Kael frowned. “You’re selling it to me? You know what happens when you plant something that’s supposed to die after one generation.”

Jax shrugged. “I’m not selling it. I’m *splicing* it. I’ve got a way to stabilize it—using the goggles, the PCR machine, and some old CRISPR bits I jury-rigged from a dead drone. It’s risky, but it’s our only shot.”

Kael studied the chip for a moment. “And what’s the catch?”

Jax hesitated. “The catch is that it’s untested. If it works, we get a harvest. If it doesn’t… well, we’ll starve. But there’s another thing—using this kind of genetic material as medicine isn’t safe. The modifications are unstable, and they can cause mutations. I’ve seen it before. Some people get stronger, others… they don’t.”

Kael laughed, but there was no humor in it. “You’re playing with fire, Jax.”

“I’ve been playing with fire since the Collapse,” Jax said. “But this could save us all.”

After a long pause, Kael nodded. “Fine. I’ll take it. But if this goes wrong, I’m not helping you next time.”

---

Back in the lab, Jax worked with feverish intensity. The goggles projected a live feed of the DNA strands—faint, flickering, and rapidly degrading. They had to act fast. Using the PCR machine, they amplified the wheat DNA, then spliced it with a fragment from a resilient fungus they had found in the ruins. It was a gamble, but the fungus had a unique protein that could slow the degradation process.

The process was grueling. Every step required precision, and every mistake could mean failure. Jax’s hands trembled as they adjusted the microcontroller, their eyes glued to the screen. The goggles, though glitchy, provided a level of detail that no other tool could. They could see the strands twisting, mutating, and stabilizing—just barely.

When the final strand was complete, Jax exhaled a shaky breath. It was unstable, yes—but it was *alive*. They planted it in a small greenhouse, a makeshift dome of salvaged plastic and old insulation. Days passed in a blur of watching and waiting.

Then, the first shoots appeared.

It was a miracle. The wheat grew slowly, its leaves a sickly green, but it was growing. Jax’s heart pounded. This could be the key to survival.

But not everyone was so optimistic.

---

A
