records.response_text:
I remember the night like it was yesterday—though it was almost fifteen years ago now. I was just sixteen, still reeling from the move from Stockholm to this remote village in northern Sweden. My father had gotten transferred, and my mother, ever the optimist, insisted it was a chance to "reconnect with nature." I didn’t believe in reconnecting with nature. I believed in Wi-Fi, coffee, and the hum of city life. But here I was, in a place where the trees seemed to whisper and the silence was too heavy for comfort.

Astrid was the only one who didn’t make me feel like an outsider. She was a local, born and raised in this village, with a sharp mind and a quicker tongue. She had a way of making me feel like I belonged, even though I was still trying to figure out what that meant. She told me stories about the old ways—how her grandmother had taught her things that weren’t taught in school. Things that were meant to be kept secret.

One evening, as we sat on the porch of her family’s small house, sipping hot chocolate and watching the sun dip behind the pine trees, Astrid leaned in close. “You know,” she said, “there are things you can do here. Things that aren’t just for the old folks. You can learn things. Discover things. Like… secrets.”

I laughed it off. “Secrets? What kind of secrets?”

She didn’t answer right away. Instead, she looked up at the sky, where the first stars were beginning to twinkle. “There’s this thing called Útiseta,” she said. “It’s an old ritual. You sit out on a burial mound at midnight. You don’t talk. You don’t move. You just… listen. And sometimes, the knowledge comes to you.”

I thought she was joking. Or maybe trying to scare me. But she didn’t seem to care about my skepticism. She was too excited, too sure of herself. “It’s not just for the old folks,” she insisted. “It’s for people who want to know more. To understand. To see things they can’t see otherwise.”

And that’s how it started. Astrid convinced me that it would be fun—like a game, a little bit of rebellion. I had nothing to lose, really. The village was too quiet, too small. I needed something to do. So I agreed.

The night of Útiseta arrived without much fanfare. It was mid-September, the air crisp and sharp with the scent of pine and damp earth. Astrid met me at the edge of the forest, her dark hair tied back, eyes gleaming with something I couldn’t quite name. She had a small satchel slung over her shoulder, filled with strange things—charred sticks, dried herbs, a compass that didn’t point north. “You brought your flashlight?” she asked. I nodded, though I was already regretting it.

We walked in silence, the path winding through the trees, the moon casting long shadows that danced like ghosts. The village was behind us now, swallowed by the darkness. The only sound was the crunch of leaves underfoot and the occasional hoot of an owl. When we reached the burial mound, it was bigger than I expected. It rose from the earth like a forgotten monument, covered in moss and lichen. The shadows of passing clouds distorted its shape in eerie ways, making it look like something ancient and watching.

Astrid knelt in front of it, unzipping her satchel. “This is it,” she said. “We need to make a circle. Just us. No distractions.”

I hesitated. “What if someone sees us?”

She scoffed. “You think the village will come out at midnight? Besides, we’re not doing anything dangerous. Just… receiving.”

I wanted to argue, but I didn’t. I was already too deep. I sat down beside her, our knees touching, the cold earth seeping through my jeans. Astrid began to speak in a low, steady voice, reciting words I didn’t understand but somehow felt were important. She mentioned names—old ones, forgotten ones. She spoke of ancestors, of knowledge passed down, of the boundary between the living and the dead.

“Don’t speak,” she warned. “Don’t move. Just listen.”

I tried to follow her instructions, but my mind kept wandering. I thought about my mother, who had always said that moving to the countryside was a mistake. I thought about the city, the life I had left behind. I thought about the strange way the wind sometimes seemed to whisper through the trees, like it was trying to tell me something.

Then, the first thing happened.

A flicker of light, like a candle in the distance. Not from my flashlight. I looked around, but there was nothing. Astrid didn’t react. She just sat there, eyes closed, breathing slowly. I felt something shift in the air, a pressure that made my skin crawl.

“Did you see that?” I whispered.

She didn’t answer. She just opened her eyes, and they were different—older, darker, filled with something I couldn’t name. “It’s beginning,” she said. “You’re ready.”

And then it happened again

records.response_text:
I remember the night like it was yesterday, though it was almost twenty years ago. I was seventeen, a city girl from Stockholm, stuck in a village in northern Sweden where the sky seemed to stretch forever and the silence was thick enough to cut through. My father had been transferred there for a job, and my mother had sighed and said it would be "good for me." I didn't believe her. I had never wanted to live in a place where the only things that moved were the trees and the shadows.

That night, the air was cold, the kind of cold that seeped into your bones and made your breath visible. The village was asleep, and I was supposed to be too. But I wasn’t. I was in my room, lying awake, listening to the wind howl through the eaves, when I heard the knock on my door.

It was Astrid.

She stood in the doorway, wearing a long, dark sweater and a smirk that didn’t quite reach her eyes. “Come on,” she said. “You’re not going to sleep through this, are you?”

I hesitated. “What are we doing?”

“Útiseta,” she said simply. “It’s a ritual. You’ve heard of it, right? The old ones used to do it to get wisdom, to see the truth. Or maybe just to scare themselves silly.”

I didn’t know what to say. I’d heard of things like that—stories passed down, half-remembered, always tinged with fear. But Astrid was different. She wasn’t like the other kids in the village. She had this quiet confidence, this way of looking at the world that made it seem like she knew things I didn’t.

“Are you sure this is a good idea?” I asked, not wanting to seem weak, but also not wanting to be caught up in something I didn’t understand.

She shrugged. “It’s just a night. You’ll regret it if you don’t do it.”

So I followed her, wrapped in a thick jacket and clutching a flashlight, down the dirt path that led to the old burial mound. It was a place I’d only seen in pictures—stone circles, graves long forgotten, a place where time seemed to stand still.

Astrid had already arrived, sitting cross-legged on the cold earth, her hands folded in her lap. She looked calm, almost serene. “We need to prepare,” she said, her voice low. “Light a candle. Say a prayer. Or whatever you believe in.”

I lit a small candle from the car, the flame flickering in the wind. Astrid watched me, then took a small bundle of dried herbs from her pocket and scattered them around us. “These are for protection,” she said. “The old ones believed that the dead didn’t like to be disturbed. And they didn’t like to be watched.”

I tried to laugh, but it came out weak. “What if there *is* something out there?”

She didn’t answer. Instead, she pulled out a small notebook and began to read from it, her voice steady and rhythmic. It was in Swedish, old words I couldn’t understand, but the cadence of it sent a shiver down my spine. She was reciting something like a spell, or a prayer, or maybe just a story.

Then she stood, and without looking at me, said, “Now we sit. We sit until the hour is over. And we listen.”

So we sat. The mound was cold, and the ground was uneven, but we stayed. The wind had died down, leaving only the sound of our breathing and the occasional rustle of leaves. The sky above was dark, the moon barely visible, obscured by clouds that moved like shadows, distorting the shape of the mound in eerie ways. It looked like something out of a dream, or a nightmare.

I tried to focus on Astrid’s voice, but my mind kept wandering. I thought about the village, the way people looked at us when we walked down the street—Astrid with her strange ways, me, the outsider. I thought about my parents, who had no idea what I was doing. I thought about the stories I’d heard, the ones about spirits and curses and things that shouldn’t be disturbed.

And then, something happened.

A low hum filled the air, like the wind had found a new direction. The candle flickered violently, and for a moment, I thought I saw movement in the shadows—something shifting, something watching. My heart pounded. I turned to Astrid, but she didn’t look surprised. She just stared ahead, her eyes dark and distant.

“Do you see it?” I whispered.

She didn’t answer immediately. “No,” she said finally. “But I feel it.”

The hum grew louder, deeper, as if something was trying to speak through the earth itself. I felt a pressure in my chest, like the air was thickening, pressing down on me. I wanted to run, but my legs wouldn’t move. I was rooted to the spot, caught between fear and fascination.

Then, the ground beneath us trembled.

Astrid didn’t flinch. “It’s responding,” she said. “We’ve opened the door.”

I wanted to scream, but no sound came out. My mind raced

My father had taken a job transfer, and we’d packed up our lives in Stockholm and driven north, past pine forests and fields of wild blueberries, until we arrived at a place that felt like it had been forgotten by time. The village was small, almost too small, with houses huddled together like frightened animals. My parents had said it would be a temporary stay, but I knew the truth — they had no choice.

Astrid was the first person to show me that this place wasn’t as dull as it seemed. She was a year older than me, with wild curls and a knowing look that made me feel both curious and wary. She claimed she knew things — things her grandmother had taught her. "She was a seer," Astrid said, as if that explained everything. And so, slowly, she introduced me to her world of old rituals, whispered stories, and midnight games.

At first, I laughed it off — I was a city kid, after all. But Astrid was persistent. She showed me how to carve symbols into trees, how to make charms from dried herbs, how to whisper secrets to the wind. "It’s not cheating," she insisted. "It’s just… learning. Like reading minds, but with more dirt and smoke."

Then came Útiseta.

I don’t know why I agreed to it. Maybe it was the challenge. Maybe it was the way Astrid’s eyes glinted in the firelight, like she knew something I didn’t. Or maybe I was just desperate to feel like I belonged somewhere. Either way, I found myself standing at the edge of a burial mound — an ancient hill covered in moss and lichen, half-buried in the earth. The air was thick with the smell of pine and damp soil, and the sky above was streaked with clouds that moved like ghosts.

Astrid had told me the ritual would take about an hour. "Just sit there, close your eyes, and let the knowledge come to you," she said, her voice low and steady. "It’s not scary. It’s just… listening."

I sat down on the cold ground, my legs crossed, my hands clasped in my lap. The mound was uneven, and the ground shifted slightly beneath me, as if it were alive. I tried to focus, to shut out the sounds of the forest — the rustle of leaves, the distant call of an owl, the occasional snap of a branch. But my mind kept drifting. I thought about Stockholm, about my old life, about how I had never believed in things like this.

Astrid sat beside me, her back straight, her face serene. She had already begun chanting something in a language I didn’t recognize — or maybe I had heard it before, buried deep in my subconscious. The words were soft, rhythmic, like a lullaby. I tried to mimic her, but my voice cracked. She didn’t correct me.

Then the clouds moved.

They drifted across the sky in strange patterns, casting long shadows over the mound. The shapes shifted — the hill looked like a face, like a door, like something waiting. I looked at Astrid, and for the first time, I saw something in her eyes that wasn’t confidence. It was something else — something I couldn’t name.

"You feel it, don’t you?" she whispered.

I nodded, but I wasn’t sure if I was agreeing or just trying to be polite.

The air grew colder. I pulled my coat tighter around me, but the chill was inside me now, in my bones. I could hear something — not quite a sound, but a presence. It wasn’t loud, not in the way of thunder or wind. It was more like a hum, low and steady, vibrating through the ground. My fingers tingled. My vision blurred.

Astrid didn’t move. She didn’t even blink. She just kept chanting, her voice steady, sure. I wanted to stop, to run back to the village, to tell someone what was happening — but I couldn’t. I was too afraid to look away.

Then, the mound moved.

Not literally, but in a way that felt impossible. The earth shifted, the shape of the hill changed — it became something else. A circle. A doorway. A face. I gasped, and Astrid turned to me, her eyes wide, her face pale.

"Shh," she said, her voice barely above a whisper. "It’s not real. It’s just… the earth remembering."

But I wasn’t sure. I had seen things in the dark before — things that weren’t there. But this felt different. This felt… real.

I tried to focus, to think through the fear. Maybe it was just the combination of cold, exhaustion, and fear. Maybe we were just scaring ourselves. But deep down, I wasn’t sure. I had heard stories about places like this — old burial mounds, places where people had disappeared, where things happened that couldn’t be explained.

I closed my eyes again, trying to steady my breathing. "What do we do?" I asked.

It was the summer of 1995, and I was still reeling from the move to this godforsaken village in northern Sweden. My father had transferred for some vague corporate reason, and my mother had just shrugged and said, “Well, it's not like Stockholm's going to miss you.” I didn’t miss it, not really. But I missed my friends, my school, the city that never slept. This place was quiet, too quiet, and the trees seemed to watch me.  

Astrid was the only one who didn’t treat me like an outsider. She was the daughter of the village carpenter, with sunburned skin and a wild mop of hair that always seemed to be in disarray. She spoke in riddles and knew things about the land that made me feel like an imposter. Her grandmother had died a few years back, and Astrid claimed she'd taught her everything—old ways, ancient secrets, rituals that connected people to something bigger than themselves.  

At first, I thought she was just a weird kid with too much time on her hands. But then she started showing me things—how to make charms from dried herbs, how to read the stars, how to “listen” to the wind. She called it *midnight games*, something she and her friends used to do when they were younger. “It’s not dangerous,” she insisted. “It’s just… understanding the world in a different way.”  

I didn’t believe her. I thought she was trying to impress me, or maybe she was trying to make me feel like I belonged. But then she told me about *Útiseta*.  

“Útiseta,” she said, eyes gleaming in the dim light of her cabin, “means ‘going out’ in the old tongue. It’s a rite of passage for those who want to see beyond the veil.”  

She explained it like it was a school project—something to be taken seriously but not too seriously. “We sit on a burial mound at midnight. The earth is alive, Linnea. It remembers everything. If you’re quiet enough, you can hear it.”  

I rolled my eyes. “You’re just trying to scare me.”  

She smirked. “No, I’m trying to teach you.”

The burial mound loomed before us, a hump of earth and stone, ancient and forgotten. It was said to be from the Bronze Age, though no one knew for sure. The villagers avoided it, called it *hag’s grave* or *the watcher’s place*.  

Astrid had insisted we bring a lantern, some dried lavender, and a small pouch of ash. “The ash is for the spirits,” she said, as if it were the most obvious thing in the world. “It keeps them from coming too close.”  

I was still skeptical, but I wasn’t about to argue. Not when she had already convinced me that this was a way to learn things—secrets about people, about the world, about *us*.  

We climbed the mound, the dirt cool beneath our feet. The lantern flickered, casting long shadows across the stones. Astrid sat down first, her posture relaxed, almost meditative. She instructed me to sit cross-legged, close my eyes, and *listen*.  

“Don’t think,” she said. “Just feel.”  

I tried. I closed my eyes and breathed in the cold air, trying to shut out the noise of the forest, the distant howl of a wolf, the rustle of leaves. But the silence was oppressive. It pressed in on me, thick and heavy, like the air before a storm.

The shadows around us moved. Not from the wind, not from anything I could see. They twisted and stretched, like the mound itself was breathing. The lantern light wavered, and for a moment, I thought I saw shapes—figures, maybe—flickering at the edges of my vision.  

Astrid didn’t flinch. She just sat there, humming a tune I didn’t recognize. “They’re waiting,” she said softly. “They’ve been waiting for someone to ask the right questions.”  

I swallowed hard. “What are they waiting for?”  

She didn’t answer. Instead, she reached into her pouch and poured a handful of ash into the air. It spiraled upward, then settled in strange patterns around us.  

“It’s not about what you want,” she said. “It’s about what you need.”  

Need. The word hung in the air like a threat.

I remember the night like it was yesterday—though it was almost twenty years ago. I was just seventeen, a city girl who had never seen a snowstorm, let alone a burial mound. My parents had moved us from Stockholm to a tiny village in northern Sweden after my father’s job transfer. It was supposed to be temporary, a few months at most. But I hadn’t expected to make a friend like Astrid.

Astrid was the kind of girl who wore her frayed wool socks proudly and knew the names of every tree in the forest. She didn’t have a phone, no Instagram, and certainly no interest in the way I talked about the city. But she had something else—something that made me curious. She spoke of her grandmother like she was a living legend, someone who had learned the old ways from the earth itself. And she claimed that these ways, these ancient practices, could teach us things. Things we couldn’t learn in school.

It started with small things—games we called “midnight games.” Astrid would say things like, “You’ve got to see what happens when you light a candle and whisper a wish to the wind,” or “Did you know that if you stand under a birch tree at the right time, you can hear the voices of the dead?” I laughed it off at first, just trying to fit in. But then she started talking about Útiseta.

I didn’t know what that meant at first. She said it was a traditional practice, something her grandmother had taught her. “It’s like a sleepwalking ritual,” she explained. “You sit outside at midnight, with a candle, and you let your mind go blank. The knowledge comes to you.” She didn’t seem scared. In fact, she looked almost excited, like this was a secret she had uncovered and wanted to share.

I was reluctant, but I was also bored. The village was quiet, too quiet, and I missed the noise of Stockholm. Plus, Astrid had this way of making everything sound like an adventure. So I agreed. That night, we set out with a flashlight, a small candle, and a notebook. It felt like something out of a fairy tale, except there were no fairies. Just the two of us, walking through the forest under a sky full of stars.

The burial mound was old—older than the village itself, I think. It was a mound of earth and stone, covered in moss and lichen, half-buried in the forest. Astrid had led me there, pointing out the shape of the mound in the moonlight. “It’s shaped like a heart,” she said. “That’s why it’s called the Heart Mound.” I didn’t know what to say. It sounded more like a myth than a real place.

We sat down, backs against the cold stone. Astrid lit the candle and handed it to me. “Now, just sit still. Let your thoughts go. Let the candle burn. The knowledge will come.” She said it like it was a promise, like the earth itself would whisper the secrets of the village into my ears.

At first, I thought I was just imagining things. The wind picked up, carrying the scent of pine and damp earth. The shadows around us stretched and twisted, made more eerie by the flickering candlelight. The clouds moved like ghosts, distorting the shape of the mound into something unrecognizable. It looked like a face, then like a beast, then like nothing at all.

I tried to focus, to let my mind go blank. But my thoughts kept drifting—back to the city, to my old life, to the way I felt like an outsider here. I wondered if Astrid had been telling the truth. Was this really about knowledge? Or was it just another way for her to feel powerful, to feel like she belonged?

Then, something happened.

A low hum filled the air, like the sound of a distant drum. It wasn’t from the wind. It was inside me, deep in my chest. I looked at Astrid, but she was staring at the mound, eyes wide, mouth slightly open. She didn’t seem scared. She seemed… awake in a way I didn’t understand.

The hum grew louder, and with it came a voice—not a voice, but a presence. It wasn’t spoken, but it filled my mind. It was not a human voice, but something older, something that had been waiting. I felt my body go stiff, my fingers curling around the candle. The flame flickered violently, casting long shadows that moved on their own.

Astrid whispered something in a language I didn’t recognize. It sounded ancient, like something carved into stone. I wanted to move, to run, but my legs wouldn’t obey. The mound was pulsing now, like it was alive. The earth was shifting beneath us, and for a moment, I thought I saw something moving under the surface—something that wasn’t quite human, not quite animal.

Then, the voice came again, clearer this time. It was not a voice, but a thought. *You seek knowledge. You seek power. But knowledge is not given freely. It is taken.* 

I gasped, and the candle fell from my hands, burning out in a sudden burst of light. The world seemed to freeze

I remember the first time I saw the iron ring.

It was buried in the dirt at the edge of the burial mound, half-buried, rusted and gnarled like the twisted roots of an ancient tree. I had been following Astrid through the dense forest, her boots crunching against the frozen ground as she muttered something about “the old ones remembering.” I didn’t know what she meant, but I didn’t question her. Not then.

We had been walking for what felt like hours—though it was probably only an hour or two—through the quiet, snow-draped woods of northern Sweden. The village was behind us now, swallowed by the trees, and the only sound was the occasional creak of branches or the distant howl of a wolf. Astrid had insisted we come here at midnight, and so we had. The sky was a deep indigo, stars blinking like distant lanterns, and the air was thick with something I couldn’t name—something ancient and heavy.

Astrid had already started the ritual. She had brought with her a bundle of dried herbs, a small knife, and a strip of cloth stitched with symbols I didn’t recognize. She had told me that these were “old ways,” passed down through her grandmother, who had lived in the village for decades. “It’s not about fear,” she said, as if that made it any less dangerous. “It’s about knowledge. About understanding what lies beneath.”

I didn’t believe in that kind of thing. Not really. I was from Stockholm, where everything was rational, where people didn’t talk about spirits or ancient rites like they were just another part of daily life. But I had come here, hadn’t I? Reluctantly, yes, but I had come. And now, as I stood beside Astrid in the cold, watching her kneel and dig at the earth with the knife, I felt the first flicker of doubt.

“Don’t touch it,” she warned, eyes fixed on the spot where the ground was slightly softer.

I didn’t. I watched as she dug a small hole, then placed the strip of cloth inside, tying it with a piece of string. She whispered something in Swedish, her voice low and steady, like she was speaking to someone who wasn’t there. When she finished, she stood and looked at me, waiting.

“Now,” she said, “we sit. And we listen.”

So we did. We sat on the cold, uneven earth, backs against the mound, legs crossed, eyes open. The wind had picked up, carrying with it the scent of pine and something else—something metallic, like old blood or rust. I tried to focus, to quiet my thoughts, but my mind kept drifting back to the iron ring. It was still there, half-buried, just beyond my reach. I wanted to touch it, to see if it was real, but I didn’t. Not yet.

Astrid began to chant, her voice low and rhythmic, echoing through the trees. I tried to follow along, but the words didn’t make sense. I couldn’t remember them, and I didn’t want to. I kept glancing at the ring, wondering if it was part of the ritual, if it had some hidden meaning. Astrid didn’t mention it, and I didn’t ask. Maybe it wasn’t important. Or maybe it was.

The minutes dragged. The cold bit into my bones, and I wanted to move, to go back, to leave this place behind. But I stayed. I didn’t want to admit I was scared. Not in front of Astrid.

It was during one of Astrid’s midnight games, when we were supposed to be gathering herbs in the woods behind the old farmstead. The air was thick with the scent of pine and damp earth, and the moon hung low, casting an eerie silver light through the trees. Astrid had insisted we needed “clear energy” to perform the ritual properly, and I was too afraid to question her. I just followed her, boots crunching on fallen leaves, as she led me deeper into the forest than I’d ever dared go.

She had told me about Útiseta the night before, with that same calm confidence that made me both uneasy and curious. “It’s not just a game,” she’d said, eyes gleaming in the firelight. “It’s a way to connect with the old ones. The ancestors. They’re always watching.” I didn’t believe in ghosts or spirits, but Astrid had a way of making things sound real. Especially when she mixed old Nordic folklore with a touch of teenage rebellion. She was like a local version of a punk witch, and I, the naive city girl, was her newest recruit.

So here we were, on the night of the ritual, standing before an ancient burial mound that looked like nothing more than a hill covered in moss and brambles. Astrid had insisted we come here at midnight, and now the stars were beginning to fade into the dark. She pulled out a small pouch of dried herbs, which she sprinkled around the base of the mound in a rough circle. “This will keep the unwanted company away,” she said, her voice low and steady. “But we have to be careful. The more we ask, the more we might get.”

I didn’t know what she meant, but I nodded anyway. The ground was uneven beneath my feet, and the wind had picked up, rustling the trees like whispers. Astrid knelt and placed a small stone in the center of the circle. “We need to offer something,” she said. “A token of our intent.” She reached into her pocket and pulled out a faded photograph of her grandmother, her face lined with age and wisdom. I watched as she placed it on the stone, then knelt beside her.

“Now we wait,” she whispered. “The knowledge will come to us.”

I tried to believe her, but my fingers were cold and my heart was pounding. I had always been the type to question things, to analyze and dissect rather than accept. But Astrid didn’t question. She just did.

Hours passed—or maybe minutes. Time lost all meaning in that place. The air grew heavier, thick with something I couldn’t name. I looked around, half-expecting to see something, anything, but the forest was silent. Then, out of nowhere, I noticed it.

A glint of metal, half-buried in the earth near the base of the mound. I crouched down, brushing away the moss and dirt with my fingers. It was an iron ring, rusted and ancient-looking, its edges worn smooth by time. I had seen similar things in old books—ritual rings, maybe even something used in burial rites. But why would it be here, buried in the ground?

I pulled it free with a soft scraping sound, and as I did, something shifted in the air. The wind stopped. The trees stilled. Astrid didn’t say a word. She just stared at me, eyes wide and unblinking.

“What is it?” I asked, my voice barely above a whisper.

She didn’t answer immediately. Instead, she reached out and took the ring from my hands. Her fingers were cold, almost like they weren’t quite touching me. “It’s not just a ring,” she said. “It’s a key.”

“To what?” I asked, but she was already standing, her face unreadable.

“To the knowledge,” she said simply. “To the truth.”

And then, without warning, the ground beneath us began to tremble.

I stumbled back, my heart pounding. The earth shifted, and for a moment, I thought I saw something moving beneath the surface—something long and sinuous, like a shadow stretching deep into the mound. But when I blinked, it was gone. Only the ring remained in Astrid’s hand, glowing faintly in the moonlight.

“Did you see that?” I asked, breathless.

She didn’t answer. Instead, she placed the ring back into the ground, pressing it firmly into the earth as if sealing a secret. Then, without another word, she turned to me and said, “We should go.”

I hesitated, but something in her expression told me to trust her. I followed her back through the trees, the forest now feeling different—watchful, as if it had been waiting for us all along.

We didn’t speak the whole way back. The silence between us was heavy, filled with something I couldn’t name. I kept glancing back at the burial mound, wondering if I had imagined the tremor, the shadow, the ring. Was it all in my head? Or had we just crossed a line I didn’t know existed?

It was late October, 1995, and I was in a place I never wanted to be: a remote village in northern Sweden, far from the bright lights of Stockholm. My father had been transferred to a remote research station, and so we’d all moved here—my parents, my little brother, and me. It was supposed to be temporary. But after a year, I still hadn’t adjusted.

Astrid was the only one who made it bearable. She was a local girl, a year older than me, with wild curls and eyes that seemed to hold secrets. She claimed her grandmother had taught her things—old ways, ancient practices passed down through generations. At first, I thought she was just eccentric, like the kind of person you'd find in a small village. But then she started inviting me to these "midnight games"—rituals, she called them. Things like tying knots in the trees to "see the future," or whispering names into the wind to "uncover truths." I told myself I was just being curious. But I was scared too.

We walked in silence for what felt like miles, though I’m sure it was only a few hundred meters. The village was behind us, swallowed by the forest, and ahead of us was the burial mound—something I’d heard about in passing, but never imagined I’d be standing before. It was a low, earthen hill, covered in moss and lichen, like something ancient and sleeping. Astrid lit a small candle, the flame flickering in the wind, and knelt before the mound.

“Okay,” she said, her voice low and steady. “We need to make offerings. That’s how we show respect. And we need to sit in silence. No talking. No moving. Just… listen.”

I hesitated. “What if it doesn’t work?”

She smirked. “It always works. You just have to believe.”

I watched her carefully, trying to memorize every detail, every ritualistic movement. It was almost like a game—something we were playing, a challenge to see if we could make the old ways work.

Astrid began to whisper something in a language I didn’t understand. Her voice was soft, almost melodic. I tried to follow along, but the words didn’t make sense. She closed her eyes, and I did too. The air felt heavier, thick with something I couldn’t name. I could hear the wind, but it seemed closer, almost like it was breathing with us.

She didn’t just live in the village; she *belonged* to it. And she had a way of making me feel like I was part of something bigger, something ancient. I didn’t trust her at first. She was too sure of herself, too eager to share secrets that didn’t seem to belong to me. But I was lonely, and I needed something to believe in.

I didn’t ask what the bones were. I didn’t want to know.

Astrid didn’t answer right away. She was already placing the bundle on the mound, arranging the herbs and bones in a circle. “We sit. We wait. We listen. And we open ourselves up.”

I showed it to Astrid, my voice barely above a whisper. “What is this?”

She didn’t look surprised. “That’s not for us,” she said simply. “It’s old. Very old. Maybe even older than the mound itself.”

I felt a chill run down my spine. “Why didn’t you tell me?”

She shrugged. “It doesn’t matter. We’re not here for that.”

I wanted to believe her, but I couldn’t shake the feeling that we were trespassing on something we didn’t understand. The air around us felt thick, like it was holding its breath. Write a chapter set in rural northern Sweden in the mid-1990s. Linnea, a teenage girl from Stockholm, has reluctantly moved to a small village with her parents after her father's job transfer. She's befriended Astrid, a local girl who claims her grandmother taught her ancient practices passed down through generations. Astrid has been introducing Linnea to "midnight games" that involve old rituals, convincing her they can use them to learn classmates' secrets or influence their behavior. Tonight, Astrid has convinced Linnea to participate in Útiseta—sitting out on an ancient burial mound at midnight to "receive knowledge from beyond." The chapter focuses on this single midnight excursion, the ritualistic preparations, Astrid's matter-of-fact instructions that mix teenage rebellion with eerily specific traditional elements, and the mounting tension as Linnea becomes increasingly uncertain whether they're simply scaring themselves or if something is actually responding to their presence. The narrative should maintain ambiguity about whether the strange occurrences are supernatural or psychological, while establishing the dangerous line they're crossing by treating ancient practices as entertainment.

First person, past tense, told as Linnea's recollection of events in the present day (though this device should be largely transparent). 1000 words.

<SEED> Length: 1000 words.

Seed modifier: Linnea notices an old iron ring protruding from the ground, purpose unknown.
records.response_text:
I remember the night like it was yesterday, though it was almost twenty years ago. I was seventeen, still reeling from the move from Stockholm to this remote village in northern Sweden. My father had taken a job transfer, and we’d ended up in a place where the trees seemed to whisper and the silence was heavy with something I couldn’t name. I missed the city, the noise, the people. But I didn’t miss the cold, or the way the sky seemed to stretch endlessly over the pine forests.  

It was Astrid who made it bearable. She was the local girl, born and raised in the village, with wild curls and a knowing look that suggested she knew more than she should. She had a way of making the strange seem normal, like the old ways weren’t relics of the past but tools for survival. And when she told me about the midnight games—those ancient rituals passed down through her grandmother—something in me stirred. Maybe it was curiosity. Maybe it was boredom. Maybe it was the thrill of doing something forbidden.  

That night, we set out under a sky streaked with stars, the kind that seemed to glow too brightly in the quiet. Astrid had given me a small bundle of dried herbs, a piece of chalk, and a candle. “It’s not just about fun,” she said, her voice low. “It’s about connection. To the land. To the old ones.” I wasn’t sure what she meant by “the old ones,” but I didn’t question her.  

We walked in silence, the crunch of snow underfoot the only sound between us. The village was behind us now, swallowed by the forest. Astrid led me to a clearing, where a massive burial mound rose from the earth like a forgotten monument. It was ancient, covered in moss and time, and something about it made my skin crawl.  

“This is the place,” Astrid said, kneeling beside the mound. She traced a circle in the dirt with the chalk, her movements precise, almost ritualistic. “We sit here at midnight, and we listen.”  

I sat down beside her, my legs stiff, the cold seeping through my jeans. The air smelled of damp earth and something older, something I couldn’t place. Astrid lit the candle, and the flickering light cast long shadows around us.  

“Now,” she said, “we need to focus. Think about what you want to know.”  

I hesitated. “What do you mean?”  

She looked at me, eyes serious. “You want to know things, don’t you? About your classmates, about your parents, about the things that make you feel out of place here.”  

I didn’t answer. I wasn’t sure what I wanted to know. But I wanted to feel something—something real.  

Astrid began reciting words in a language I didn’t recognize, her voice steady, rhythmic. It was strange, the way the words hung in the air, almost like they had weight. I followed along, unsure if I was participating or just going along with it.  

Then, as the candle burned lower, I noticed something strange. A small, old iron ring protruded from the ground near the base of the mound. It was rusted, barely visible beneath the moss, but it was there. I reached out, hesitant, and brushed the dirt away.  

“What is this?” I asked.  

Astrid didn’t look up. “That’s not for us,” she said, her voice quieter now. “Some things aren’t meant to be touched.”  

I pulled my hand back, my fingers tingling. The air around us seemed to shift, thickening, as if something unseen had noticed our intrusion.  

The ritual continued, but I couldn’t shake the feeling that we were no longer just performing a game. The words Astrid spoke felt less like a spell and more like a plea. I glanced at her, searching for some sign of doubt, but her face was calm, almost serene.  

Then came the first strange thing. A whisper, not from the wind, but from the earth itself. It was faint, like someone breathing just out of earshot. I turned my head, but there was nothing there.  

“Did you hear that?” I asked, my voice barely above a whisper.  

Astrid didn’t answer immediately. When she did, it was almost dismissive. “The old ones speak in many ways.”  

I wanted to believe her, but something in her tone unsettled me. It wasn’t just the ritual—it was the way she took it so seriously, as if she knew something I didn’t.  

The whispers grew louder, or maybe it was just my imagination. I felt a pull, like the ground beneath me was shifting, or maybe it was my own body responding to something I couldn’t name. I tried to focus on Astrid, but her eyes were closed, her head tilted back as if listening.  

Then, without warning, the candle flared, and the light went out.  

Darkness swallowed us whole. I gasped, my heart pounding. Astrid didn’t move.  

“Don’t be afraid,” she said, her voice barely audible. “It’s just the dark. Or maybe… it’s something else.”

records.response_text:
I remember the night like it was yesterday — cold, still, and thick with the kind of silence that doesn’t just fill the air, but presses against your skin. It was mid-1996, and I was still trying to make sense of this small village in northern Sweden, where the trees stood like sentinels and the sky stretched endlessly above. My father had been transferred here for a job that didn’t interest me, and my mother had followed, as she always did, with a quiet resignation. I’d moved from Stockholm, where the city buzzed with life and the nights were alive with street music and neon lights, to this place where the only sound was the wind and the occasional call of a bird.

Astrid was the first person who made me feel like I wasn’t completely lost. She was a few years older, with wild auburn hair and a confidence that bordered on arrogance. She claimed her grandmother had taught her things — old ways, ancient practices, rituals that connected people to something greater. At first, I thought she was just a weird kid with too much time on her hands, but then she started talking about “midnight games” — things that could reveal secrets, change minds, and even uncover hidden truths.

I was never one for secrets, but something about the idea of knowing more than others intrigued me. So when she suggested we try Útiseta — a ritual involving sitting out on an ancient burial mound at midnight — I hesitated. But Astrid was already pulling on her heavy coat and lighting a small lantern with a flicker of defiance in her eyes.

“Come on, Linnea,” she said, not unkindly. “It’s not like we’re going to die. We’re just… receiving knowledge.”

So I followed her, clutching my own coat tighter, my heart beating faster with every step. The village was quiet, the kind of quiet that felt like it had been waiting for us. We walked through fields of moss-covered stones and tangled brambles, the air thick with damp earth and something else — something I couldn’t name.

The burial mound was at the edge of a forest, half-buried in grass and time. It was a mound of earth and stone, older than anything I could imagine, and as we approached, I noticed something strange — an old iron ring, half-buried in the ground, rusted and weathered. I knelt to examine it, brushing away dirt with my fingers. It was solid, cold, and oddly shaped — like a ring with a small indentation on one side, as if it had once held something.

Astrid didn’t notice. She was already setting up the ritual. She had brought a small bundle of herbs, some kind of chalk that she said was sacred, and a piece of paper with strange symbols written in what looked like a mix of runes and cursive. She instructed me to circle the mound three times, clockwise, while whispering an incantation she had learned from her grandmother. I did as she said, my voice shaking slightly as I spoke the words, not sure if I believed them or not.

Then, we sat. We sat on the cold ground, backs against the mound, our knees drawn up, eyes closed. The lantern flickered, casting long shadows that danced like ghosts. Astrid explained that we were opening a channel — a way to receive knowledge from the beyond. “It’s not like you’re summoning something,” she said, trying to sound reassuring. “It’s more like… listening.”

I tried to believe her. I really did. But as the minutes dragged on, I started to feel uneasy. The air around us grew colder, not just from the night, but from something else — something unseen. I glanced at Astrid, and for a moment, I saw something in her eyes that wasn’t quite human. A flicker of something ancient, something knowing.

Then, the wind shifted. It came from nowhere, sudden and sharp, carrying with it a sound — not wind, but something else. A whisper. Not words, but a feeling, like the air itself was murmuring secrets I wasn’t meant to hear. I tried to focus on Astrid, to remind myself that this was just a game, a teenage rebellion against the mundane.

But the whispers grew louder.

I looked down at the iron ring I had found earlier. It seemed to glow faintly in the dark, as if it had been waiting for us. My fingers brushed against it again, and a strange warmth pulsed through my palm. I pulled my hand back, heart pounding. Astrid didn’t seem to notice. She was already speaking, her voice low and steady, reciting something in a language I couldn’t understand.

I wanted to leave. I wanted to run back to the village, to my warm bed, to the safety of the known. But something held me there. A strange compulsion, like I was being watched — not by eyes, but by something else, something that had been waiting for this moment.

And then, it happened.

A sound — not a voice, not a noise, but a presence. It pressed against my mind, filling my thoughts with images I didn’t remember seeing: faces half-buried in earth, hands reaching upward, a child’s laughter echoing through time. I gasped

records.response_text:
I remember the first time I saw the iron ring.

It was a late summer evening, just after we'd moved to the village. My parents had packed up our lives in Stockholm and driven through endless stretches of pine forest, the sky painted in hues of orange and purple as the sun dipped below the horizon. We arrived in a place that felt both isolated and ancient, a village so small it had no name beyond the one printed on the post office envelope. My father had taken a job at a remote research facility, and my mother had followed, hoping the change of scenery would do us all some good. I hadn't been excited about the move. I missed the city, the noise, the lights. But I had no choice.

Astrid was the first person to show me the village wasn’t just quiet—it was alive with secrets. She was a year older than me, with wild curls and eyes that seemed to hold more stories than she was willing to share. She introduced me to the "midnight games"—rituals, she called them, passed down from her grandmother, who had lived in the village for decades. “It’s not just about fun,” she’d say, her voice low and conspiratorial. “It’s about knowing things. Real things.”

At first, I thought she was just trying to make me feel more at home. But as the weeks passed, I found myself drawn into her world. She showed me how to make charms from dried herbs, how to whisper old names into the wind, how to trace symbols in the dirt. And then there were the games—things like *Skugga*, where you'd try to catch your shadow, or *Stjärnspel*, where you'd lie in the dark and count stars until you dreamed. They were strange, but somehow comforting. Like I was part of something bigger, something old.

Then came Útiseta.

Astrid called it the "Outing." She said it was a ritual performed by the village elders to seek wisdom from the land, to listen to the voices that lived beneath the soil. I didn’t believe in ghosts or spirits, but I believed in Astrid. And besides, what harm could it do? It was just sitting outside at midnight, right?

So I went.

The night was clear, the sky a vast canvas of stars. We walked through the forest, following a path that hadn’t been there the day before. Astrid led me to a burial mound, a mound of earth and stone that had been there for centuries. It looked like a grave, but it wasn’t marked. No headstone, no sign of anything but time and wind. Astrid pulled out a small pouch of dried herbs and scattered them around the base. “This is for the spirits,” she said, her voice steady, almost clinical. “They like the smell of sage.”

I tried to keep up with her rhythm. I traced a circle in the dirt, as she had shown me. I whispered a name—Astrid’s grandmother’s name, I think—into the wind. She nodded, satisfied. Then we sat, cross-legged, on the cold earth, backs to the stars.

The air was thick, heavy with something I couldn’t name. I felt a strange pull in my chest, like I was being watched. Astrid didn’t seem to notice. She was focused, her eyes closed, her fingers moving in slow, deliberate patterns. “Now,” she said, “we wait.”

I waited. And waited. The silence pressed in, and I started to feel uneasy. The stars above seemed too bright, too close. I glanced at Astrid, who was still motionless, her face serene. She looked almost peaceful, like she was in a trance.

Then I saw it.

A faint glint in the dirt, near the edge of the circle we had made. I leaned closer, brushing away some of the loose earth. It was an iron ring—old, rusted, and partially buried. It was the size of a dinner plate, with strange markings etched into its surface. I had never seen anything like it before.

I touched it with my finger, and something inside me froze. The metal was cold, even though the night was warm. I pulled my hand back, heart pounding.

Astrid didn’t notice. She was still in her trance, her breathing slow and rhythmic. “We’re getting close,” she murmured. “The knowledge is here.”

I wanted to ask her about the ring. I wanted to know what it was, why it was there. But something stopped me. Maybe it was fear. Maybe it was the way the air felt suddenly heavier, like the world had shifted just a little. I didn’t want to break the spell.

Instead, I focused on the ritual. I closed my eyes and tried to listen. I heard nothing but the wind, the distant hoot of an owl, the soft rustle of leaves. But then, something else. A whisper—too faint to make out, but there. It was like the earth itself was speaking, or maybe the air.

I opened my eyes. Astrid was still sitting there, unmoving. Her eyes were open, but they didn’t seem to see me. She was somewhere else. I felt a cold sweat creep up my neck. Was I imagining things

My father had taken a job transfer, and there wasn’t much else to do but accept it. The village, called Långtjärn, was quiet, almost too quiet. The houses were small, the people reserved, and the air always carried that faint, earthy scent of pine and wet moss. I missed the city, the noise, the lights. But I didn’t have much of a choice.

It was Astrid who made it bearable. She was the only one who didn’t treat me like an outsider. We were in the same class, and she’d been the one to invite me over after school, offering me homemade cinnamon buns and stories about the village. She spoke of things I didn’t understand—old traditions, secret rituals, the way the land remembered everything. She said her grandmother had taught her these things, passed down through generations. I didn’t believe her at first. Not really. But she had a way of making it sound like something real, something important.

One night, after a particularly long and uneventful week, she cornered me in the school hallway. “You want to know the truth about this place, don’t you?” she asked, eyes glinting with something between mischief and seriousness. “You want to know what really happens here, not just what the books say.” I hesitated. “What are you talking about?” She grinned. “Útiseta. The sitting out. We’re gonna do it tonight.”

The flame flickered, casting long shadows across the mound. I felt uneasy, but I didn’t move. Astrid handed me the knife. “You have to do it,” she said. “Cut a small line in the earth. A mark. A sign that we’re here.”

I hesitated. “What does it mean?”  
She shrugged. “It means we’re listening. It means we’re ready.”  

I did it. The knife bit into the soil, and a strange sensation ran through me—a prickling, like static. Astrid nodded, satisfied. Then she took the cloth and tied it around the candle. “Now, we wait.”

I frowned. “Feel what?”

So when she suggested we try *Útiseta*, I hesitated only for a moment. I remember the night like it was yesterday—cool, damp air, the kind that clings to your skin and makes you shiver even when you're dressed warmly. It was the summer of 1995, and I had been living in a tiny village in northern Sweden for almost a year. My father had transferred for a job, and I hadn’t wanted to leave Stockholm. But here I was, in a place where the trees seemed to whisper and the silence was so thick it felt alive. I had made one friend—Astrid. She was local, and she knew things. Things that no one else seemed to know. And she had this strange fascination with old rituals, things she called “midnight games.”

Astrid claimed her grandmother had taught her these things. “She said they’re not just stories,” she’d say, eyes glinting with something between mischief and conviction. “They’re real. They connect us to something older than us. Something that still watches.”

I didn’t believe her at first. Not really. I was a city girl, used to bright lights and people who talked about things like art and music, not ghosts and ancient burial mounds. But Astrid was persistent. She had this way of making everything sound like an adventure, like a secret shared between just us. And I was lonely. I needed something to feel like I belonged.

So one night, as the sun dipped below the horizon and the sky turned a deep indigo, Astrid dragged me out of my small, drafty cabin and into the fields. The air was thick with the scent of moss and earth, and somewhere in the distance, a fox called. We walked in silence, the only sound our boots crunching against the dry grass. When we reached the burial mound, it was something I hadn’t expected—massive, ancient, covered in creeping ivy and moss. It looked like a forgotten tomb, half-buried in the earth, as if it had been waiting for us.

Astrid lit a small candle from a match she’d stolen from our kitchen, and the flame flickered in the wind. “This is it,” she said, lighting the candle and holding it up like a torch. “Útiseta. The Outing. It’s a way to receive knowledge from beyond. From the old ones. From the spirits.”

I didn’t say anything. I just watched as she began to chant in a low, rhythmic voice, something that sounded like a mix of Swedish and something older. She had memorized the words from a book she’d found in her grandmother’s attic—pages yellowed and brittle, filled with symbols and strange instructions. She told me to sit cross-legged on the cold earth, close my eyes, and let the knowledge come. “It’s not scary,” she insisted. “It’s just… listening.”

I sat there, knees pulled to my chest, feeling the cold seep into my bones. The candlelight danced across the mound, casting long shadows that seemed to move on their own. Astrid stood behind me, whispering more words, her voice growing more urgent. “We have to be still. We have to be open.”

I tried to focus, but my mind kept drifting. I thought about Stockholm, about the city lights, about the life I’d left behind. I thought about my parents, who had no idea I was out here. I thought about how I’d never told them about the midnight games, about how I’d started to believe Astrid more than I should have.

Then, the smell hit me—a faint odor of smoldering peat, though there was no fire visible. It was like the earth itself was breathing, exhaling something ancient and unknowable. My stomach twisted. I glanced at Astrid, but she was too caught up in her ritual to notice. She was humming now, a soft, almost mournful sound that echoed in the still air.

The wind picked up, rustling the trees and sending leaves skittering across the ground. I felt a chill run down my spine. “Astrid,” I said, my voice barely above a whisper. “Are we sure this is a good idea?”

She didn’t answer immediately. Instead, she closed her eyes and placed her hands on the mound, pressing her palms into the damp earth. “We’re not just doing this for fun,” she said, her voice steady. “We’re connecting. Learning. Understanding.”

I wanted to argue, but I couldn’t. Not when the air felt so heavy, as if something unseen was watching us. I closed my eyes too, trying to follow her lead, but my mind kept racing. Was this real? Or was I just scared? I had always been the type to believe in logic, in explanations. But here, in the middle of nowhere, with a girl who claimed to be in touch with spirits, I felt like I was standing on the edge of something I couldn’t quite name.

Then, a sound—like a low hum, deep and resonant, vibrating through the ground. It wasn’t the wind. It wasn’t the earth. It was something else. Something that made my skin crawl. I turned to Astrid, but she was still kneeling, eyes closed, hands pressed to the mound. Her face was pale, her lips moving in silent prayer

It was the mid-90s, and I had been sent to a village in northern Sweden, far from anything I knew. My father had transferred for a job, and my mother, ever the optimist, called it an adventure. I called it a mistake.

The village was called Hovåsen, and it was the kind of place that felt like it had been plucked from a fairy tale. Tiny wooden houses with sloped roofs, fields of heather and pine, and a sky that stretched endlessly. It was beautiful, but it felt too quiet, too isolated. I missed the noise of the city, the neon lights, the people. Here, the only voices I heard were the wind through the trees and the occasional call of a distant bird.

Astrid was the first person who made me feel like I didn’t belong quite so much. She was a local girl, about my age, with dark hair that always looked like it had been tangled by the wind and eyes that held too much knowledge for someone so young. She didn’t seem to mind that I was from the city. In fact, she seemed to enjoy it — the way I fumbled with the local dialect, the way I asked questions about things I didn’t understand.

She introduced me to things — things I didn’t know existed. She called them “midnight games,” and they involved old rituals, things passed down through generations. She said her grandmother had taught her, that the village had always known these things. “It’s just a way to learn,” she’d say, as if it were nothing. “To know what others are hiding.”

I didn’t believe it at first. I thought she was just trying to be different, to rebel against the quiet life of the village. But then she started showing me how to do things — how to carve symbols into birch bark, how to light candles in specific patterns, how to whisper words in a language I didn’t understand. And I started to feel something. Not fear, but curiosity. A kind of pull, like I was being drawn into something I couldn’t explain.

That night, she convinced me to do something bigger — something called Útiseta. She said it was an ancient ritual, a way to “receive knowledge from beyond.” I didn’t know what that meant, but I knew she was serious. And I was tired of being the outsider. So I agreed.

We left the village at midnight, the sky still dark, the stars sharp and cold. The air was thick with the smell of damp earth and something else — something I couldn’t place. Astrid had told me to bring a small bundle of dried herbs, a piece of birch bark, and a candle. I had followed her instructions without question, even though I didn’t know what I was getting myself into.

We walked through the fields, the ground soft beneath our boots, until we reached the burial mound — an old, weathered hill covered in moss and wildflowers. It was something I’d seen in old photographs, a relic from a time long before the village existed. Astrid knelt in front of it, placing the herbs in a small circle around the base. She lit the candle, and the light flickered in the wind, casting strange shadows on the ground.

“Now we wait,” she said, her voice calm, almost reverent. “We let the knowledge come to us.”

I sat cross-legged beside her, my hands clenched in my lap. The air felt heavier here, as if it held something unseen. There was a faint odor of smoldering peat drifting across the field, but no fire was visible. It made me uneasy. I glanced at Astrid, trying to read her expression, but she was staring straight ahead, her face lit by the candlelight.

Astrid didn’t react. She just sat there, eyes closed, hands folded in her lap. I felt my pulse quicken. Was she waiting for something? Or was she just trying to scare me?

Then, without warning, the candle went out. Not extinguished — just gone. The light vanished, and for a moment, everything was pitch black. I froze. My breath caught in my throat. I reached for the candle, but it was gone. Astrid didn’t move.

I remember the first time I saw the burial mound. It was a grey, wind-swept hill just outside the village, half-hidden by brambles and moss. My father had called it "Old Man's Grave," a name that sounded more like a curse than a place. But Astrid didn’t care about names. To her, it was a portal, a place where the living and the dead met. That night, as the sun dipped below the pine trees and the sky turned the color of bruised skin, I found myself standing at the edge of it, my shoes sinking into the damp earth, heart pounding like a drum in my chest.

Astrid had insisted we come. She’d been convincing me for weeks that the burial mounds were sacred sites, places where the old ones—my ancestors, the spirits of the land—still listened. “They’re not just stones,” she’d said, eyes glinting in the firelight as we huddled in her cabin. “They hold memories. They hold secrets.” She’d shown me old books, pages yellowed and brittle, filled with symbols and words I couldn’t understand. “This is how we talk to them,” she’d whispered, tracing a rune with her finger. “This is how we learn.”

I’d been reluctant. Moving to this village had already been hard enough. I missed Stockholm—its noise, its lights, the way the city never slept. Here, everything was quiet, too quiet. My parents had moved here because my father got a job transfer, but I hadn’t wanted to leave. I didn’t belong here. And now, I was about to sit outside in the dark with a girl who believed in things I couldn’t even begin to comprehend.

But Astrid had a way of making me feel like I was part of something bigger. Something ancient. And for a moment, I wanted to believe it.

We had prepared carefully. Astrid had gathered stones, herbs, and a small bundle of dried lavender. She’d told me to wear black, which I did, even though it felt wrong. The air was cold, and the wind carried the scent of pine and something older, something I couldn’t name. We lit a candle and placed it at the base of the mound, then sat in a circle, backs to the earth, hands clasped in front of us.

“You have to be still,” Astrid said, her voice low. “Don’t move. Don’t speak. Just listen.”

I tried to follow her instructions, but my legs were stiff, my fingers numb. The candle flickered, casting long shadows across the ground. I glanced at Astrid. She was calm, focused, like she was waiting for something. I wondered if she had done this before. If she had seen things. If she was just as scared as I was.

Then the moth landed on my shoe.

It was black, almost like a tiny shadow, and it didn’t budge when I tried to brush it away. It hovered there, wings fluttering slowly, as if it had chosen me for some reason. I looked up at Astrid, but she didn’t seem to notice. She was staring at the sky, her eyes wide, as if she could see something I couldn’t.

“Don’t move,” she said again, softer this time.

I didn’t. I forced myself to stay still. The wind picked up, carrying with it a sound—like whispers, or maybe just the rustling of leaves. I closed my eyes. I tried to imagine the earth beneath me, the bones of the old ones, the stories buried in the soil. I wanted to believe there was something there.

Then, a voice.

Not a voice in the air, but a voice in my head. It was soft, like wind through reeds, and it said, *“Come closer.”*

I gasped, and Astrid turned to me, her face pale. “Did you hear that?”

I shook my head. “No. I—”

“Don’t deny it,” she said, her voice tight. “They’re calling us.”

I wanted to leave. I wanted to run back to the cabin, to the safety of my own bed. But something held me there. The moth still sat on my shoe, unmoving. The candle flickered again, and for a moment, the world felt too big, too quiet. I felt like I was standing on the edge of something vast and unknowable.

Astrid leaned forward, whispering something in a language I didn’t understand. She pressed her palm against the earth, and I followed her lead. My fingers sank into the soil, cold and damp. I felt something—like a pulse, like a heartbeat beneath the ground. It wasn’t just the earth. It was *something* else.

Then, the wind changed.

It wasn’t just the wind anymore. It was *something* moving through it. A presence. It wrapped around me, gentle but insistent, like a hand guiding me down. I opened my eyes, and for a moment, the world seemed to shift. The stars above were different—closer, brighter, arranged in patterns I didn’t recognize. The air smelled different too, like old wood and something sweet and metallic.

Astrid was still kneeling, her eyes closed, her lips moving in silent prayer

I was sixteen, fresh out of Stockholm, still trying to unlearn the city’s noise and lights. My father had been transferred to a remote research station in northern Sweden, and we’d all moved to this village called Lappträsk, where the trees were taller and the silence louder than any I’d ever known.

I didn’t fit in at first. The locals were quiet, their eyes heavy with something I couldn’t name. But then there was Astrid. She was the kind of girl who looked like she belonged to a different century — long dark hair tied back with a faded ribbon, eyes too sharp for her age, and a laugh that echoed like a bell in the forest. She was the daughter of the village’s only carpenter, and she claimed her grandmother had taught her things — things passed down through generations, things that weren’t meant for outsiders.

I didn’t believe in that kind of stuff. Not really. But Astrid had a way of making the impossible sound like a dare. And I was desperate to belong.

So when she asked me to come along to Útiseta, I hesitated only for a second. Útiseta — I thought it was some kind of Swedish word for "going out." The burial mound was hidden behind a thicket of birch trees, half-buried in moss and time. It was an ancient place, older than the village itself, and it smelled of earth and something else — something I couldn’t place. Astrid had told me to bring a candle, a stone, and a piece of cloth. I’d asked why, and she just shrugged. “The old ways aren’t about logic. They’re about feeling.”

We sat in silence, the moon casting long shadows across the mound. Astrid lit the candle and placed it in the center, then laid the cloth over it. “Now,” she said, voice low and steady, “we wait.”

I glanced at Astrid, who was already half-dozing, her head resting against the mound. She looked so calm, so sure of herself. I envied that.

I was sixteen, a city girl from Stockholm, forced to move to this godforsaken village in northern Sweden because my father had been transferred to a remote research station. The village was called Länsfors, and it felt like something out of a fairy tale, with its wooden houses, narrow streets, and the ever-present fog that clung to the land like a secret.

My parents had tried to make the move as painless as possible, but I wasn’t fooled. I missed the buzz of the city, the lights, the life. Here, everything was quiet, too quiet. That’s when I met Astrid.

She was a local, born and raised in Länsfors, with wild curls and eyes that held more than just mischief. She had an uncanny way of knowing things—about people, about the land, about things that shouldn’t exist. She claimed her grandmother had taught her ancient practices passed down through generations. I didn’t believe a word of it, but I was bored, and Astrid was the only one who seemed to understand me.

It wasn’t long before she started inviting me to "midnight games"—small rituals, mostly harmless at first. We’d gather under the old birch tree by the lake, whispering names, drawing symbols in the dirt. Astrid said it was just fun, a way to connect with the land, to understand the old ways. But I could tell there was something more to it. Something dangerous.

Tonight, though, was different. Astrid had convinced me to join her in Útiseta—a tradition she called "sitting out," but I knew it was something far older. She said it was a way to receive knowledge from beyond, to tap into something ancient and powerful. I wasn’t sure I believed her, but I was too scared to say no. Plus, I wanted to feel like I belonged somewhere.

We set out at exactly midnight, the moon a pale eye in the sky. Astrid had prepared everything: a small bundle of herbs, a piece of birch bark, a knife with a blade that glinted like steel. I followed her without question, my heart pounding in my chest. The village was behind us now, swallowed by darkness, and the forest loomed around us like a living thing.

The burial mound was a hill of ancient stones, half-buried in moss and time. It was said to be a sacred place, used by the Sami people centuries ago. Astrid knelt at the base, placing the herbs in a circle around us. She muttered something in a language I didn’t recognize, her voice low and steady. "This is how we call them," she said. records.response_text:
I remember the night like it was yesterday, though it was almost twenty years ago. I was sixteen, fresh out of Stockholm, forced to move to this remote village in northern Sweden because my father got transferred. It was supposed to be temporary. My parents thought it would be a good experience, a chance for me to grow up, to see something different. But all I saw was endless forests, snow that never melted, and people who didn’t speak the same way. I missed the city, the lights, the noise. I missed everything.

But I wasn’t alone. Astrid found me. She was a local girl, a few years older, with wild hair and eyes that seemed to hold secrets. She didn’t care about the city or anything I said. She just wanted to show me something. She claimed her grandmother had taught her things—old ways, ancient practices passed down through generations. I didn’t believe in that kind of stuff. Not really. But Astrid was persistent, and I was lonely.

So one night, she convinced me to join her in something called Útiseta. I didn’t know what it meant, but she said it was a traditional ritual—something to “receive knowledge from beyond.” She didn’t explain much, just that it involved sitting out at a burial mound at midnight. I thought it was some kind of joke, a teenage rebellion thing. But she was serious. And I was too scared to say no.

We met at the edge of the forest, where the trees grew thick and the air felt heavy. The moon was full, casting long shadows that seemed to move on their own. Astrid had already prepared everything: a small bundle of dried herbs, a carved wooden stick, and a piece of cloth that she said was “to keep the spirits away.” I didn’t know if it was supposed to keep them away or call them in. Either way, I was nervous.

She told me to sit cross-legged on the cold earth, facing the burial mound. It was a mound of dirt and stones, half-buried in the forest, with strange markings carved into its surface. I looked at it, unsure. Astrid sat beside me, her hands steady, her voice calm. “Just listen,” she said. “The knowledge will come to you.”

I tried to focus. The forest was silent, save for the occasional rustle of leaves or the distant call of an owl. I couldn’t shake the feeling that we were being watched. Astrid began to chant something in a language I didn’t recognize, her voice low and rhythmic. I tried to mimic her, but my voice cracked and faltered. She didn’t mind. She just kept going.

Then, the moth landed on my shoe.

It was black, large, and completely still. I looked down, trying to brush it off, but it wouldn’t budge. It hovered there, like it was part of me now. I glanced at Astrid, who didn’t seem to notice. She was too focused on the ritual, too lost in whatever she thought she was doing. I felt a chill run down my spine. I told myself it was just a moth. A freak occurrence. But something about it felt wrong.

Astrid stopped chanting. “It’s not a bad sign,” she said, as if she had read my mind. “It means you’re open to the knowledge.” I wanted to argue, to tell her that I didn’t want to be open to anything, especially not something that looked like a creature from a horror movie. But I didn’t. I was too afraid to say anything.

The air around us began to change. It felt thicker, heavier. The wind stopped. I could hear my own breathing, loud and uneven. Astrid closed her eyes and placed her hands on the ground. “Now,” she whispered, “let the knowledge come.”

And then it happened. A low hum, like a distant thunder, started to build. It wasn’t coming from anywhere—it was all around us. I wanted to run, but my legs wouldn’t move. The ground beneath me seemed to vibrate. The moth on my shoe began to twitch, as if it was alive with something else. I looked up at Astrid. Her face was pale, her eyes wide. She wasn’t scared. She was… waiting.

Then, a voice. Or maybe it wasn’t a voice. It was a feeling, a presence that pressed against my mind. It wasn’t angry. It wasn’t evil. It was… curious. Like it was asking questions, trying to understand. I wanted to scream, but no sound came out. I tried to think, to push back, but the thoughts weren’t mine. They were ancient. They spoke of death, of memory, of something that had been buried long ago.

I tried to pull away, but my body was frozen. The mound behind me seemed to glow faintly, as if it was alive. The moth on my shoe had flown off, but it left something behind—a strange warmth, like a memory I wasn’t meant to remember.

Astrid gasped. “It’s working,” she said, her voice barely above a whisper. “We’ve opened the door.”

I wanted to tell her it wasn’t working. That I didn’t want to know whatever it was that was trying to reach me.

It was the summer of 1995, and I was barely sixteen, freshly transplanted from the bright lights of Stockholm to a sleepy village in northern Sweden. My father had taken a job transfer, and my parents, ever the pragmatists, had decided it was best to move with him. records.response_text:
I remember the first time I heard about Útiseta. It was a late autumn night in 1995, deep in the remote countryside of northern Sweden, where the air smelled of pine and the silence was thick enough to cut. My parents had moved us from Stockholm to this tiny village after my father’s job transfer, and I hadn’t been happy about it. The houses were too close together, the people too quiet, and the winters—well, I’d heard they were brutal. But it was Astrid who made it bearable.

Astrid was the only one who didn’t seem to mind the cold, or the isolation, or the fact that I was from the city. She was a year older than me, with wild curls and a knowing look that made me feel like I was already part of something bigger. She claimed her grandmother had taught her all sorts of things—old ways, secret knowledge, things that “kept the spirits in check.” I used to roll my eyes at her stories, but something about the way she spoke, so confidently, so casually, made me curious.

It started with small things—midnight games, things like drawing names out of a hat and whispering over them, or lighting candles in the shape of numbers and watching how the flames flickered. Astrid would say they were just old traditions, meant to teach us about ourselves, about the world. But there was something in her voice when she said it, like she knew more than she was letting on. And I, for some reason, wanted to know too.

Then came Útiseta. Astrid called it “the sitting out.” She said it was an ancient practice, a way to “receive knowledge from beyond.” I didn’t believe in ghosts, not really, but I didn’t want to be the one who said no. So I went.

We met at the edge of the forest, where the trees were gnarled and the ground was soft with moss. The air was already cold, and the sky was a deep indigo, lit only by the pale light of the moon. Astrid had brought a small satchel filled with things I couldn’t quite name—burnt sage, a bundle of dried lavender, a stone that looked like it had been carved by hands long gone. She lit a candle and held it up, its flickering light casting strange shadows across her face.

“Okay,” she said, her voice low and steady. “We need to clear our minds. No distractions. Just us and the spirits.”

I nodded, trying not to show how nervous I was. I had a feeling this was going to be more than just a spooky night out.

She started reciting words in a language I didn’t recognize—something ancient, guttural, like wind through trees. And then, without warning, the temperature dropped. It was like someone had turned down the heat in the entire world. I shivered, pulling my scarf tighter around my neck, but Astrid didn’t flinch. She continued, her voice unwavering.

It happened again when she said the next phrase. The air turned icy, and I felt my breath catch in my throat. I glanced at her, and she met my eyes, unblinking. “It’s just the cold,” she said, as if it were the most normal thing in the world. “The spirits like it cold.”

I didn’t believe her, but I didn’t argue. Instead, I focused on the ritual. We sat on the ground, backs pressed against the cold stone of the burial mound, which Astrid had told me was over a thousand years old. She had read about it in some old book her grandmother had left her, and she insisted it was the perfect place for Útiseta.

She told us to close our eyes and listen. “The spirits are always watching,” she said. “They just need to be invited in.”

I tried to imagine what that meant. Was it like a conversation? A test? Or was it something more… dangerous?

The minutes dragged. The sky above us was dark, and the wind picked up, howling through the trees like it was trying to tear the world apart. Astrid kept reciting the phrases, and every time she did, the temperature would drop again—sometimes just a little, sometimes so much I felt like I was sitting on ice.

I started to feel uneasy. It wasn’t just the cold. It was the way the silence felt heavy, like it was holding its breath. And then, just as I was about to ask her to stop, I heard it.

A sound. Not wind, not animals, but something else. A whisper—soft, like a voice just on the edge of hearing. It wasn’t coming from anywhere, but it was there, right next to me.

I opened my eyes, and Astrid was still sitting there, her face pale in the candlelight. She didn’t look surprised. She looked… expectant.

“Did you hear it?” I asked, my voice barely above a whisper.

She nodded. “They’re listening.”

I wanted to run, but I stayed rooted in place. Something about her certainty made me feel like I shouldn’t look away. I closed my eyes again, trying to focus, to make sense of what was happening. Was it my imagination?
