“Lost, huh?” he said without looking up.

I hesitated. I didn’t know if I should answer. I’d been taught to be careful with strangers. Especially after what happened back home.

“Just… looking for somewhere to sleep,” I finally said.

He chuckled, a low, raspy sound. “You’re not the first. But you’re the first one who looked like they actually *meant* it.”

He put down his saxophone and turned to face me. His name was Jax, he said, and he’d been playing on the street for years. He had a tattoo of a phoenix on his wrist, and I immediately thought of my own scars — the ones I hid under long sleeves and tight clothes.

“I used to be a cop,” he said, as if reading my mind. “Until I realized I’d rather chase ghosts than people.”

I didn’t know what to say. I mean, I had my own ghosts. I’d spent years running from them, too. But Jax didn’t seem like a threat. He looked like he’d been through hell and back, and somehow still had a laugh in him.

“Where you headed?” he asked.

I shrugged. “Nowhere. Just… trying to figure it out.”

He nodded like he understood. “You’re not alone, kid. Not in this city. Not in this world.”

I wanted to believe him. I wanted to trust him. But I’d been burned before.

“I don’t know if I can trust anyone,” I admitted, my voice quiet.

He looked at me for a long moment, then pulled out a crumpled piece of paper from his pocket. It was a list — names, addresses, places. “This is a map of the underbelly of this city. Safe spots. People who don’t ask questions. You’re not the first to run, and you won’t be the last.”

I stared at the paper. “Why are you helping me?”

He smiled, but it didn’t reach his eyes. “Because I’ve been there. And I know what it’s like to be invisible. To feel like you don’t belong.”

That hit me harder than I expected. I’d spent so long trying to fit in, to be seen, and when I finally decided I wasn’t going to pretend anymore — to be *me* — I got thrown out. I didn’t belong anywhere. Not in Tennessee. Not in the world I thought I knew.

“Do you… do you ever feel like you’re just waiting to be found?” I asked, more to myself than to him.

Jax paused. “Yeah. All the time. But sometimes, you gotta find yourself before someone else does.”

He handed me the paper. “You’re not alone, kid. Not here. Not anywhere.”

I nodded, unsure, but grateful. He didn’t push me to stay, didn’t demand anything. Just gave me a chance. And for the first time in days, I felt like maybe I wasn’t completely lost.

“I used to play like that,” I say, almost to myself. “Before everything went sideways.”

He stops. “Everything goes sideways, kid. That’s just life.”

“I’m Taylor,” I say, finally.

He smiles slightly. “Nice to meet you, Taylor.”

We walk for a while after that, Jax carrying his saxophone, me carrying my hoodie and my hope. He points things out — the best places to eat, the safest places to sleep, the ones to avoid. He’s not a hero, not exactly, but he’s got a kind of wisdom that feels real.

“Lost?” she asks, not unkindly.

I shrug. “Kind of.”

She smirks. “Yeah. Me too.”

Her name is Lila, and she’s a runaway from somewhere in Texas. She says she left because her mom “thought she was a curse” and her dad “thought she was a distraction.” She doesn’t sound bitter—just tired. We talk, and somehow, the conversation flows like it’s been waiting to happen.

She tells me about her last job, flipping burgers at a diner that smelled like regret. I tell her about my family, how they didn’t believe me when I told them I was trans, how they called me “a phase” and “a mistake.” We both laugh, but it’s a hollow laugh. We’re not strangers anymore, but we’re still strangers.

Lila says she’s staying in a shelter for now, but she’s got a plan. “I’m going to get my GED, then I’m going to move to California. I’ve got a cousin who’s a teacher. She’ll take me in.” She’s optimistic, but there’s a crack in her voice when she says it.

I ask her if she’s ever run away before. She pauses. “Yeah. Once. When I was 14. I thought I could just disappear for a while. I didn’t realize how hard it was to not be seen.”

I think about that. I think about the way my parents’ voices echo in my head, the way they used to say things like “you’re still our child” but never meant it. I wonder if I ever really was.

We end up walking through the French Quarter, Lila showing me places she likes—cafés with good coffee, street vendors selling beignets, a bookstore that smells like old paper and secrets. She’s got a way of making the city feel like home, even if it’s not.

“I used to think New Orleans was just about the music and the Mardi Gras,” she says. “But it’s also about people. Like, real people. You know? People who don’t fit.”

I nod. “Yeah. I think I’m one of them.”

We sit on a stoop outside a bar, people laughing and clinking glasses inside. Lila pulls out a cigarette, offers me one. I decline, but I don’t say no. I’ve smoked before—when I was 15, when I thought I had nothing left to lose.

She lights it, and the smoke curls up like a question mark. “So, what’s your story?”

I hesitate. “It’s not a story. It’s a disaster.”

She laughs. “That’s the first sign of a good one.”

I try to smile, but it doesn’t reach my eyes. “I came out to my family two days ago. They didn’t take it well. I didn’t think they’d be that… violent.” I trail off. “I didn’t think they’d say things like, ‘You’re not even real.’

records.response_text:
**Chapter 3: Gone South**

I’ve never been good at staying put. Not really. Even before I came out, I was always running—just not from people, but from the noise of them. The small town of Mill Creek, Tennessee, had everything: a gas station that doubled as a diner, a church that played hymns louder than a rock concert, and a school counselor who called my gender transition “concerning behavioural patterns.” Yeah, that’s how it went down. Two days ago, I told my mom I was trans. She didn’t cry. She didn’t yell. She just said, “We’ll talk about it later.” And then she didn’t. So I packed a duffel bag, stole a jacket from my dad’s closet, and left. 

I didn’t think I’d make it out of the state. But I did. Two states away, in the middle of New Orleans, I’m standing on a crowded street, holding a stolen backpack, trying not to look like a lost cause. My feet hurt. My stomach growls. And the humidity is thick enough to slice through my skin. 

A saxophone is playing in the background, low and mournful, like it's trying to understand me too. The music wraps around me, a weird comfort in the chaos. I don’t know if it’s the sound or the fact that I’m finally somewhere where no one knows me, but for the first time in days, I feel… free. Not in the way I thought I would. Free isn’t a feeling that comes with running. It’s a feeling that comes with not being chased.

I walk past street vendors hawking beignets and doubloons, past people in sequins and sunglasses, past tourists taking selfies like they’re in a movie. I’m invisible, I think. Or maybe I just don’t belong here.

That’s when I see her.

She’s leaning against a brick wall, her boots scuffed, her jeans patched, and her eyes tired. She’s got a leather jacket that’s too big for her, and a hoodie that reads “I’m not a morning person.” She’s smoking a cigarette, and when she sees me, she doesn’t look surprised. Like she’s seen this before.

“Lost?” she asks, blowing out a smoke ring that floats in the air like a question.

I hesitate. “Not really. Just… not sure where I’m going.”

She smirks. “That’s a good start.” She takes a drag, then flicks the butt into a trash can. “Name’s Mara. And you? Don’t bother lying. I can tell.”

I open my mouth to say something smart, but all that comes out is, “Taylor.”

She nods. “Well, Taylor. You’re either brave or stupid. Probably both.”

I don’t know what to say. She’s not exactly the type of person I expected to meet—someone who looks like they’ve been through hell and back, but still has the energy to joke.

“Where are you headed?” she asks.

I shrug. “Nowhere. Just… trying to figure it out.”

She laughs. “You’re not the first. But you might be the first who actually *wants* to figure it out.”

I don’t know what to make of that. Maybe I don’t want to figure it out. Maybe I just want to disappear. But the thought of being alone in a city like this—where everything is loud and bright and full of people who don’t know me—feels too much. So I nod. “Okay. Where do you sleep?”

She points down an alley. “That’s my spot. But you’re not gonna like it. It smells like old pizza and regret.”

I follow her, and the saxophone fades into the background as we walk. The city is alive, but in a way that feels dangerous. I’m not used to this kind of energy. In Mill Creek, everything was slow and predictable. Here, everything is fast and unpredictable. And I’m not sure if I like it.

We end up in a tiny, run-down apartment above a laundromat. It smells like mildew and smoke. Mara tosses me a blanket and a mattress on the floor. “You can take the couch. It’s not comfortable, but it’s dry.”

I lay down, and for a moment, I just breathe. It’s the first time I’ve been able to do that since I left.

“Why’d you run?” she asks, sitting on the edge of the mattress.

I stare at the ceiling. “My family… didn’t get it. They didn’t want me to be me. They just wanted me to be… something else.”

She’s quiet for a second. “Yeah. I know that feeling.”

I turn my head to look at her. “You ran too?”

She nods. “Yeah. I left my hometown when I was 15. My mom was an addict. My dad was a cop who didn’t know how to handle anything that wasn’t a crime. I was just… in the way.”

I feel something in my chest tighten. “You’re not… like me?”

She laughs again. “No. I’m not. I’m just… broken. Like everyone else.”

I think about that

**Chapter 3: The City That Doesn’t Sleep**

I’ve never been good at staying put. Not really. You know, I mean, I’ve lived in the same town my whole life—Tennessee, backwoods, small enough that everyone knows your business before you do. And I’ve always been the one who knew more than they wanted to. So, yeah, running away wasn’t exactly a surprise. It was just… unexpected how fast it all happened. Two days ago, I came out to my family. Two days ago, I got kicked out. Two days ago, I decided I needed a new town, and a new life. Now I’m in New Orleans, standing in the middle of a street that smells like hot sauce and regret, trying to figure out if I’m going to die of heatstroke or be arrested for loitering.

The saxophone is playing in the background—some street musician with a worn-out instrument and a voice that sounds like it’s been through a blender. It’s sad, but somehow fitting. I used to listen to jazz when I was younger, before everything got too complicated. Now, it just feels like a reminder of all the things I’ve lost.

I’m dressed in whatever I could stuff into a backpack: a hoodie that’s seen better days, jeans that are too tight, and a pair of shoes that have been walking on concrete for the past 12 hours. My phone is dead. My wallet is gone. My family thinks I’m dead. And I don’t know if I’m more scared or relieved.

I walk past a busker with a guitar, a guy in a trench coat who looks like he’s been through hell and back. He’s singing something about love and loss, and I almost stop to listen. But then I remember the last time I let someone get close—my mom, my sister, even my best friend, Jamie. All of them, in their own ways, let me down. So I keep walking.

The city is loud, alive, and terrifying. People are everywhere, moving like they have somewhere to be, and I feel like I’m just… floating. I don’t belong here. Not yet.

I end up in a park near the river, sitting on a bench that’s covered in graffiti. It’s weird—graffiti in a place that’s supposed to be peaceful. Someone’s painted a phoenix with wings made of broken hearts. I stare at it for a while, trying to make sense of it, but I can’t. Life’s too messy for symbols.

That’s when I see her.

She’s sitting a few benches over, legs crossed, wearing a leather jacket that’s too big for her, and a hoodie that says “I’m not a monster.” Her eyes are tired, but there’s something sharp about them—like she’s been through hell and learned to survive. She’s got a small knife tucked into her boot, and a scar running from her eyebrow to her jaw. I immediately assume she’s a gang member or a drug dealer or something. But then she looks up and sees me, and for a second, I think I see something in her eyes that’s familiar: fear, maybe. Or maybe just exhaustion.

“Lost?” she asks, not unkindly.

I hesitate. I don’t know what to say. I’ve been so used to being alone, I’m not sure how to start a conversation with someone else who’s running.

“Sort of,” I say, shrugging. “I mean, I’m not exactly lost, but I’m not exactly… found either.”

She smirks. “That’s a good line.”

I’m surprised. I didn’t think anyone would find my sarcasm amusing. Maybe I’ve been around too much drama.

“Name’s Lexi,” she says, extending a hand. “You’re not from around here, are you?”

I shake her hand, and her grip is firm, like she’s used to holding on tight. “Taylor,” I say. “I’m not from anywhere. Or anywhere I want to be.”

She nods. “Well, you’re in the right place then. This city’s got a way of taking people in, and then letting them out when they’re ready.”

I raise an eyebrow. “So you’re one of them?”

She laughs, low and bitter. “I was. For a while. Now I’m just… trying to survive.”

I sit down beside her. The sun is starting to set, casting long shadows across the park. The saxophone music fades into the hum of the city.

“So what’s your deal?” I ask. “Why are you running?”

Lexi looks away, staring at the river. “You know, I’ve asked myself that question a lot. I used to think it was because I hated my parents. I used to think I was running from them, from their expectations, from their disappointment. But now I think I was just running from myself.”

I watch her for a second. There’s something raw in her voice, something that makes me want to listen more.

“What did you do?” I ask quietly.

She exhales, like she’s holding back a storm. “I was in foster care for a long time. They said I was ‘difficult.’ They said I was ‘unmanageable.’ I didn’t fit

He laughs. “That’s what they call it when you’re too scared to stay.”

records.response_text:
**Chapter 3: Gone South**

So, I’m in New Orleans. Two states away from home. Two states away from everything. And my phone battery is at 12%. No charger. No plan. Just me, a backpack full of clothes that don’t fit anymore, and a heart that’s been doing a terrible job of staying steady.

I got out of that town like I was running from a fire, and I thought maybe this would be the escape I needed. But now, standing on the sidewalk in the middle of Bourbon Street, I feel like I’m just another lost kid with nowhere to go. The city is loud, smoky, and full of people who look like they know where they’re going. I don’t.

I’m used to being the weird one. The one who doesn’t fit. But here, I feel like I’m just… invisible. Or maybe I’m too loud. I keep checking my phone, hoping it’ll light up with a message from Mom or a text from someone who *actually* cares. But nothing. Just the blinking red light, taunting me.

I remember the day I told my family I was trans. I thought they’d understand. I thought they’d support me. But all I got was silence, then anger, then a door slamming in my face. I didn’t even get to say goodbye. I just… left. My mom’s voice still echoes in my head: *“You’re making a mistake.”* Like I’m the one who’s wrong. Like I’m the one who’s choosing this.

I used to think being trans was something you just *became*. Like a label you put on. But now I know it’s more than that. It’s who I am. And I can’t keep living like I’m not.

So I ran. I packed my stuff, got a bus to Memphis, then another to New Orleans. It was all so quick, so reckless. I didn’t think about the consequences. I didn’t think about the cold, or the hunger, or the fact that I’d be stranded in a city I didn’t know.

Now, I’m hungry. And tired. And I have no idea what to do next.

I start walking, just aimlessly, trying to find somewhere to sleep. I pass a street musician playing a guitar, his voice raspy and sad. I stop for a second, listening. It’s a song about love and loss, and I feel like I’ve been through both. I toss him a dollar. He smiles, like he knows exactly what I’m going through.

“Lost, huh?” he asks, not looking up from his guitar.

“Something like that,” I say, shrugging.

He nods. “Well, New Orleans is a city that takes in the lost. You’ll find your way.”

I don’t believe him. I’ve been lost before. And it didn’t end well.

I end up in a park, sitting on a bench under a tree that’s older than I am. The sun is setting, casting long shadows across the grass. I take out my phone again—just to check the time. 6:47 PM. Battery at 10%. I close it, not wanting to see the time anymore.

**Chapter 3: Gone South**

I’ve never been good at staying put. Not really. I mean, I’ve lived in the same town my whole life, but that’s not the same as *being* there. You can live somewhere and still feel like you're escaping it. Like I am now.

I landed in New Orleans at 3:17 a.m. on a street corner that smelled like fried food, sweat, and something I can't quite name. My feet hurt. My backpack feels heavier than it should. And my phone—oh, my phone—has 12% battery left. No charger. No signal. No plan.

I had a plan, though. Or at least, I thought I did. I called a friend from a gas station in Mississippi, who said they’d meet me in New Orleans. But I didn’t wait. I got on a bus. A bus that didn’t go to New Orleans. A bus that eventually did, because buses are like that. And now here I am, stuck in a city I’ve only seen in movies, with a heart full of panic and a mind full of questions.

I’m trans. I’ve been trans for a long time. But I didn’t come out until two days ago. And it didn’t go well. My parents didn’t take it well. My brother laughed. My mom cried. My dad just... stared. Like he was trying to figure out if I was a hallucination or something. I didn’t stay to find out.

So I ran. I ran like a coward, like I always do. And now I’m here, in a city that’s supposed to be full of music, culture, and danger. I’m not sure which of those I’m ready for.

I’m sitting on a bench outside a diner, eating a greasy burger and trying not to cry. The sky is pink and orange, like it’s trying to apologize for everything. I take a sip of lukewarm coffee, and my phone buzzes. It’s a text from my friend Avery, the one who was supposed to meet me.

**Avery:** Where are you?

**Taylor:** I’m in New Orleans. I think I’m in the right place.

**Avery:** Wait, what? You’re not supposed to be here yet.

**Taylor:** I didn’t wait.

**Avery:** Taylor, you’re gonna get yourself killed.

**Taylor:** I’m already dead.

The message makes me laugh, but it doesn’t feel like a good laugh. I close my phone, not wanting to see the time. It’s 8:30 a.m. I’ve been here for five hours. I’m starting to think maybe Avery was right. Maybe I *am* gonna get killed.

And then I see them.

A girl, maybe my age, sitting on the sidewalk with a sketchbook. She’s wearing a hoodie that’s too big, and her hair is a wild mess of curls and dye. She looks like she’s been sleeping on the street for days. But she’s drawing something—something detailed, like she’s trying to capture the world in a way no one else can.

I hesitate. I’ve been alone for a while, and I’m not sure if I can handle another person right now. But something about her—her eyes, maybe—makes me want to talk.

“Hey,” I say, voice cracking. “You lost?”

She looks up, startled. “No. I’m not lost. I’m just... here.”

“Me too.”

She smiles a little, like she knows what that feels like. “You look like you’ve been running.”

“I have.”

She opens her sketchbook to show me a drawing of a city—New Orleans, I think. “I’ve been drawing it for weeks. But I’ve never been here. Just imagining it.”

“I’m not sure I can imagine it. I’ve only ever seen it in movies.”

She laughs, and it’s the first real laugh I’ve heard all day. “Movies don’t do it justice.”

“Yeah, well, I’m not sure I’m ready for the real thing.”

She pauses, then says, “I get it. I used to run too. From my family, from everything. I was 15 when I left. Now I’m 17. And I’m still running.”

I look at her, and for the first time since I left home, I feel like someone understands.

“Name’s Lena,” she says.

“Taylor.”

“Nice to meet you, Taylor. So, what’s your story?”

I hesitate. I don’t want to tell her everything. But something about her makes me feel safe. Like maybe I don’t have to be alone.

“I came out to my family two days ago,” I start. “And it didn’t go well. They didn’t... they didn’t accept it. I just... I couldn’t stay. So I ran.”

Lena nods, like she’s heard this before. “I came out to my parents when I was 14. They kicked me out. I ended up in a shelter for a while. Then I got on the road.”

I’m silent for a second. “You’re... still on the road?”

“I’m trying to find somewhere to stay. I’m not sure where

**Chapter 3: Gone South**

I’ve been in New Orleans for less than twelve hours and already I feel like I’ve been here forever. The air smells like fried food and something older, something like regret. I’m standing on the corner of Royal and Dauphine, watching a street performer juggle flaming torches while a group of tourists gawps like they’ve never seen a human being before. My phone battery is at 12%. No charger. No plan. No family. Just me and a backpack full of clothes that don’t fit anymore.

I was supposed to meet someone at the bus station, but that was two hours ago. I lost track of time somewhere between Tennessee and Louisiana. The bus ride was a blur of bad seats, bad music, and my own thoughts looping like a broken record. I thought about my mom’s face when I told her I was trans. She didn’t cry. She just said, “Taylor, I love you,” and then hung up. Like that was the end of it. It wasn’t. It was the beginning of something worse.

I’m not used to being alone. Not like this. I used to have my best friend, Jamie, who used to call me “Tay-Tay” and pretend I was a cat. Now I’m just... me. A kid who ran away from a town that didn’t want me. A kid who doesn’t know where to go next.

“Hey, you lost?”

I jump. A girl with a leather jacket and a pierced eyebrow is leaning against a lamppost, smirking. She’s got a sketchbook tucked under one arm and a cigarette between her fingers. She looks like she’s been through hell and back, and somehow still knows how to look cool.

“Not lost,” I say, trying to sound confident. “Just... exploring.”

She raises an eyebrow. “Exploring? You look like you’ve been exploring for a while.”

I don’t want to tell her I’ve been on the run for two days. I don’t want to tell her I left my family behind, or that I don’t know how to use a map. I don’t even know how to use a phone anymore, honestly. I just press buttons and hope for the best.

“I’m Lexi,” she says, tapping the cigarette against the lamppost. “And you? You’re either really brave or really stupid. I’m leaning toward stupid.”

“I’m Taylor,” I say. “And I’m... neither.”

She laughs, and it’s the kind of laugh that makes you want to trust her, even if you don’t know her. “Okay, Taylor. You’re either a rebel or a rebel in training. Which is it?”

“I’m... neither.”

She shrugs. “Well, that’s a shame. You’re standing in the middle of the French Quarter, and you don’t even know where the nearest café is?”

“I know where the nearest café is,” I say, pointing down the street. “That one. With the giant statue of a cat.”

She looks at me like I’ve just said the most ridiculous thing ever. “That’s a statue of a cat? Are you serious?”

“I’ve seen worse,” I say, and then immediately regret it. I don’t know why I said that. It feels too much like I’m trying to be funny, but I’m not. I’m just tired.

Lexi smirks again. “You’re weird. But I like that. Come on, I’m hungry, and I’ve got a friend who works at a café that takes cash. No questions asked. Just bring your ID.”

I hesitate. “What if I don’t have ID?”

She snorts. “Then you’re in trouble, pal. But I’m not going to let a kid with no place to go starve to death on my watch.”

I look at her. She’s got a scar running along her jawline, and her eyes are sharp, like she’s seen a lot. I don’t know what she’s running from, but I feel like she’s been there. I feel like she gets it.

“Okay,” I say. “Lead the way.”

We walk, and I notice she’s got a tattoo on her wrist—a serpent coiled around a heart. I ask her about it later. She says it’s her “safe word.” Something about a past she doesn’t talk about. I don’t push.

The café is tiny, with mismatched chairs and a smell like coffee and hope. The barista is a guy named Duke, who looks like he’s seen more of the world than most people ever will. He doesn’t ask questions. Just takes our cash and hands us two cups of coffee.

“Free refill if you tell me your story,” he says, winking.

I almost say no. But I’m tired of being careful. So I do.

“I ran away from home. My family... they didn’t take it well. I came out two days ago. They didn’t understand. They didn’t care. So I left.”

Lexi’s eyes soften. “That’s... heavy.”

“I know,” I say, taking a sip of coffee. It’s bitter, but it tastes good. “I just... I don’t know what to do now.”

Duke nods. “

**Chapter 3: Gone South**

I’ve been in New Orleans for less than 24 hours, and already I feel like a ghost in a city that’s alive with ghosts. The air smells like fried food and regret. My boots are soaked from the rain that started an hour ago, and my phone battery is at 12%. No charger. No plan. Just me, a backpack full of clothes that don’t fit, and a heart that’s been pounding like a war drum since I left Tennessee.

I’m not sure what I expected when I packed my bag and ran out of my house that night. Maybe a little peace. Maybe a chance to breathe. But what I got was a 12-hour bus ride, a lost wallet, and a flat tire that left me stranded in the middle of Bourbon Street with nothing but a hoodie and a burning need to disappear.

I found a shelter last night—some kind of soup kitchen run by a woman named Ms. Ruby who looked like she’d seen too much and had learned to trust no one. She gave me a blanket, a plate of gumbo, and a warning: “You don’t just run from home, kid. You run from yourself.”

I didn’t have the words to argue.

Now it’s morning. I’m sitting on a park bench, watching the sun rise over the Mississippi, trying not to cry because I’ve run out of tears. My phone is dead, so I can’t call my mom. I can’t call my sister. I can’t even call the one person who might’ve understood—my old best friend, Jamie, who used to say I was too dramatic for my own good. Maybe I still am.

I’m so tired of pretending I’m not who I am.

Then I see her.

She’s sitting a few feet away, legs crossed, eating a bag of chips with one hand and scrolling through her phone with the other. Her hair is a wild mess of curls, and she’s wearing a band T-shirt that says *“I’m not a fan of the police.”* She looks like she’s been through hell and somehow still managed to look cool about it.

I hesitate. I’ve met a lot of people on the run, and most of them are either dangerous or desperate. But something about her vibe feels… familiar.

I clear my throat. “Hey. You lost too?”

She looks up, eyes sharp, like she’s expecting a threat. “No. I’m not lost. I’m just… waiting for someone.”

I sit down beside her. “Waiting for someone?”

She nods. “My brother. He’s supposed to pick me up at 9. He didn’t show. I figured I’d wait a while, then go find him myself. But honestly? I’m not sure I want to.”

I feel a weird connection, like we’re both stuck in the same bad dream. “You’re running too?”

She smirks. “Running? I’m *escaping*. There’s a difference.”

I want to argue, but I don’t have the energy. “Yeah. I get it.”

We sit in silence for a moment, listening to the sounds of the city—people laughing, a street musician playing a blues tune, the distant hum of traffic. It’s loud and alive, and for the first time in days, I feel like I might be okay.

“Name’s Lila,” she says finally. “What’s yours?”

“Taylor.”

She nods. “Okay, Taylor. So, tell me—what’s your story?”

I hesitate. I don’t know how much to tell. I mean, what’s the point? I’m already a mess. But Lila seems… real. Not the kind of person who’d judge or try to fix me. Just… listen.

I blink. “You’ve run before?”

She nods. “Yeah. I ran from my mom. She’s an addict. I used to take care of her. But I couldn’t do it anymore. So I left. I ended up in Dallas for a while. Then Austin. Now I’m here.”

She laughs bitterly. “I had a plan. I thought I’d find my brother and we’d figure it out. But he’s not here. And I’m not sure I want to go back.”

“I don’t have a plan either,” I admit. “But maybe we can figure it out together.”

She studies me for a moment, then nods. “Alright. I guess I could use a friend.”

**Chapter 3: Gone South**

I’ve never been good at staying put. Not really. I mean, I’ve lived in the same town my whole life—Pineville, Tennessee, population: 2,147, and 13 of those people are my relatives. I guess that’s not exactly *the* backwater, but it’s close enough. So when I finally decided I had had enough, I didn’t think it would take me *two states* to get out of town. I thought I’d just grab my backpack, sneak out of the house, and head toward the highway. But I didn’t count on the car breaking down halfway to Memphis, or the guy I met at the gas station trying to sell me a “special deal” on a used van, or the fact that I had no money, no plan, and a phone battery at 12%.

I ended up in New Orleans. Downtown. At 2 a.m. In the middle of a city I only knew from music videos and tourist brochures. I had no idea where I was, no idea how to get out of the way of the traffic, and no idea how to explain why I looked like I’d just crawled out of a dumpster.

I’m still not sure I’ve figured out any of that.

The first thing I noticed was the smell. It wasn’t the usual pine-scented air of Tennessee. This was wet, smoky, and somehow *alive*. Like the city was breathing, and I had just stumbled into its lungs.

I found a bench in a park, sat down, and tried to catch my breath. My legs were shaking, my head was spinning, and my phone was dying. I had a few messages from my mom—she’d been calling every hour since I left. I didn’t answer. Not yet. I couldn’t. Not until I knew where I was going.

I pulled out my phone, checked the battery. 12%. I groaned. No charger. No money. No plan.

Then I heard it: a voice, rough and low, calling out from the shadows.

“Hey, you okay?”

I froze. My instincts screamed *run*, but I was too tired. I looked up and saw a guy leaning against a lamppost, arms crossed, wearing a leather jacket that looked like it had seen better days. He had a scar running down his cheek and a tattoo of a dragon on his neck. He looked like he could take me apart with one hand.

“I’m fine,” I muttered, shifting my backpack higher on my shoulders.

He raised an eyebrow. “You look like you’ve been through hell.”

I didn’t respond. I didn’t know what to say. I’d been through hell, but I wasn’t ready to talk about it. Not yet.

He sighed. “I’m Jax. You lost?”

“I’m not lost,” I said, trying to sound confident. “I’m just… visiting.”

He smirked. “Yeah, sure. You look like someone who’s been running from something.”

I didn’t like that. I didn’t like how he knew me. I didn’t like how I felt like I was being judged. But something about him felt… familiar. Like I’d seen that look before—someone who had been through the same thing.

“I’m not running,” I said, more defiantly than I intended.

He shrugged. “Well, that’s a shame. This city’s not safe for people who don’t know how to hide.”

I frowned. “What do you mean?”

He didn’t answer immediately. Instead, he stepped closer, and I caught a glimpse of a tattoo on his wrist—a heart with a knife through it.

“I mean,” he said finally, “you don’t just show up here and expect to be okay. You’ve got to know the rules.”

I crossed my arms. “What rules?”

He glanced around, then pulled out a cigarette and lit it with a lighter that looked like it had been through a war. “First rule: don’t trust anyone. Second rule: don’t let them see your face. Third rule: if you’re lost, find someone who’s lost too.”

I studied him. There was something in his eyes—something guarded, something tired. Like he’d been through a lot too.

“I’m not looking for a mentor,” I said, but even as I said it, I felt a flicker of curiosity.

He smirked again. “You’re not. You’re looking for someone who gets it.”

I didn’t know what to say to that. I wanted to say *no*, but I also wanted to know more. I wanted to know if there was someone out there who understood what it was like to be hunted, to be invisible, to be *seen* in a way you never wanted to be.

“I’m Taylor,” I said finally.

He nodded. “I know.”

My stomach dropped. “You do?”

He nodded again. “Yeah. You ran out of Pineville two days ago. Left your family in a mess. You were pretty loud about it, too. You screamed like a banshee at the house.”

I felt my face heat up. “I didn’t scream.”

“Yeah, you did.”

I looked away. I didn’t want to admit it. I didn’t

records.response_text:
**Chapter Two: Gone South**

I’ve never been good at staying put. Not really. Not since I was eleven and I got kicked out of Sunday school for drawing a rainbow on the bulletin board and calling the priest a “rainbow chaser.” But this? This is different. This isn’t just a prank or a rebellion—it’s survival. I left my family behind two days ago, after they told me I was “confused” and that I needed to “pray about it.” I didn’t wait for them to pray. I ran.

Now I’m in New Orleans, with a busted phone battery at 12%, no charger, and a backpack full of clothes that don’t fit anymore. I should be scared. I *am* scared. But I’m also... something else. Hope? Maybe. Or maybe just the kind of desperate hope that comes from knowing you’ve already lost everything.

I stumbled into the Quarter around noon, tired, hungry, and more than a little lost. The streets were alive with music, people, and the kind of energy that makes you want to either dance or run. I didn’t do either. I just kept walking, trying not to look like a target. 

That’s when I saw her.

She was sitting on the steps of a closed-down café, her feet propped up on a milk crate, wearing a hoodie that was too big and a necklace with a cracked locket. Her eyes were red, like she’d been crying—or maybe just been through too much. She looked familiar, like I’d seen her somewhere before. Or maybe I’d just seen too many people in my life who looked like they were about to break.

I hesitated. I didn’t know what to do. But then she looked up, and I knew I wasn’t alone anymore.

“Hey,” she said, voice rough. “You lost?”

I almost laughed. “No. I’m just... not sure where I’m going.”

She smirked. “Me too.”

Her name was Jax. She was 17, like me. She’d run from a group home in Alabama, after they tried to “rehabilitate” her by giving her a diagnosis of “borderline personality disorder” and locking her in a room for hours because she “acted out.” She had a history of cutting, of running, of trying to make sense of a world that didn’t want to understand her.

We ended up sitting on the steps for hours, talking about everything and nothing. She told me about her mom, who left when she was eight, and her foster parents, who called her “unstable.” I told her about my family, the way they called me “confused,” the way they made me feel like I was the problem.

“I’m not a problem,” I said, voice cracking. “I’m just... different.”

“Yeah,” she said. “And that’s a problem in some places.”

We shared food—sandwiches we’d stolen from a bodega, a slice of pizza we bought with my last dollar. She had a knife in her pocket, just in case. I had a pocketknife I’d taken from my dad’s toolbox, which I hadn’t even realized I was carrying until I fumbled it out.

“Do you trust me?” she asked, halfway through the pizza.

I shrugged. “I don’t know. I trust people sometimes. Most of the time, I don’t.”

She laughed, but it didn’t sound happy. “Same here.”

We split up that night, but not before agreeing to meet at the same spot the next day. I didn’t know what I was doing, but I knew I wasn’t doing it alone anymore.

The rest of the day was a blur of wandering, trying to find something—anything—that felt like home. I got a job at a diner, just for a few hours, helping flip pancakes and pretending I didn’t notice the way the manager kept glancing at me like I was a mistake. I tried to sleep in a park, but the noise and the heat kept me awake. I got lost twice, once in a neighborhood that looked like a dream and once in a place that felt like a nightmare.

My phone battery was down to 5%. I had no signal. No way to call my mom. No way to call anyone.

I kept thinking about my family. They’d called me “concerning.” They’d called me a “problem.” But I wasn’t a problem. I was just... me. A kid who had to figure out who they were in a world that didn’t want to see them.

I thought about Jax. She was messy, unpredictable, and kind of scary. But she was also real. And for the first time in a long time, I didn’t feel like I was alone.

That night, I met someone else. A guy named Malik, who worked at a used bookstore and knew more about poetry than I did. He gave me a book—*The Bell Jar*—and said, “You need this. You’re already in a bell jar, kid. Just don’t let it crush you.”

I didn’t know what to say. I just nodded. He didn’t push for answers. He just let me be.

I ended up staying at a shelter, a place that smelled like old coffee and hope

**Chapter Three: Gone South**

I’ve never been good at staying put. Not really. Even before I came out, I was always running—either from something, or to something, or just because I needed to feel like I was in control of my own life. But this? This is different. This isn’t just a weekend escape or a midnight drive with friends. This is a full-on, no-coming-back kind of run. And I’m in New Orleans, which, honestly, is way more chaotic than I expected. 

I got here in a stolen minivan that broke down in the middle of Bourbon Street. The driver? A guy I met in a gas station who thought he was being cool by offering me a ride. He said he was “just passing through” and “had a bunch of extra stuff.” I didn’t ask questions. I didn’t have time. I just needed to get out of Tennessee. 

Now I’m standing in front of a neon-lit bar, my backpack slung over one shoulder, my phone at 12% with no charger in sight. I’ve got two hours of battery left. That’s not much, but it’s enough to call Mom—assuming I can find a signal. But I’m not calling Mom. Not yet. Not after what happened. 

I remember the way the air felt in my house that night. Like it was holding its breath. My mom’s voice, trembling, asking, “Are you sure?” My dad, silent. My sister, who I hadn’t even told yet, just… watching. I wanted to scream. I wanted to run. And I did. 

New Orleans is loud, and I mean *loud*. The music, the people, the smell of fried food and sweat and something sweet and spicy that I can’t place. It’s overwhelming. I feel like I’m going to pass out, or cry, or both. 

I’m not used to being alone. Not like this. Not with no plan and no backup. 

A voice cuts through the noise. “You lost?”

I jump. A girl, maybe my age, is leaning against the door of a biker bar, wearing a leather jacket and a smirk that says *I know something you don’t know*. Her name is Mira, and she’s got a scar across her eyebrow and a tattoo of a bird on her wrist that doesn’t look like it belongs to any bird I’ve ever seen. 

“I’m not lost,” I say, defensive. “I’m just… passing through.” 

She raises an eyebrow. “You sure about that?” 

I don’t know what to say. I don’t want to tell her I’m a runaway. Not yet. Not when I’m still figuring out what I’m doing. 

She smirks again. “You look like you’re running from something.” 

I almost tell her the truth. That I’m running from my family, from my old life, from the way people used to look at me and assume I was “just confused.” But I stop myself. What if she’s like them? What if she thinks I’m faking it? 

“I’m just… tired,” I say. 

Mira nods. “Yeah. I get that.” She steps aside. “Come on. You can crash at my place. I’ve got a couch. It’s not much, but it’s safe. And I’ve got a charger. And I’ve got a bunch of questions.” 

I hesitate. It’s not like I have a choice. 

“Okay,” I say. “But I’m not staying long.” 

She laughs. “You’ll stay longer than that. Trust me.” 

So I follow her. 

Her apartment is in a building that looks like it’s seen better days. The walls are peeling, the floors creak, and there’s a smell of something like incense and old pizza. But it’s clean. And there’s a couch. And a laptop. And a charging cable. 

I plug in my phone. The battery starts to climb. 

“Your phone’s dead,” she says, watching me. “You didn’t even have a charger.” 

“I didn’t think I’d need one.” 

She rolls her eyes. “You’re not the first person to think that.” 

I sit down. The couch is uncomfortable, but it’s better than standing. 

“So,” she says, leaning back against the couch. “What’s your story?” 

I look at her. “You don’t even know me.” 

“And I don’t need to. I’ve heard this one before.” 

I feel like I should be insulted, but I’m not. I’m too tired for that. 

“I’m Taylor,” I say. “I’m… I’m trans.” 

She doesn’t react. Just stares at me. 

“Okay,” she says. “That’s not surprising.” 

I blink. “What?” 

She sighs. “You’re not the first person to run away because of that. I’ve met a few.” 

I feel a mix of relief and shame. “You’ve met people like me?” 

“Yeah. And some of them are pretty messed up.” She pauses. “But some of them… they’re just like you. Running because they had no choice

**Chapter 3: Gone South**

The strap on my backpack keeps slipping off my shoulder. I keep adjusting it, like it’s going to fix something. Like maybe if I hold it tight enough, the weight of everything I’ve left behind will just... disappear. But it doesn’t. It just drags along behind me, a constant reminder that I’m not just running from my town—I’m running from myself.

I got here in a stolen van, a guy named Marcus who’d been doing a bit of shady stuff with his cousin’s meth stash. He said he’d take me to Baton Rouge, but when we hit the Mississippi River, he changed his mind. “You’re too much trouble,” he said, tossing me out of the van in front of a 24-hour gas station. I landed hard on the asphalt, my backpack bouncing off my head, and for a second, I thought I’d finally hit bottom. But then I remembered that I was already at the bottom. I just didn’t know it yet.

So now I’m standing in New Orleans, wearing a stolen hoodie that smells like regret and motor oil, trying to figure out what the hell to do next. My phone is dead. My wallet’s missing. My family’s probably calling the cops. And I’ve got a backpack that won’t stay on my shoulder.

I’ve been running for two days, but I feel like I’ve been running for years. The last time I told someone I was trans, my mom said, “You’re not *that* kind of kid.” And my dad just stared at me like I’d grown a third eye. It wasn’t even about the truth—it was about the *label*. Like being trans was some kind of joke, or a phase, or worse, a choice. And I’d had enough. I didn’t want to be the joke anymore. I didn’t want to be the phase. I didn’t want to be anyone else’s problem.

So I ran. And now I’m here, in a city I’ve only ever seen in movies, surrounded by jazz and grit and secrets.

I start walking, hoping that if I keep moving, the city will swallow me whole. I pass a street musician playing a saxophone that sounds like it’s been through a war. He nods at me, like he knows what I’m going through. I don’t respond. I don’t want to talk to anyone.

Then I see it—a flicker of movement behind a chain-link fence. A girl, maybe my age, sitting on a blanket, eating a sandwich. She looks up, sees me, and raises an eyebrow. I freeze. She doesn’t look like she’s in trouble. She looks like she’s waiting for something—or someone.

“Lost?” she asks.

I hesitate. I’ve been so careful not to trust anyone. But this girl? She looks like she’s been through something too. Maybe she’s like me.

“Sort of,” I say, and it comes out more like a question than an answer.

She smirks. “I’ve been lost longer than you’ve been alive.”

Her name is Jordan. She’s got a scar running down her wrist, and a tattoo of a phoenix on her ankle. She’s wearing a hoodie that’s too big for her, and her eyes are sharp, like she’s seen too much.

“I used to live in Memphis,” she says. “Same town as you, basically. Except I ran a *long* time ago.”

I sit down beside her, careful not to let my backpack slip again.

“So what’s your story?” she asks.

I open my mouth, but the words catch in my throat. I don’t know where to start. I don’t even know if I want to. But Jordan just waits, like she’s used to people being stuck in their heads.

“I came out to my family two days ago,” I say finally. “And it didn’t go well.”

Jordan nods, like she’s heard this before. “Yeah. That happens.”

“I left. I didn’t even think about it. Just... I needed to get out.”

She looks at me, and for a second, I think she’s going to judge me. But instead, she pulls out a bag of chips and offers me one.

“You’re not the first kid to run from home,” she says. “You’re not the first to run from a family that doesn’t get it. And you won’t be the last.”

I take the chip. It’s stale, but it’s something.

“So what do you do now?” I ask.

She shrugs. “I sleep in parks. I work at a diner. I’ve got a friend who’s a hacker and he helps me get free Wi-Fi. I’m trying to figure out what I’m going to do with my life.”

I stare at her. “You’re not running?”

She laughs. “I’m not running. I’m surviving. And I’m trying to find something that feels like it’s *mine*.”

I think about that. I’ve spent so long trying to fit into the roles people expected of me—son, brother, friend. But I didn’t want to be any of those things anymore. I wanted to be *me*.

“Do you ever feel like you’re just... waiting to fall apart?” I ask her.

She

records.response_text:
**Chapter 3: Gone South**

I’m in New Orleans.  
Literally.  
I’m standing in a street corner in the French Quarter, looking up at a neon sign that says “Frenchmen Street” and wondering if I’ve just committed the biggest mistake of my life. My backpack is slung over one shoulder, and it keeps slipping. The strap’s frayed and the pack itself is full of stolen clothes, a half-eaten energy bar, and a tiny bottle of water that’s been sitting in the sun for hours. I keep adjusting it, like it’ll fix something.

Two days ago, I came out to my family. Not in a dramatic, “I’m-a-unicorn” kind of way. Just… *I’m trans*. I said it like it was a fact, not a question. But it wasn’t a fact. It was a truth that had been building for years, and they didn’t get it. They didn’t understand. They said things like, “We love you, Taylor,” and “We just want what’s best for you,” but what they really meant was, *We don’t want to lose you*. And then, when I didn’t disappear like they expected me to, they shut me out. Locked the door. Said I was “disappointed” in them. Whatever.

So I ran. I packed a bag, got a friend to drive me, and we hit the highway. I didn’t even think about where I was going—just that it had to be somewhere *away*. And now I’m here, in the middle of a city that’s supposed to be full of music, culture, and secrets. But I don’t feel any of that. I feel lost. And scared.

The backpack keeps slipping. I pull it up again, and the strap catches on the collar of my hoodie. It’s a borrowed hoodie, one of my sister’s old ones. I used to love it. Now it feels like a shroud.

I’m hungry. I’m tired. I’m still processing the fact that I left everything behind—my house, my family, my life. I don’t even know where I’m going next. I just keep walking, hoping that somewhere out there, someone might take me in.

And then I see them.

A kid, sitting on a bench, legs crossed, staring at a phone. They’re wearing a hoodie too, similar to mine—black, with a faded logo. They look about my age, maybe a little older. Their posture is tense, like they’re holding something back. I hesitate. I’ve been so careful not to trust anyone, not since the last time I opened up to someone and got hurt. But something about them feels… familiar. Like a mirror.

I walk closer. “Hey,” I say, unsure. “You okay?”

They don’t look up. “I’m fine.”

I sit down next to them. “You don’t look fine.”

They finally glance at me. Their eyes are dark, tired. “You don’t look like someone who’s fine either.”

I blink. “You’re not wrong.”

We sit in silence for a moment. The sounds of the city hum around us—music, chatter, the occasional honk of a car. It’s loud, alive. But I don’t feel alive. I feel like I’m floating, disconnected from everything.

“I’m… not from here,” I say, trying to start a conversation. “I just… ran. From home, basically.”

The kid sighs. “Yeah. I get that.”

They pull out a phone and slide it across the table. It’s cracked, the screen slightly swollen. “I’m Jax. Or, I used to be. But that’s probably not important.”

I take the phone. It’s unlocked. A photo of a kid, maybe 14, standing in front of a tree, smiling. There’s a caption: *“Before the world decided I was too much.”*

I look up. “That’s you?”

Jax nods. “Used to be. Now I’m… not sure what I am.”

I feel a flicker of something—understanding, maybe. “You ran too?”

They look away. “Not exactly. I didn’t run. I *fled*. My mom… she’s a recovering addict. She got clean, tried to be a good mom, but… things went wrong. I got caught in the middle. I didn’t want to be a burden. So I left. A month ago.”

I feel my chest tighten. “You left and never came back?”

Jax shakes their head. “No. I stayed in touch. Sent messages. She said she’d come get me. But she never did.”

I don’t know what to say. I feel like I should say something profound, something that would make everything better. But all I can think is: *I get it.*

The sun starts to set, casting long shadows across the street. Jax looks up at it. “You ever wonder if you’re just… too much for the world?”

I hesitate. “Yeah. Sometimes.”

We sit in silence again. I watch the way Jax’s fingers tap against their knee, like they’re counting something. Like they’re waiting for something to happen.

“I’m Taylor,” I say finally.

J

**Chapter Two: New Orleans**

The strap on my backpack keeps slipping off my shoulder. I guess I’m not built for carrying things. Or maybe I’m just built for running. I’ve been running for two days straight—first from my house, then from my mom’s voice, then from my little brother’s tears, and finally from the look in my dad’s eyes when he said, “We’ll talk about this when you’re older.” Yeah, sure. That’s the same line he used when I told him I wanted to be a girl. Or at least, that’s what I think he used. I’m not sure anymore. Everything’s blurred. Like I’m watching a movie someone else made, and I can’t tell if I’m in it or not.

I ended up in New Orleans. I didn’t plan it. I just kept going, south, south, south, until the map ran out and the road kept going. I thought maybe I’d find a place where no one knew me. Where I could breathe without the weight of my parents’ expectations or my brother’s judgment or my own fear pressing down like a lead blanket.

But here I am. In the middle of a city that smells like fried food and history and something else I can’t name. The sun is hot, and my backpack keeps falling off. I’m tired, hungry, and more lost than I’ve ever been. I don’t even know if I should trust the street signs.

I’m sitting on the steps of a church, watching people walk by in suits and heels and baseball caps. Some of them look like they belong here. Others look like they’re trying to belong. I feel like I’m one of the latter. A tourist who’s accidentally wandered into a movie set. Except I don’t have a script. I don’t know the lines. I don’t even know my own character anymore.

That’s when I see her.

She’s sitting on a bench across the street, legs crossed, staring at her phone. Her hair is short and messy, like she just woke up and decided to stay that way. She’s wearing a hoodie that’s too big for her, and there’s a tattoo peeking out from the sleeve—something abstract, like a swirl or a flame. She looks like she’s been through something. Or maybe she’s still in the middle of it.

I watch her for a second. She’s not running. She’s not screaming. She’s just… waiting. Like she’s waiting for something to happen. Or someone.

I decide to take a risk.

“Hey,” I say, my voice cracking. “You lost too?”

She looks up, eyes sharp, like she’s expecting a threat. “No,” she says. “I’m not lost. I’m just… not ready.”

I blink. That’s not an answer, but it’s better than nothing. “I’m Taylor,” I say. “I’m kind of lost.”

She smirks. “I’m Jade. And I’m not lost either. I’m just… somewhere in between.”

I like that. Between. It sounds like a place I’ve been.

“Where are you headed?” I ask.

She shrugs. “Nowhere. Anywhere. Maybe both.”

I laugh, a little too loudly. “That’s not helpful.”

She rolls her eyes. “It’s not meant to be. It’s just… a starting point.”

I like her already. She’s got that same kind of messed-up energy I’ve been carrying around like a backpack I can’t carry. Maybe she’s a runaway too. Or maybe she’s just a kid who’s figured out how to survive without being found.

“Where do you sleep?” I ask.

She looks around, like she’s checking for cameras or people who might report her. “Under a bridge. Sometimes. Depends on the weather.”

I swallow hard. “That’s… not safe.”

She smirks again. “It’s not safe. But it’s mine.”

I nod. “Yeah. I get that.”

We sit in silence for a while, watching the people pass by. I notice she’s got a scar on her wrist, thin and pale, like it was a recent thing. I don’t ask. She doesn’t offer.

Eventually, she says, “You’re not from around here.”

“I’m not,” I admit. “I’m from Tennessee. Small town. Backwater. You know the drill.”

She nods. “I know the drill.”

I look at her. “You’re a runaway too?”

She hesitates. “I was. A while back.”

“Why did you come back?”

She doesn’t answer right away. When she does, her voice is quiet. “Because I didn’t know where else to go.”

I feel something in my chest tighten. Like I’m not alone in this. Like maybe we’re both just… trying to figure it out.

“Where do you sleep now?” I ask again.

She looks at me like I’ve asked the wrong question. “I don’t know.”

**Chapter Two: Gone South**

I’ve been running for 48 hours, and my backpack is already starting to feel like a second skin — or maybe a prison. The strap keeps slipping off my shoulder, and every time I catch it, I have to stop and adjust it, like I’m trying to fix a broken part of myself. I don’t even know what’s in the bag anymore. My clothes, some snacks, a few books, a photo of my mom with a note that says, “I love you” in crayon. That’s it. Everything else — the stuff that made me feel like I belonged — I left behind in a town where no one wanted me.

New Orleans is loud. Smells like fried food, sweat, and something sweet and dangerous. I’m standing in a park near the river, watching people walk by in suits and dresses, like they’re in a movie. I don’t fit in. Not here, not anywhere. I’m just… me — a kid who got tired of pretending to be someone else.

I was supposed to meet someone at a diner downtown. A “safe house,” they called it. A place where runaways could hide. But I got ejected from the van, like I was some kind of cursed luggage. I had no idea where I was, and the city was bigger than I thought. I figured I’d just walk until I found someone who looked like they might have the same problems I do.

That’s when I saw her.

She was sitting on a bench, eating a sandwich with one hand and scrolling through her phone with the other. Her hair was dyed purple, and there was a silver chain around her neck with a tiny key on it. She looked like she didn’t belong either. 

I hesitated, then sat down next to her.

“You lost?” she asked without looking up.

“I’m not lost,” I said, but I didn’t know what else to say. “I’m… just trying to find somewhere to sleep.”

She finally looked at me, and I caught a glimpse of something behind her eyes — something tired, like she’d been through hell and back. She didn’t look like someone who belonged here either.

“Name’s Jules,” she said. “You’re not from around here, are you?”

“Taylor,” I said. “I’m not from anywhere.”

She smirked. “That’s a good one.”

I didn’t know what to say. I wanted to tell her everything — about coming out, about the screaming, the tears, the way my family looked at me like I was some kind of monster. But I didn’t know if she’d believe me. Or worse, if she’d think I was crazy.

“I got kicked out of my house,” I finally said. “I left in a hurry. I didn’t pack much.”

She nodded, like she understood. “I get it. I ran too. A long time ago.”

I stared at her. “You ran?”

She shrugged. “I had to. My mom was… abusive. Not the kind you think. She didn’t hit me. She just… controlled me. Like I was a thing she could turn on and off.”

I felt a pang of sympathy, but also fear. Was she like me? Or was she just another lost kid with bad choices?

“Where do you sleep?” I asked.

She looked at me like she was deciding whether to trust me. “There’s a shelter downtown. It’s not great, but it’s safe. If you’re serious about staying, I can show you.”

I nodded. “Yeah. I’m serious.”

We walked in silence for a while, me watching the way she moved — careful, like she was always waiting for something to go wrong. She kept checking her phone, like she was expecting a call that would change everything.

“I used to live in Texas,” she said suddenly. “In a town called Amarillo. My mom was a nurse. She’d say I was ‘too sensitive’ for this world. Like I was a burden.”

I felt my chest tighten. “I’m trans,” I said, almost too quickly. “I came out two days ago. It didn’t go well.”

She stopped walking. For a moment, I thought she was going to run. But instead, she looked at me, and something in her eyes softened.

“You’re not the first kid I’ve met who’s been through that,” she said. “And you won’t be the last.”

We kept walking, but the air between us felt different now. Like we were both carrying something heavy, and maybe, just maybe, we weren’t alone.

The shelter was a crumbling building on the edge of the French Quarter. It smelled like old food and desperation. Jules led me inside, and I felt my stomach churn. This wasn’t a place I wanted to be. But I didn’t have a choice.

There were kids and adults sprawled out on cots, some sleeping, some staring at the ceiling like they were waiting for something to happen. A woman in a stained shirt handed me a cup of warm soup. “You look like you need it,” she said.

I nodded, too tired to argue.

Jules found a spot near the back, and I lay down next to her. She didn’t say much, just kept fiddling

records.response_text:
**Chapter Three: Gone South**

I’ve been running for two days, and my backpack still won’t stay on. The strap keeps slipping off my shoulder, like it’s mocking me. Or maybe it’s just tired. I don’t know. All I know is that I’m in New Orleans, and I have no idea what to do with myself.

I ended up in a place called Frenchmen Street. It’s supposed to be cool, but all I saw were people in weird hats and people who looked like they were trying too hard to be artsy. I was half-expecting to find a group of people like me—runaways, outcasts, people who’ve been kicked out of their homes for being too much. But no, it’s just tourists and hipsters. Or maybe they’re the same thing.

I’m not used to being alone. Not like this. I mean, I’ve been alone before—just not this alone. I’ve got a backpack full of clothes, a phone with one battery left, and a heart that’s been beat up for years. I thought running would fix it. I thought escaping would make everything better. But all I’ve done is land myself in a city I don’t know, with no money, no plan, and no one to call.

I’m sitting on a bench outside a bodega, eating a sandwich I bought with the last of my cash. The sandwich is cold, and it tastes like regret. I keep looking around, half-expecting someone I know to show up. Mom. Dad. My sister. But they’re all back in Tennessee, pretending they never heard me say “I’m trans.” Or maybe they just didn’t believe me. Either way, I’m not going back.

That’s when I see them.

A kid, maybe a few years older than me, sitting on the ground a few feet away. They’re holding a guitar, tuning it with a pick. Their hair is a mess, like they just woke up and decided to go full punk. Their eyes are tired, like they’ve been running for a while too. And they’re wearing the same kind of clothes I’m wearing—old, worn-out jeans, a hoodie that’s seen better days, and a backpack that’s also constantly slipping off their shoulder.

I almost laugh. It’s like we’re two mirrors in a hall of mirrors, both of us trying to escape something we can’t explain.

“Hey,” I say, not sure if I’m trying to be friendly or just testing the waters. “You lost too?”

The kid looks up, surprised. “You could say that.”

I sit down next to them, careful not to touch their guitar. “So, uh… what’s your name?”

“Jace.” They shrug. “And you?”

“Taylor.” I pause. “I’m… not sure where I’m going. Just running.”

Jace nods, like they’ve heard that one before. “Me too.” They strum a chord, then stop. “Just not sure if I’m running from something or to something.”

I study them. There’s something about Jace that feels… familiar. Like we’re both trying to survive, but not sure how.

“Why’d you run?” I ask, not because I’m nosy, but because I’m trying to understand something. To connect.

Jace looks away. “My mom… she didn’t want me. Not really. She said I was a mistake. But I wasn’t. I just… didn’t fit. Not in her world. Not in hers.”

I feel a pang. I know that feeling. “You’re trans too?”

Jace hesitates. “Yeah. And I’m… not just that. I’m also a drug addict. And I’ve been in and out of rehab like it’s a vacation. And I’m not sure if I want to be clean anymore.”

I blink. “You’re… serious?”

“Yeah.” They laugh, but it doesn’t sound like a laugh. “I tried. I really did. But every time I get close to being okay, something happens. My mom gets sick. My dad leaves. I end up somewhere I don’t want to be.”

I feel my chest tighten. “So you just… run?”

“I try not to. But sometimes you don’t have a choice.”

I look at my own hands. They’re shaking slightly. “I ran too. Two days ago. I came out to my family, and it didn’t go well. They didn’t understand. They didn’t listen. So I left. I thought I’d be okay. I thought I’d find somewhere safe.”

Jace looks at me, and for the first time, I see something in their eyes—understanding. “You’re not alone, Taylor.”

I almost cry. “I feel like I am.”

I’ve been running for 48 hours, and my backpack is already starting to feel like a second skin—or maybe a millstone. The strap keeps slipping off my shoulder, and every time I catch it, I flinch. It's not just the weight; it's the memory of the last time I carried it. That was before I came out. Before I was *anything* but a problem. The school counselor called me “concerning,” like I was a case study, not a person. I don’t even know what that means anymore.

I ended up in New Orleans, of all places. I thought I’d end up in Memphis, maybe Nashville—somewhere I could hide. But the car broke down in the middle of a street in the French Quarter, and I got kicked out. Now I’m sitting on the steps of a crumbling church, eating a granola bar that’s gone stale and hard as a rock. My phone is dead. My money is gone. And I’m freezing, even though it’s mid-September.

I’ve never been to New Orleans before. I thought I knew what it was: jazz, beignets, Mardi Gras. But I didn’t know it was also full of people who look like me—runaways, drifters, kids with secrets they don’t know how to tell. I don’t know if that’s comforting or terrifying.

A voice cuts through the noise of the street. “You lost?”

I look up. A girl about my age, maybe 17, is standing in front of me. She’s wearing a hoodie that’s too big, and her jeans are patched with things that look like fabric from old shirts. She has a scar running from her eyebrow to her cheekbone, and she’s holding a guitar case like it’s a lifeline.

“Not lost,” I say, trying to sound confident. “Just… exploring.”

She raises an eyebrow. “Exploring? You look like you’ve been exploring for a while.”

I shrug. “I’m a bit of a nomad.”

She smirks. “I like that.” She plops down beside me on the steps. “Name’s Jules. You’re the first person I’ve seen in this neighborhood who doesn’t look like they’re trying to get to a shelter before midnight.”

I hesitate. “I’m Taylor.”

She nods. “You’re not from around here, are you?”

“No. Tennessee. Small town. You know how it is.”

She nods again, like she gets it. “Yeah. I know how it is.”

There’s something in her voice—something quiet, like she’s holding back a story. I want to ask her what’s going on, but I don’t want to come off as desperate. I’ve been desperate for too long.

Instead, I say, “So, what’s your deal? You look like you’ve got more going on than just a guitar case.”

She laughs, low and tired. “You have no idea.”

We sit there for a while, sharing the last of my granola bar. She talks about her mom, who left when she was 12. She talks about running away when she was 15, ending up in a homeless shelter in Dallas, then hitchhiking to New Orleans. She doesn’t talk about why she left. She just says, “I needed to get out of that house. It wasn’t safe.”

I feel a pang of something—sympathy, maybe, or just the weight of shared pain. I don’t know how to respond, so I just nod.

“Do you have a place to stay?” she asks.

I shake my head. “Not really.”

She eyes my backpack. “That thing’s got a mind of its own.”

I roll my eyes. “It’s just heavy.”

She looks at me like she’s seeing something I can’t explain. “You’re trans, aren’t you?”

I freeze. The question isn’t accusatory. It’s just… there. Like she knows. Like she’s seen it in my eyes, in the way I hold myself, the way I move through the world like I’m always half-expected to disappear.

I don’t answer right away. I just stare at the ground.

She leans back on her elbows. “I am too.”

I blink. “You are?”

She nods. “I came out to my mom when I was 16. She didn’t take it well. She said I was ‘confused’ and ‘disappointing.’ So I left. Not long after, I met this guy who said he’d take me in. Turns out he was into something… darker. I ran. And now I’m here.”

I feel my chest tighten. “That’s… not good.”

She laughs again, but it doesn’t sound happy. “No. It’s not. But I’m here. And I’m alive. And I’m not alone.”

**Chapter Two: The Strap on My Backpack Keeps Slipping**

I’ve been running for 48 hours, and I still don’t know if I’m free or just running from something bigger. The strap on my backpack keeps slipping off my shoulder, and every time it does, I have to stop and fish it out of the dirt or the puddle or the goddamn sidewalk. It’s like the universe is mocking me, trying to tell me I’m not ready for this.

I ended up in New Orleans last night, after a night of bad decisions, worse driving, and a really unfortunate encounter with a cop who thought I was a tourist with a stolen car. I was lucky — he didn’t arrest me, just gave me a warning and told me to “get off the street.” I took that as a hint and kept going. Now I’m sitting on the steps of a crumbling church in the French Quarter, watching the sun rise over the Mississippi, and trying not to cry.

My phone is dead. My money is gone. My family thinks I’m dead. And I have no idea what I’m going to do next.

The first thing I noticed when I got here was the noise — the music, the chatter, the smell of fried food and bourbon. It was louder than anything I’d ever heard in Tennessee. It felt like the world was alive here, like it was breathing in a way that my small town never did. But I couldn’t shake the feeling that I was an intruder, a ghost in a place that didn’t want me.

I was about to give up and just lie down in the grass when I heard it — a voice. Not the kind of voice that belongs to someone who’s lost, but the kind that’s got a rhythm to it, like they’re used to being alone.

“Lost, are we?”

I turned my head slowly. A guy was leaning against a lamppost, arms crossed, wearing a leather jacket that looked like it had been through a war. He had a tattoo on his neck — a snake coiled around a heart. I didn’t like that.

“Not lost,” I said, trying to sound confident. “Just… taking a break.”

He raised an eyebrow. “Taking a break from what?”

“From everything.”

He smirked, like he knew exactly what I meant. “Well, I’ve got a name. It’s Jax. And I’ve got a question for you — are you running or just wandering?”

I didn’t answer right away. I didn’t know what to say. Running was the only thing I knew how to do anymore.

“I’m not sure,” I finally said. “I guess I’m both.”

He nodded like that made sense. “Me too. For a while.”

He sat down beside me, and I couldn’t help but notice how he didn’t look like someone who belonged in a place like this. He looked like someone who’d been through a lot, and maybe still was.

“I used to live in Memphis,” he said. “My parents were religious, like, *super* religious. They thought I was a bad influence. I didn’t fit in. So I left when I was 15. I’ve been on the move ever since.”

I looked at him, really looked at him. He was older — maybe 19 or 20. But there was something about him that felt… familiar. Like we were both trying to survive in a world that didn’t want us.

“Why’d you come to New Orleans?” I asked.

“Same reason you did,” he said. “I needed a fresh start. And I needed someone to talk to.”

I frowned. “You don’t know me.”

“Yeah, but I know what it’s like to be alone. I know what it’s like to feel like you don’t belong. I know what it’s like to be *seen* in a way that’s not… safe.”

I felt something in my chest tighten. He wasn’t just talking about being a runaway. He was talking about something deeper.

“I’m trans,” I said, almost as if I was testing if he’d react.

He looked at me, really looked at me, and for a second, I thought he was going to run. But then he just smiled — a small, tired smile.

“I figured,” he said. “You don’t come out to your family and just walk away without a fight, do you?”

I shook my head. “No. I didn’t. They didn’t even believe me.”

He nodded. “Yeah. That’s the worst part.”

We sat there in silence for a while, the city humming around us. I didn’t know what to do with this — someone who understood. Someone who didn’t judge.

“Where do you sleep?” I asked.

He shrugged. “Where I can. Sometimes I crash on friends’ couches. Sometimes I just find a park or a bench. I’ve learned to be comfortable with the ground.”

I glanced at my backpack again. “I don’t even know where I’m going. I just… left.”

The strap on my backpack keeps slipping off my shoulder. I’ve been trying to fix it for the last ten minutes, but it’s like the damn thing is alive and determined to escape me. I guess it’s fitting, really. Everything about this trip has felt like a runaway train I can’t stop.

I landed in New Orleans in the early hours of the morning, exhausted, hungry, and legally homeless. The ride to the city had been a blur—my friend Jesse, who I’d met online through some weird trans teen forum, picked me up in his beat-up van with a broken AC and a playlist that sounded like it was recorded in a landfill. He said he was “just passing through,” but I knew better. I knew he was running too.

We hit the interstate at midnight, and by the time we got to New Orleans, the sun was just starting to peek over the horizon. But now, standing on the sidewalk of a busy street in the French Quarter, I feel like I’ve been dropped into a dream that doesn’t belong to me. The air smells like fried food and beer, and the sounds of jazz and laughter mix with the distant echo of sirens. I’m not sure if I’m in a movie or a nightmare.

I try to look like I belong. I walk with purpose, like I know where I’m going, but honestly, I have no idea. I just want to find somewhere to sleep, somewhere safe. But I know better than to trust strangers.

“Hey, you lost?”

I freeze. The voice is soft, but there’s something in it—something sharp, like a blade wrapped in velvet. They’re wearing a leather jacket that’s too big for them, and their eyes are tired, like they’ve seen too much.

“I’m not lost,” I manage to say. “I’m just… figuring things out.”

The kid smirks. “Yeah, I get that. I’m not lost either. I’m just… stuck.”

They take a drag from the cigarette and exhale slowly. “Name’s Lex. You’re the first runaway I’ve seen in a week. That’s a record.”

I blink. “You’ve been running too?”

Lex nods. “Longer than you, I think. I’ve been on the move since I was thirteen.”

I want to ask them a million questions—where are you going? Why are you running? Are you safe? But I don’t. I’m not sure I can trust anyone, not even someone who looks like they’ve been through the same thing.

“Where do you sleep?” I ask instead.

Lex shrugs. “Depends. Sometimes I crash at a shelter. Sometimes I find a couch. Sometimes I just sleep in the park.”

“I used to come here,” Lex says. “Before everything went to hell.”

I glance at the diner. The sign is faded, the windows are cracked, and the door creaks like it might fall off if you looked at it the wrong way.

“Are you sure this is a good idea?” I ask.

Lex smirks again. “It’s the only place in this neighborhood that still has a working door.”

“Welcome to the End of the Line,” the man says, not looking up from his newspaper.

Lex nods. “Yeah. A little.”

**Chapter Three: Gone South**

So I'm in New Orleans. Two states away from home, a stolen car, a broken heart, and a whole lot of bad decisions. I thought I'd be more prepared. I thought I'd have a plan. But honestly, I just wanted to run. Run from my family, from the silence, from the looks, from the *what do we do now?* that followed me like a ghost every time I opened my mouth.

I was soaked. Not just from the drizzle—though that didn’t help—but from the weight of everything. I’d been walking for hours, my feet numb, my head spinning. I didn’t even know where I was going, just that I couldn’t go back. I thought maybe the city would be different. More free. More... *alive*. But New Orleans isn’t just a city—it’s a living thing, and it’s got its own kind of chaos. And I’m not sure I belong here either.

I stumbled under a café awning, the drizzle turning the pavement into a mirror. The smell of coffee and fried dough hit me like a wave. I looked around, trying to figure out if this place was safe. The café was empty except for a couple of old people sipping coffee and a barista who looked like they'd rather be anywhere else. I didn’t have money. I didn’t have a plan. I just had this stupid hope that maybe, somewhere in this city, someone would understand.

That’s when I saw him.

He was sitting on a stool, hunched over a laptop, looking like he didn’t belong there either. His hair was messed up, his jeans were too big, and there was a tattoo peeking out from under a sleeve—something jagged and dark. He looked like he’d been through hell and back, and maybe he had. I didn’t know what to do. Say hi? Ask for help? Or just disappear again?

I chose the latter. I turned to leave, but he called out before I could take more than two steps.

“Hey, you gonna stand there all day or what?”

I froze. I didn’t expect someone to notice me. Not like this. I glanced back. He had this intense look, like he was tired of everything but still somehow alert. Like he was waiting for something—or someone.

“Yeah, I’m gonna go,” I said, trying to keep my voice steady. It didn’t work. It cracked halfway through.

He raised an eyebrow. “You’re not from around here, are you?”

“No,” I said, and then I realized how stupid that sounded. “I mean, I’m from Tennessee. But I’m not from *here*.”

He smirked. “Well, welcome to the Big Easy. You’re gonna need more than that to survive.”

I looked at him like he was nuts. “I don’t even know where I am.”

He leaned back, crossing his arms. “You’re in the French Quarter. You’re either a tourist or a runaway. Which is it?”

I hesitated. Part of me wanted to run. But something in his tone—something familiar—made me stay. “Both,” I admitted.

He nodded like he expected that. “Name’s Jax. And you?”

“Taylor.”

“Taylor,” he repeated, like he was testing the name. “You look like you’ve been running for a while.”

“I have.”

He looked me up and down. “You look like you’ve been running from something.”

I didn’t answer. I didn’t know how. The words caught in my throat. I’d been trying to avoid this conversation for so long—trying to convince myself that this was just a phase, that I’d come out to them, and they’d accept me. But they didn’t. And now I’m here, alone, with no one to talk to.

Jax must’ve seen something in my face. He sighed, rubbing his temples. “Look, I get it. I’ve been there. Before I was a runaway, I was… a mess. Not just in the way you think. I had my own stuff. My own demons.”

I looked at him, surprised. “You were a runaway too?”

“Used to be. A few years back. I thought I could outrun it. I thought if I just left, everything would make sense. But it didn’t. It just got worse.”

I felt a weird pull toward him. Like maybe we were both just trying to find our way in a world that didn’t want us.

“Where are you staying?” he asked.

I shrugged. “Nowhere. I just… I needed to get out.”

He looked at me for a second, then stood up. “Come on. I’ve got a couch. It’s not much, but it’s dry. And I’ve got some food. You look like you haven’t eaten in days.”

I hesitated. “You don’t even know me.”

“I know enough,” he said. “You’re lost. And you’re not the only one.”
