records.response_text:
**Chapter 2: Gone South**

I’ve been running for two days, and I still don’t know if I’m running from something or to something. My feet hurt. My clothes smell like sweat and regret. And my head? It’s full of noise—voices from my family, the school counselor’s smug voice on the phone, my own voice echoing back at me, *“You’re not a problem, Taylor. You’re just… different.”* Yeah, that’s right. Different. Like I’m some kind of alien who stumbled into a small town in Tennessee and messed up the whole ecosystem.

I got out of there in thirty minutes. A backpack, a gas station napkin with a stolen phone number, and a plan that was basically, “Drive until the map runs out.” I didn’t know where I was going, just that I wasn’t going back. And now I’m in New Orleans, standing under a café awning, a light drizzle making the streets look like a watercolor painting of despair. My shoes are soaked, my jeans are soaked, and I’m pretty sure my soul is too.

I’m not sure how long I’ve been standing there, just staring at the neon sign that says *“Pineapple & Prosecco”*—some kind of café that probably serves more attitude than coffee. I don’t know why I chose it. Maybe because it looked like it might have a bathroom. Or maybe because I thought someone might be in there who looked like they might be a friend. Or maybe I just wanted to stop moving for a second.

That’s the thing about running: you don’t really get to choose where you end up. You just end up somewhere, and then you have to figure out how to stay.

A door creaks open behind me, and a voice says, “Hey, you okay?”

I jump. My heart thumps like it’s trying to escape my chest.

“Yeah,” I say, too quickly. “Just… waiting for the rain to stop.”

The person behind me—some kid, probably around my age—steps out and squints at me. They’re wearing a leather jacket, a beanie that’s too big, and a look that says they’ve been through hell and back. Their eyes are tired, like they’ve seen too much and not enough. I feel like I know them already.

“Rain’s not stopping anytime soon,” they say. “You wanna come in? It’s not much, but it’s dry.”

I hesitate. I don’t trust people. Not after what happened back home. But something about this kid—something in the way they hold themselves, the way they don’t look at me like I’m a problem—makes me want to say yes.

“Okay,” I say. “Thanks.”

They nod and lead me inside. The café is small, dimly lit, and smells like coffee, incense, and something else I can’t place. There are a few people inside—some sitting at tables, others at the bar. A few are playing video games on phones, others are reading books that look like they’ve been through a war. It feels like a sanctuary.

The kid—*I guess his name is Alex*—pulls out a stool at the counter. “Name’s Alex. You’re… not from around here, are you?”

I sit down, my back against the cold metal. “Nope. From Tennessee. Small town. Backwater. You know the type.”

He smirks. “Yeah, I know the type.” He pauses, then adds, “I used to be from a small town too. In Texas. And I ran too. Right before everything went to hell.”

I look at him. He’s got a scar on his eyebrow, and his fingers are calloused. He doesn’t look like someone who runs. He looks like someone who’s been through it all and is still standing.

“What happened?” I ask.

He shrugs. “Family stuff. Parents didn’t get it. Didn’t get me, didn’t get my… *stuff*.” He hesitates. “You’re trans, aren’t you?”

I blink. “How do you know?”

He laughs. “You don’t look like you’re hiding it. And you’re not in a dress or anything. You’re just… you. And you ran. That’s a sign. You don’t run from things you’re not scared of.”

I don’t know what to say. Part of me wants to tell him everything—about coming out, about the silence, about the rejection. But I’m still so raw. So exposed.

“I’m… yeah,” I say finally. “I’m trans. And I’m not sure if I’m running from it or to it.”

He nods. “I get it. Sometimes you run because you’re scared. Sometimes you run because you’re tired of being scared.”

I look down at my hands. They’re shaking a little. “I didn’t think I’d ever leave. Not really. I thought I’d just… fade away, you know? Be ignored. Be… not enough.”

Alex leans back. “You’re not not enough. You’re just… not in the right place yet.”

I look up at him. “You’re not either, are you?”

He smirks again

**Chapter Three: Gone South**

I’ve been in New Orleans for less than twelve hours, and already I feel like I’ve been here my whole life. The air smells like Bourbon Street and regret. My boots are soaked from the drizzle that started an hour ago, and I’m still trying to figure out if I’m more tired from running or from the emotional rollercoaster that’s been my life since I came out.

I ended up in this part of the city by accident. I had a vague idea of heading south, figuring that if I could get to the Gulf, maybe I could just float away on the water. But I didn’t plan on ending up in a neighborhood that smells like fried chicken and desperation. I’m hiding under a café awning, watching the rain trickle down my neck, and trying not to think about the fact that my family probably thinks I’m dead.

I didn’t even know what I was until I was 13, and by 16, I was so tired of pretending I wasn’t me. So I packed a duffel bag, stole a jacket from my dad’s closet (he never noticed), and ran.

I didn’t think I’d make it out of Tennessee. But I did. And now I’m here, in a city I’ve only ever seen in movies, and I’m completely lost.

She smirks. “You’re not in Tennessee anymore. Here, you can buy coffee for a dollar. If you’re lucky.”

She holds out a hand. “Name’s Jules. You’re new here, right?”

I hesitate. “Yeah. I guess.”

“Cool. So, you’re either a tourist or a runaway.” She pauses, like she’s waiting for me to pick a side. “I’m a runaway. So, I’m assuming you’re one too.”

I look down at my feet. “Yeah. I am.”

She nods like she’s expected this. “Good. Then we’re gonna get along.”

I don’t know why I’m surprised she didn’t run away the second I said that. Maybe because she already knew what it felt like to be invisible.

“Where you headed?” she asks.

I shrug. “Nowhere. Just… trying to figure it out.”

She laughs. “That’s the first step. Figuring out where you are.”

She pauses, and for a moment, I think she’s going to cry. But instead, she just takes a sip of her coffee and looks at me.

She shrugs. “I like the music. The vibe. It’s… chaotic. Like me.”

I smile a little. “Yeah. I guess it is.”

records.response_text:
**Chapter 2: Gone South**

So, here I am. Two states away from home, a stolen car, a broken heart, and a whole lot of regret. I never thought I’d end up in New Orleans. I mean, I’ve seen pictures of it—those big, colorful houses, the jazz music, the Mardi Gras madness—but none of that feels real right now. I’m just a kid in a city that’s way too big and way too loud. And I’m totally lost.

I got out of my car in the middle of the street, rain drizzling down like someone’s trying to drown me. My shoes are soaked, my jeans are muddy, and I’m holding a backpack that feels heavier than it should. I’d been planning this escape for weeks, but I didn’t expect it to feel so… empty. Like I’d left something behind that I can’t even name.

I remember the last conversation with my mom. She said, “You’re not ready for this.” And I thought, *What the hell is she talking about?* I was ready. I had been for months. I just needed to get out before it all went too far.

Now, I’m standing under a café awning, shivering, trying to figure out what the hell I’m going to do next. The city is buzzing around me—people in suits, tourists with cameras, street performers playing guitar—but I feel like an outsider. Like I don’t belong here. Like I never will.

A voice cuts through the rain. “Hey, you okay?”

I turn to see a kid, maybe my age, sitting on a bench outside the café. They’re wearing a hoodie that’s too big, jeans that are too short, and a backpack that’s just like mine. Their eyes are tired, like they’ve been running for a long time. There’s something in their posture—defiant, but also broken.

I nod. “I think so.”

They smirk. “You look like you’ve been running for a while.”

“Used to be,” I say, shrugging. “Not so much anymore.”

They slide over on the bench, making room. “Name’s Jordan. You’re new here?”

I hesitate. “Yeah. New to everything.”

Jordan eyes my backpack. “You’ve got a lot in there.”

I glance down. There’s a worn-out journal, a few clothes, a half-eaten granola bar, and a small, folded piece of paper with a phone number on it. My mom’s number. I didn’t even think about it until now. I should’ve left it behind.

“I guess I do,” I say quietly.

Jordan nods, like they understand. “I’ve been running too. For a while now.”

I look at them, curious. “Why?”

Jordan hesitates, then sighs. “I didn’t come out. Not really. I told my parents I was gay, but they didn’t believe me. They said I was just being dramatic. Like Taylor was.”

Wait—*Taylor*? That’s my name. I blink. “You know me?”

Jordan laughs, but it doesn’t sound happy. “You’re the one who got out of town. The one who left your family behind. I saw the news. ‘Teen runaway from rural Tennessee’—they made it sound like a movie.”

I feel my face heat up. “Yeah, well, I’m not a movie. I’m just… me.”

Jordan leans back, staring at the rain. “I get it. I’ve been trying to be someone else for so long. Pretending I was okay with being straight, okay with being quiet, okay with not being… *me*.”

I feel something in me shift. Like, maybe I’m not so alone here. Maybe there’s someone else who knows what it’s like to feel like you’re disappearing.

“So,” I say, trying to keep the conversation light, “what’s your story? How’d you end up here?”

Jordan shrugs. “I was in a bad place. My parents didn’t get it. My brother did. He got kicked out too. We used to run together. But he got caught. Left me to fend for myself.”

I don’t know what to say. I feel like I should offer comfort, but I don’t know if I can. I’ve been so used to being alone that I don’t know how to be around someone else who’s hurting.

“Where are you staying?” I ask.

Jordan looks up. “No idea. I was supposed to meet someone. A friend. But they didn’t show.”

I think about my own plan—how I was supposed to meet someone in the city, someone who could help me. But I didn’t even make it to the meeting. I just… ran.

“Me too,” I say. “I was supposed to meet someone. But I got lost.”

Jordan smirks. “So we’re both lost. That’s something.”

I laugh, but it’s weak. “Yeah. Something.”

We sit there for a while, sharing the only dry spot under the awning. The rain keeps falling, but it feels like it’s washing away something. Maybe the guilt. Maybe the fear.

“Do you think we’ll ever stop running?” Jordan asks.

I think about home.

I’ve never been good at staying put. Not really. Even before I came out, I was always on the move—running from one thing or another. But this? This is different. This is real. I didn’t run from a bad day or a stupid argument. I ran from a family that didn’t know how to love me, and a town that didn’t know how to keep me. Two days ago, I was still living in the same house, breathing the same air, pretending I didn’t know who I was. Now I’m in New Orleans, drenched in a light drizzle, standing under the awning of a café that smells like coffee and regret.

I’m tired. So tired. My feet hurt, my stomach is empty, and my heart feels like it’s trying to escape my chest. I should’ve packed a better plan. I should’ve thought about the basics—money, clothes, a plan. The drizzle is cold, but I don’t have an umbrella. I just stand there, watching the rain blur the neon signs and the people passing by, all so normal, so *safe*. I feel like an intruder in this city, like I don’t belong here. Maybe I don’t. Maybe I never did.

He sighs and steps closer. “You look like you need a place to crash. Or at least a place to eat.”

I look up. “I’m not looking for a place to crash.”

“Then why’re you standing out here in the rain?”

I open my mouth to say something smart, something sarcastic—something that would make him walk away. But instead, I just mutter, “I don’t know.”

He raises an eyebrow. “That’s not an answer.”

He smirks. “Charity? I’m just trying to be a decent human being.”

I snort. “You’re not trying to be a decent human being. You’re trying to be a hero.”

He laughs. “Okay, maybe I am. But I’m not the only one who’s been kicked out of their house.”

I freeze. That’s it. That’s the thing that makes me pause. That’s the thing that makes me want to believe him.

“Whoa,” he says, noticing my reaction. “You’re not from around here, are you?”

I shake my head. “No. I’m from Tennessee.”

He nods slowly. “So you’re… you know.”

I don’t answer. I don’t need to. He gets it.

He nods. “Taylor. Sounds like a name that needs a story.”

He looks at me like he understands. “Yeah. I’ve been there.”

I hesitate. It’s tempting. I’ve been alone for two days, and I don’t know how to do anything else. But I also don’t trust anyone. Not even someone who seems to understand me.

“I don’t know,” I say, voice small. “I don’t know if I can trust anyone.”

He sighs. “You can’t trust everyone, but you can trust someone. And if you don’t, you’re gonna end up like me—sleeping on park benches and eating cereal for dinner.”

records.response_text:
**Chapter Three: Gone South**

I’ve been running for two days, and I still don’t know if I’m running from something or to something. Maybe both. My feet hurt. My clothes are soaked from the drizzle that started an hour ago, and I’m pretty sure I’ve lost my phone somewhere in the chaos of leaving my house. But I’m alive. That’s something.

New Orleans. I didn’t expect to end up here. I thought I’d head straight to Memphis, or maybe Atlanta, somewhere where I could disappear into the city noise and not have to think about my family, my stupid little town, or the way my mom’s voice cracked when she said, “We love you, Taylor,” like that was supposed to fix anything.

I found this café on a whim—there was a sign that said “Open 24 Hours” and that seemed promising. The drizzle is relentless now, and I’m hunched under the awning, trying not to catch a cold. My stomach growls. I should’ve eaten something before I left. But I was too busy packing, too busy thinking about how much I hated everything.

A voice cuts through the rain. “You look like you could use a hot meal.”

I turn to see a guy leaning against the doorframe, arms crossed, wearing a leather jacket that probably cost more than my entire wardrobe. He’s maybe my age, early twenties, with a beard that looks like it’s been neglected for weeks. His eyes are sharp, like he’s seen too much. Or maybe he’s just been through too much.

“Thanks,” I say, hesitating. “But I’m not sure I’m allowed to just… show up and eat.”

He smirks. “You’re not allowed to show up and not eat. That’s a rule I made up. You’re welcome.”

I eye him warily. “What’s your name?”

“Jax.” He holds out a hand. “And you?”

“Taylor.” I shake it, not really sure what else to do. “I’m… not sure where I’m going.”

Jax nods like he gets it. “Me neither, for a while. But I’m staying at the Shelter on Frenchmen Street. You should come. They have food, showers, and a couch that doesn’t smell like old socks.”

I consider this. The shelter thing sounds… safe? Or maybe just temporary. I’ve been alone for two days, and I’m starting to think I need someone else’s brain to survive this.

“Okay,” I say. “But I’m not staying forever.”

Jax grins. “You won’t be staying forever. But you’ll be staying *for now*.”

We walk through the rain, and I notice he’s carrying a duffel bag, a backpack, and a guitar case. There’s something about him that feels… familiar. Like I’ve seen that look before—someone who’s been through a lot, who’s trying to piece themselves back together.

Inside the shelter, it’s warm and loud. People are eating, talking, laughing. It’s not the kind of chaos I expected. It feels… real.

“First time here?” Jax asks as we walk past a group of kids playing cards.

“Second,” I say. “First time running away.”

He pauses. “You’re not the first, either.”

I look at him. “You’ve done this before?”

He shrugs. “Not exactly. I mean, I’ve been on the move. Had to leave my town. My family didn’t… get it.” He pauses, and for a second, I think I see something in his eyes—pain, maybe guilt. “I didn’t come out. Not really. I tried. They didn’t care. So I left.”

I feel a pang of something I can’t name. Not pity. Not sympathy. Just… connection.

“Did it help?” I ask quietly.

He looks away. “Sometimes. Sometimes it just makes you feel more alone.”

I don’t know what to say to that. I’m still processing my own feelings—anger, fear, relief. I came out to my family and they shut me down. I didn’t expect them to, but I knew they wouldn’t. And now I’m here, in a city I’ve never been to, with a guy who’s basically a stranger but who somehow understands me more than anyone ever has.

“Do you have a place to stay?” I ask, trying to sound casual.

He nods. “For now. I’m trying to find a job. Maybe get back on my feet.”

“Me too,” I say. “I just… don’t know where to start.”

He smiles. “That’s the first step. Starting somewhere.”

Later that night, we sit on a couch in the shelter, sharing a plate of leftover gumbo. The room is dimly lit, the hum of the city outside a constant backdrop.

“Why did you come out?” Jax asks, breaking the silence.

I hesitate. “Because I couldn’t keep pretending anymore. I was tired of being someone I wasn’t. I used to be a girl, but I’m not anymore. I’m Taylor. I’m trans. And I’m done hiding.”

He listens without interrupting. When I finish, he nods. “

records.response_text:
**Chapter 3: Gone South (Part 2)**

I’ve never been good at staying put. Not really. I mean, I’ve lived in the same town my whole life—Tennessee, of all places—and I’ve never *needed* to run. But sometimes you just can’t outrun the noise in your head.

So here I am, in New Orleans. Two states away from home, and I’m still not sure if this is a good idea or just another chapter in a book I didn’t ask to write.

The drizzle started out of nowhere, like the sky had decided to cry without warning. I was walking down a street I didn’t recognize, trying to figure out where the nearest bus stop was, when the rain forced me under a café awning. The smell of coffee and fried dough hit me immediately, and I almost laughed—like I was in one of those movies where the protagonist finds salvation in the most unexpected places. But I’m not a protagonist. I’m a runaway with a history of bad decisions, a family that didn’t know how to handle my truth, and a body that’s still figuring out how to exist in a world that doesn’t always get it.

I pulled my hoodie tighter around my neck, trying to block out the cold and the noise of the city. The awning wasn’t big enough to shelter me from the rain, and I could feel the water soaking through my clothes. I was already late for whatever plan I had—there wasn’t one, really. Just a vague idea that I needed to get out of Tennessee, and New Orleans was the first place that came to mind.

That’s the thing about running: you don’t plan. You just… *run*. And then you hope someone else is already running too.

A voice cut through the drizzle. “You look like you need a place to dry off.”

I turned to see a kid leaning against the café door, wearing a leather jacket that looked like it had seen better days. Their hair was dark, messy, and tied back with a piece of duct tape. They had a scar running from their eyebrow down to their cheek, and their eyes were the color of storm clouds.

“Yeah,” I muttered, “but I don’t think I’m allowed to just… *take* shelter.”

The kid smirked. “You’re not. You’re *borrowing* shelter.”

I raised an eyebrow. “You’re one to talk.”

They shrugged. “Used to be. Now I’m just a guy who knows how to survive in a city that doesn’t care about you.”

I hesitated. Part of me wanted to run. Another part, the part that had spent the last 48 hours wandering through a minefield of emotions, wanted to sit down and ask questions.

“Name’s Jax,” the kid said, stepping aside to let me in. “You’re… not from around here.”

I followed them inside, the smell of coffee and something else—something smoky and sweet—filling the air. A few people glanced up, but no one said anything. It felt… safe, in a way. Like this place had already seen too much to be surprised by me.

“I’m Taylor,” I said, sliding into a corner booth. “And I’m not sure if I’m from around here or not.”

Jax sat across from me, leaning back with a lazy grin. “Well, that’s a start.”

I looked at them, trying to decide if they were an ally or a threat. “So… what’s your deal? You just randomly decided to start a café?”

Jax snorted. “No. I work here. And I’ve been running for a lot longer than you have.”

I leaned in. “You’re a runaway too?”

“Used to be,” they said, avoiding my eyes. “Still kinda am, I guess.”

I felt a weird connection, like we were both just trying to survive. “Why’d you run?”

Jax hesitated, and for a second, I thought they weren’t going to answer. But then they sighed. “My mom… she’s not the best. She’d rather pretend I didn’t exist than face the fact that I’m… different.”

I knew that feeling. “Different how?”

“Gay,” they said, almost too quickly. “And I’m not just gay. I’m… messed up. I do things. Things I can’t explain. And she’s terrified of it.”

I nodded slowly. “I get it.”

We sat in silence for a while, the rain still tapping against the café window. I was tired. Not just physically, but emotionally. I’d spent the last two days in a car with someone I barely knew, trying to figure out how to get to New Orleans without getting caught. I’d been kicked out of my family, my home, my identity. And now I was sitting here with someone who seemed to understand.

“What’s your story?” Jax asked.

I looked down at my hands. “I came out to my family two days ago. They didn’t take it well. I tried to talk to them, but it just… got worse. I had to leave.”

Jax nodded. “That’s not something you just walk away from.”

“I know,” I said. “But I didn’t have a choice.”

There

**Chapter Two: Gone South**

I've never been good at staying put. Not since I was eight and carved my initials into the wooden fence behind my old house, only to be caught by my dad and told I was "too much trouble." That was the first time I realized the world didn't want me to be anything more than a problem to solve.

So when I decided to run, I didn’t plan on ending up in New Orleans. I thought I’d just drive straight through, maybe hit up some cities, figure out a way to get to somewhere safe. But the last 48 hours had been a blur of yelling, tears, and a car that broke down in the middle of nowhere. I got out of that car in a puddle of rain, my boots soaked, my heart pounding like a drum in my chest. I was lost, hungry, and pretty sure I’d made the biggest mistake of my life.

And then I saw the café.

It was a small place, tucked between a laundromat and a boarded-up bar. The awning was sagging, the sign above it faded, but it was dry. I slipped under it, shivering, and caught my breath. The smell of coffee and fried dough hit me like a balm. I hadn’t realized how much I missed normal things—like warmth, and food, and not being yelled at.

I leaned against the wall, trying to gather my thoughts. My phone was dead, my wallet was stolen, and I had nowhere to go. I was pretty much a ghost in a city that was anything but. The drizzle had stopped, but the air still felt heavy, like the sky was holding its breath.

That’s when I heard it—someone else under the awning, too. A voice, low and rough, muttering something about “stupid luck.”

I turned my head slightly. A guy was sitting on a stool at the counter, hunched over a laptop. He looked about my age, maybe a little older. His hair was messy, his jeans torn at the knees, and he had a tattoo on his wrist that looked like a broken chain. He looked like someone who had run too far, too fast, and had nowhere left to run.

“Hey,” I said, my voice barely above a whisper.

He looked up, startled. His eyes were dark, tired. “Yeah? You lost?”

“Kind of,” I admitted. “I mean, I’m not exactly lost, but I’m not exactly… anywhere.”

He let out a dry laugh. “You’re not the first person I’ve seen out here.”

I sat down across from him, my back against the wall. “What’s your name?”

“Jax,” he said. “And you?”

“Taylor.”

He nodded, like he was expecting that. “So, what’s your story?”

I hesitated. I didn’t know how much to tell him. I’d told my mom, my sister, my brother—no, they didn’t get it. They didn’t *want* to get it. It was too much, too hard, too… real. And now I was here, in a city I didn’t even know how to navigate, with a guy who looked like he’d been through hell and back.

“I came out to my family,” I said finally. “And it didn’t go well.”

Jax didn’t say anything for a second. He just stared at his laptop, fingers tapping on the keyboard. Then he looked up at me again. “That’s not the first time I’ve heard that.”

I raised an eyebrow. “You’ve done this before?”

He shrugged. “Not exactly. But I’ve run. A lot. I left my town when I was 15. My mom was an addict, my dad was a cop who didn’t care. I just… got tired of being invisible.”

I felt a strange connection, like we were both just trying to survive. “So, what do you do now?”

He smirked. “I do what I have to. Sleep in laundromats, eat whatever I can scrounge up, and hope someone doesn’t recognize me.”

“Sounds like a lifestyle.”

“Yeah,” he said, “but it’s not all bad. You get to meet people. Like you.”

I rolled my eyes, but I couldn’t help smiling a little. “So, what’s your plan?”

He paused, then looked away. “I don’t know. I was heading to Florida. My cousin lives there. But I don’t think I can go back. Not yet.”

I felt a pang of sympathy. “What’s the deal with Florida?”

He looked at me, like he was deciding whether to trust me. “My cousin… she’s got issues. She’s a recovering addict. And I… I’m not sure I’m ready to be around people who still think I’m the same person I was before.”

I didn’t know what to say. I guess I just nodded. “Yeah. I get that.”

The silence between us was heavy, but not uncomfortable. We were both outcasts, both trying to find a place in a world that didn’t want us.

Jax finally looked at his watch. “I should go. I’ve got a train in an hour.”

I felt a rush of panic

**Chapter 3: Gone South**

So, I'm in New Orleans. Two states away from home, a stolen car, a broken heart, and a whole lot of regret. I still don’t know how I got here. One minute I was sitting in my room, staring at the ceiling, trying to decide if I was going to live or die, and the next, I was halfway to the nearest gas station, hyped on adrenaline and the idea that maybe, just maybe, I could be free.

I’ve been walking for hours. My feet hurt. My clothes are soaked from the rain that started an hour ago. I’m hungry. The smell of fresh beignets wafts from a nearby bakery, reminding me I haven’t eaten since yesterday. I pause, my stomach growling loudly, and look around. It’s not the first time I’ve felt like I’m in a movie. New Orleans is supposed to be this vibrant, colorful place—music in the air, people laughing, jazz spilling out of street corners. But I’m too tired to care. I just want to find somewhere to crash, to hide, to not think about the way my mom’s voice cracked when she said, “We love you, Taylor. We just… we don’t understand.”

I’m not sure what I expected when I left. Maybe a sense of clarity. Maybe a place where I could be myself without fear. But here I am, lost in a city that feels both alive and indifferent. I pass a street performer with a guitar, playing something I don’t recognize, and I feel like an outsider in a place that’s supposed to embrace difference. Maybe that’s the point. Maybe this city isn’t so different from my town after all.

I’m about to head back to the sidewalk when I hear a voice. “Hey, you okay?”

I turn and see a kid—maybe my age, maybe a little older—leaning against a lamppost, wearing a faded hoodie and a scowl that matches the one on my face. They’re holding a crumpled dollar bill like it’s a lifeline. Their eyes are tired, like they’ve seen too much, and I feel a strange connection, like we’re both just trying to survive.

“Not really,” I say, and immediately regret it. It sounds too defeated. I add, “Not anymore.”

The kid smirks. “That’s the first honest thing I’ve heard all day.”

I raise an eyebrow. “You look like you’ve been through hell.”

They shrug. “I have. But hey, I’m not dead. So I guess I’m not doing too bad.”

I consider this. “Name?”

“Jax. And you?”

“Taylor.”

“Nice. So, what’s your story? Or are you one of those people who just runs away because they’re bored?”

I laugh, but it’s hollow. “I ran because I didn’t want to be anyone else anymore.”

Jax’s expression softens. “That’s… brave.”

“Is it?” I ask, looking down at my muddy sneakers. “I just wanted to be seen. To be *me*. And my family… they didn’t get it. They didn’t even know what I was talking about.”

Jax nods slowly. “Yeah. I know that feeling.”

They step closer, and I notice a scar running along their wrist. It looks old, but still fresh enough to be painful. “You’ve been through stuff,” I say.

Jax sighs. “You could say that. I ran too. From a lot of things. Family, school, everything. I’m not sure I even know what I’m running from anymore.”

I look up at them, intrigued. “So, what do you do now? Just wander?”

Jax shrugs. “I try to survive. And sometimes, I find people who aren’t so bad. Like you.”

I’m about to say something when a loud voice cuts through the air. “Hey, Jax! You lost again?”

We both turn to see a woman in her late 40s, wearing a bright pink dress and a pair of sunglasses that are clearly too expensive for this neighborhood. She’s flanked by two guys in matching bandanas, one holding a camera, the other holding a switchblade. I freeze.

“Who the hell are you?” Jax asks, stepping in front of me.

The woman smirks. “We’re the Saints. And you, little punk, are trespassing on our territory.”

Jax’s eyes flick to me. “You coming with me or not?”

I hesitate. I don’t know these people. But I also don’t want to be alone. I’ve spent too long being alone.

“I’m coming,” I say, and I follow Jax as they start walking away.

The woman calls after us, “You’ll regret this, kid!”

Jax just smirks. “She’s not the first to say that.”

We walk in silence for a while, the city around us alive with sound and color. I keep glancing at Jax, trying to figure them out. They seem tough, but not unkind. There’s something about the way they move—like they’re used to being on the edge, always one step away from falling.

**Chapter 3: Gone South**

I haven’t eaten since yesterday. The smell of fresh beignets wafts from a nearby bakery, and my stomach growls so loudly I’m pretty sure the street vendor can hear it. I’m standing on the edge of the French Market, looking like a ghost in a crowd of tourists and locals alike. My jeans are three sizes too big, my hoodie is stained with something I don’t want to think about, and my phone is dead. I had one charge left when I left Tennessee, and I spent it trying to find a way out of that town without getting caught.

I used to think running away was a bad idea. I used to think that staying and fighting for my place was better, that I could work through it, that my family would finally understand. But I was wrong. They didn’t understand. They didn’t listen. They just shut down. And so I left. Fast. No goodbye. No plan. Just a stolen car, a GPS app, and a hope that somewhere out there, there’d be somewhere safe.

Now, two states away, I’m not sure if I’m any closer to safety.

I pull my hoodie tighter around my neck, trying to block out the noise, the smells, the people. I don’t belong here. I don’t even know what I’m doing. I just know I can’t go back. Not yet.

A voice cuts through the chaos. “Hey, you okay?”

I jump. My heart hammers. I turn around and see a guy about my age, maybe 17 or 18, leaning against a streetlight. He’s wearing a faded hoodie that says “I’m not a fan of Mondays” and has a backpack slung over one shoulder. His eyes are tired, but there’s something sharp in them—like he’s been through this before.

I nod slowly. “Yeah. Just… lost.”

He smirks. “Lost? You look like you’ve been lost for a while.”

I glare at him. “You don’t know me.”

“I know enough,” he says, stepping closer. “You’re not from around here. You’re not dressed like a tourist. You’re not dressed like you’ve got a plan. You’re dressed like you’ve been running.”

I don’t know what to say. My mind races. Am I in danger? Am I being followed? Did I just walk into a trap?

He notices my hesitation. “Relax. I’m not the cops. I’m not even the kind of guy who’d sell you out. I’m just… a guy who knows what it’s like to be lost.”

I hesitate. I’ve learned to be careful. People don’t usually offer help when you’re in a place like this. Especially not strangers.

But something about him—his posture, the way he carries himself—makes me feel a little less alone.

“I’m Jace,” he says. “You’re…?”

“Taylor,” I say. “I don’t have a last name.”

He raises an eyebrow. “No last name? Or just don’t want to say it?”

I don’t answer. I don’t know what to say. I’ve spent so long hiding who I am, I’m not sure I can even tell myself anymore.

Jace pulls out a baguette from his backpack and takes a bite. “You hungry?”

I nod. “Yeah.”

He grins. “Great. I’ve got a place. It’s not much—just a couch, some Wi-Fi, and a fridge full of expired snacks. But it’s safe. And you can eat.”

I look at him like he’s crazy. “You just… let me in?”

“I don’t have a choice,” he says. “You’re either going to starve out here, or you’re going to end up in some sketchy situation with a guy who thinks he’s tough because he has a tattoo and a skateboard. I’d rather have you here.”

I hesitate. Then, just like that, I decide to take the chance.

We walk through the French Market, past the stalls selling everything from spices to vintage postcards. Jace points things out—what’s worth buying, what’s a scam, which vendors are friendly. He’s not a total stranger, but he’s not exactly a friend either. He’s got secrets, I can tell. The way he keeps glancing over his shoulder, the way he talks about “old habits” and “people who don’t listen.”

I ask him about it.

He stops walking and looks at me. “You know, I used to think running was the answer too. I left my town, my family, everything. I thought I’d find something better. But all I found was… more of the same. Just different people, different problems.”

I swallow hard. “You ran away too?”

He nods. “Yeah. And I’m still running.”

I feel something click in my chest. Like we’re both carrying the same weight, just in different ways.

**Chapter 3: Gone South**

I’ve been running for 36 hours straight. Two states, one stolen car, and a lifetime of bad decisions. I landed in New Orleans at midnight, bruised, hungry, and more lost than ever. My feet hurt. My head hurts. And I’m pretty sure my soul has been kidnapped by a gremlin with a megaphone.

I’m sitting on the steps of a crumbling building near the French Market, watching the sun rise over the Mississippi. The air smells like wet concrete and something sweet — like cinnamon and powdered sugar. Wait, that’s not wet concrete. That’s beignets. 

Oh. God. 

I haven’t eaten since yesterday. I remember eating a granola bar in the backseat of that stolen car. That was it. I didn’t want to think about the fact that I’d left my mom’s house without a word, without a plan, just… gone. And now I’m here, in a city that smells like sugar and regret, and I’m starving.

I try to remember the last time I felt safe. It wasn’t long ago. It was yesterday, when I was still in Tennessee, curled up in my bedroom, crying into my pillow, trying to make sense of the words I’d just said: *“I’m trans.”* I didn’t mean to say it out loud. I didn’t mean to be so *final* about it. But I was tired. Tired of pretending I was a boy, tired of the jokes, the slurs, the way my family would look at me like I was some kind of curse.

They didn’t believe me. Not really. Not until I told them I was leaving. Then they just… stopped talking. Mom cried. Dad slammed a door. My little brother started crying too, like it was his fault. Like he could fix this somehow. 

I didn’t want to leave. I didn’t want to run. But I had no choice. So I ran.

Now I’m in New Orleans, and I’m not even sure where I am. I have a backpack full of clothes, a phone with no service, and a heart that’s about to cave in. I should’ve called someone. I should’ve texted my best friend, Jamie, who’s been my anchor since middle school. But I was too scared. What if she called the cops? What if she told my mom?

I don’t even know if I’m safe here. I’ve heard stories about runaways getting into trouble, getting kidnapped, getting… lost. I don’t know what the difference is between being lost and being safe, honestly.

A voice cuts through my thoughts. “Hey, you okay?”

I jump. A girl is standing in front of me, wearing a jacket that’s too big for her, and a frown that says *I’ve seen this look before.*

“I’m… fine,” I say, too quickly. “Just… tired.”

She sits down beside me. “You look like you’ve been running for a while.”

I nod. “Yeah. A while.”

She studies me for a second. “Name’s Maya. I’m… not from around here either. Or at least, not anymore.”

I blink. “You’re a runaway too?”

She laughs, a little bitterly. “Used to be. Now I’m just… surviving.”

I feel a weird pull toward her. Like we’re both ghosts, both trying to make sense of the world without anyone to hold us together. “What happened to you?”

She hesitates. “My mom… she’s an addict. And I used to take care of her. Not that I had a choice.” She looks down at her hands. “I tried to leave once. I got caught. They called the cops. They thought I was running away from school. I was 15. They put me in a group home. It was worse than my mom’s house.”

I feel a pang of sympathy. “That’s… brutal.”

She shrugs. “It’s not the first time I’ve run. I’ve done it before. Just… not for long. Not like this.”

“Where are you staying?” she asks.

I shake my head. “I don’t know. I just… I needed to get out. I don’t have a plan.”

Maya nods. “Me neither. But I do have a place. If you’re up for it.”

I hesitate. “You don’t even know me.”

“I know enough,” she says. “We’re both running from something. That’s enough.”

I look at her, and for the first time since I left Tennessee, I feel like maybe I’m not completely alone.

“Okay,” I say. “Let’s go.”

**Chapter Three: Gone South**

So here I am, in the middle of New Orleans, with nothing but a backpack, a stolen credit card, and a half-burned copy of *The Catcher in the Rye* that I found in my mom’s car. I’ve been running for two days, and I’m still not sure if I’m running from something or to something. My feet hurt. My stomach growls. And the smell of fresh beignets is driving me insane. I haven’t eaten since yesterday, and I’m starving. But I can’t go back to the bakery. Not yet. Not when I’m still trying to figure out where I even belong.

I’m staying in a park near the river, under a crumbling bench that’s more like a throne for pigeons. It’s quiet here, too quiet. I keep expecting someone to come out of nowhere and yell at me for being a troublemaker. But New Orleans is loud—musical, chaotic, alive. I don’t belong here, but I feel like I’m already part of the noise. Or maybe I just don’t know how to shut up my own thoughts.

I remember the call from the school counselor. “Concerning behavioural patterns.” Yeah, that’s what they called it. Like I was some kind of ticking time bomb. But I wasn’t. I was just… me. And I had the guts to say it out loud, even though it scared me. Even though it scared everyone else. My parents didn’t understand. My brother didn’t know what to do. My sister just cried. And then I packed my stuff and left. I didn’t even say goodbye. I just ran.

I got out of Tennessee in thirty minutes. That’s how desperate I was. I had a friend who had a car, and we hit the road. We didn’t plan anything. Just drove until the map said “Louisiana” and then kept going. New Orleans was the first city that looked like it might be worth staying in. It felt like a place where things happened. Where people had secrets. Where maybe, just maybe, I could find someone who didn’t mind that I’m trans, or that I’m a mess.

But I’m still alone.

The sun is setting, and the sky is this weird mix of orange and purple. I’m starting to get cold, even though it’s still warm out. I pull my hoodie tighter around my neck. I think about my family again. I wonder if they’re worried. If they’ve called the cops. If they’re still looking for me. I hate that I left them, but I don’t know if I can go back.

A voice cuts through my thoughts. “You gonna sit there all night or what?”

I jump. A kid my age is leaning against a tree nearby, arms crossed, wearing a leather jacket that looks like it's been through a war. He's got a messy black beard and a smirk that says he's seen it all. I don't trust him, but I'm too tired to care.

“Who the hell are you?” I ask, keeping my voice low.

“Name’s Jax. And you’re either lost, hungry, or both.” He steps closer, squinting at me. “You look like you’ve been running for a while.”

I don’t answer. I don’t know what to say. I’m not used to people just… showing up.

He sighs. “Look, I’ve been out here for a week. I know the drill. You’re either a runaway, a druggie, or both. But you’re not the usual type. You don’t look like you belong in this town. You look like you’re trying to survive.”

I scoff. “What’s it to you?”

He shrugs. “Nothing. But I’m not going to let you starve to death. Not unless you want to. So, come on. Let’s go get something to eat before it gets too dark.”

I hesitate. I don’t know if I can trust him. But I’m starving, and I’ve been alone for too long. Maybe this is what I need—someone who doesn’t care if I’m a mess.

“Okay,” I say, standing up. “But I’m not staying with you.”

“Didn’t say you had to,” he says with a grin. “Just come with me for a bit. I know a place.”

We walk through the park, past street performers and people sipping coffee like it's the best thing in the world. Jax talks about how he left his town too, how he ran away from his dad, who was a cop. He says he’s been on the road for a while, sleeping in parks, eating whatever he can find. He’s got a history of “concerning behavioural patterns” too, he says, but he’s not trans. He’s just… angry. And messed up. And, honestly, a little bit of a jerk.

I don’t know if I like him, but I can’t deny that he’s different from the people I’ve known. He doesn’t act like the world owes him anything. He doesn’t expect people to understand him. He just… is.

We end up at this tiny diner that smells like coffee and regret. The owner is an old woman

**Chapter Three: Gone South**

I’ve been in New Orleans for less than 24 hours, and already I feel like I’ve been here forever. The smell of fresh beignets wafts from a nearby bakery, reminding me I haven’t eaten since yesterday. My stomach growls, loud and unhinged, like it’s trying to remind me I’m not just a runaway — I’m a hungry, disoriented, emotionally unstable runaway. 

I’m standing on the edge of a street corner, watching a street performer juggle flaming torches while a group of tourists snaps photos like they’re at a zoo. I should be somewhere safer. Somewhere quieter. Somewhere I don’t have to hear the sound of my own thoughts echoing in my skull. But I’m not. I’m here. In the middle of a city that smells like jazz and regret and something sweet and sugary and totally out of my reach. 

I was supposed to meet someone at a bus station in Baton Rouge, but the bus got delayed, and then I got lost, and then I ended up here. I don’t even remember how I got here. I was just… running. 

My phone is dead. My backpack is light — too light. I left everything behind. My old life, my family, my house, my room, my *identity*, all of it. I didn’t take anything. I thought I’d be okay without it. But I’m not. 

I’ve been called a lot of things in my life — weird, messy, a mess — but “disappeared” wasn’t one of them. Not until now. 

A voice cuts through the noise. “You lost?”

I turn around. A girl about my age, maybe 17, is leaning against a lamppost, arms crossed, wearing a hoodie that’s too big for her. She has a tattoo of a cat on her wrist — or maybe a wolf? I can’t tell from this angle. Her eyes are sharp, like she’s been through something and doesn’t want to talk about it. 

I nod, unsure. “Kind of.”

She smirks. “Yeah, well, I’ve been lost for a while. Not that it matters.” She takes a step closer. “Name’s Maya. You’re new here, huh?”

I hesitantly step forward. “Taylor. I… I’m not sure where I am.”

She gestures to the street. “You’re in the French Quarter. You’re not *that* lost.” She looks me up and down. “You look like you’ve been running for a while.”

I don’t know what to say. I want to tell her everything — that I came out to my family two days ago, that they didn’t take it well, that I left in a hurry, that I don’t even know where I’m going. But I don’t. I’m too tired. 

Instead, I say, “I’m not sure where I’m going.”

She laughs, low and dry. “Yeah, me neither.” 

She offers her hand. “Want to grab a beignet? I know a place that’s *not* tourist trap.”

I hesitate, but my stomach is already reminding me I need food. I take her hand. “Sure.”

We walk through the narrow streets, past art galleries and tiny cafes, and eventually end up at a place that looks like it was built in the 1920s. The sign is faded, but the smell is unmistakable. Beignets. 

Maya pulls out a chair. “This place is amazing. They don’t take credit cards. Just cash or food stamps.”

I blink. “You pay with food stamps?”

She shrugs. “I’m not exactly in the best shape financially. But hey, I’ve survived on beignets and hope. You?”

I sit down, hands shaking slightly. “I… I don’t have anything.”

She nods. “Me neither. But we’re both here, right?”

She orders two beignets and a coffee. I watch her as she takes a bite, eyes closed, like it’s the best thing she’s ever tasted.

I feel a flicker of something — connection? Or maybe just the need to be understood.

Maya’s eyes soften. “That’s… heavy.”

She looks at me for a long moment. “You’re not the first person to run from something they couldn’t handle.”

**Chapter 4: Beignets and Broken Things**

I’ve never been good at staying put. Not really. I mean, I’ve been in the same town for practically my whole life, but that doesn’t count. I’ve never *chosen* to stay. I’ve always been running from something—or someone—or both. And now, here I am, in New Orleans, with a backpack full of clothes that don’t quite fit anymore, a phone that’s probably been lost in the chaos of my escape, and a stomach that’s been growling since yesterday.

The smell of fresh beignets wafts from a nearby bakery, reminding me I haven’t eaten since yesterday. I take a deep breath, trying to ignore the way my stomach clenches. I’m too tired to think about food right now, but I also can’t afford to pass out on the sidewalk. Not yet.

I’m not sure how I ended up here. I remember the plan being simple: drive south, ditch the car, find a place to crash, and hope no one finds me. But plans are for people who don’t run away. I didn’t even have a plan for the car. Just a stolen gas card and a map that was probably outdated. I didn’t think about the fact that I’d end up in a city where I didn’t know anyone, and I definitely didn’t know how to get from the interstate to anywhere safe.

I’m sitting on the edge of a bench in the French Quarter, staring at the tourists who walk by like they don’t have a care in the world. They’re laughing, taking pictures, sipping coffee like it’s the best thing in the world. I used to do that. Used to pretend I was just like them. But now, I feel like an outsider in every sense of the word.

My phone buzzes. I almost drop it. It’s a text from my mom. *“Where are you? We’re worried.”* I should reply. I really should. But I can’t. Not yet. Not when I’m still trying to figure out where I belong.

I don’t even know if I can go back. Not after what happened. My family didn’t understand. They didn’t listen. They just… shut down. Like they’d been waiting for me to break. And when I did, they didn’t know how to fix it. They just called me “concerning.” Like I was some kind of problem to be solved.

I sigh and rub my temples. The sun is starting to set, casting long shadows across the cobblestones. I remember once seeing a street performer here, someone who played guitar and sang about love and loss. I used to think that kind of thing was for people who had nowhere else to go. Now I’m starting to think it’s for people who *do* have nowhere else to go.

A voice cuts through my thoughts. “Hey, you okay?”

I jump. Look up. A kid, maybe my age, is standing a few feet away, leaning against a lamppost. They’re wearing a hoodie that’s too big, and their eyes are tired but curious. They look like they’ve been running too.

I nod slowly. “Yeah. Just… lost.”

They smirk. “You’re not lost. You’re *found*.” They gesture vaguely at the city around us. “This place has a way of finding you. Or you finding it.”

I raise an eyebrow. “You’re one of those people, aren’t you?”

They laugh. “You’re not the first to say that. But yeah, I guess I am.”

I lean back against the bench. “What’s your name?”

“Casey. And you’re…?”

“Taylor.”

They sit down beside me. “So, Taylor. You look like you’ve been through a lot.”

I almost laugh. “You have no idea.”

We sit in silence for a moment. The city hums around us, the sounds of street musicians, vendors calling out, the occasional honk of a car. It’s overwhelming, but in a way that feels… alive. Like this place is full of stories, and I’m just one of them.

Casey pulls out a bag from their backpack. “Hungry?”

I shake my head. “Not really.”

“Lie. You’re hungry.”

I smirk. “Okay, fine. I’m starving.”

They hand me a beignet. “You’re gonna have to eat something. You can’t survive on drama and bad decisions.”

I take a bite. The pastry is warm, fluffy, and utterly delicious. I almost cry. “This is amazing.”

Casey nods. “It’s the best thing in the world. But only if you’re not eating it alone.”

I look at them, curious. “What do you mean?”

They shrug. “I’ve been running too. Not for the same reason, but… I get it. You don’t know where to go, or who to trust. You just… want to be somewhere that doesn’t feel like home.”

I think about my family, the silence, the accusations. “I don’t think I can trust anyone anymore.”

Casey sighs. “Yeah, I get that. But you don’t have to do it alone. Not forever.”

I’ve been in New Orleans for less than 24 hours, and already I feel like I’ve lived a lifetime. The city is loud, sticky, and full of people who don’t seem to care if you're lost—or if you’re running from something. I’m wearing a borrowed hoodie from the guy I stole a ride from, and my feet are blistered from walking three miles through the French Quarter. I’m also carrying a guitar pick in my pocket, one I’ve been fidgeting with for the last hour. It’s a nervous habit from home, something I do when I’m trying not to think about what I left behind.

My phone is dead. No signal. No money. Just a backpack full of clothes, a few snacks, and a heart full of betrayal.

I had a plan. Or at least, I thought I did. I’d called a friend in Memphis, someone I used to hang out with before things got too complicated. They said they’d meet me at a diner. But when I got there, the diner was closed, and the only person around was a guy in a leather jacket who looked like he'd rather be somewhere else. He asked me what I was doing, and I told him I was “just passing through.” He didn’t believe me.

I ended up at a payphone, trying to call my mom. She didn’t answer. I called my brother. He didn’t answer. I called my sister. She answered, and I could hear the tension in her voice before she even said a word. “Taylor, what the hell are you doing? You can’t just—”

“I can’t just stay there anymore,” I interrupted, my voice cracking. “You all don’t get it. I’m not who I used to be.”

She didn’t say anything for a second. Then she said, “We love you, Taylor. We just… we don’t know how to do this.”

And that’s when I realized I’d already made my choice. I’d left. I’d run. And now, I was stuck in a city I barely knew, with nothing but my own thoughts and a guitar pick to keep me company.

I ended up sitting on a bench outside a jazz club, watching people move like they had somewhere to be. I was tired. I was hungry. I was scared. I kept checking my phone, hoping it would come back on, hoping someone would call me, hoping—God, I just wanted someone to talk to.

That’s when I saw her.

She was sitting across from me, hunched over a cup of coffee, her eyes fixed on the ground. She had a leather jacket like the guy at the diner, and a black hoodie pulled over her head. Her hair was short, dyed a dark red that looked like it had been dyed in a hurry. She looked like she was trying to disappear.

I didn’t know what to do. I mean, I was a runaway, too. But I didn’t think I’d ever be sitting next to another one.

She looked up. Her eyes were brown, but they were tired. Like she’d been running for a long time.

“You’re new here,” she said. Not a question. A statement.

I nodded. “Yeah. I guess.”

She smirked. “Me too. Sort of.”

I raised an eyebrow. “Sort of?”

She shrugged. “I mean, I ran. But I didn’t run far. I just… stayed in the same town, hiding in plain sight. It’s not the same.”

I wanted to ask her what she meant, but I was too tired. Plus, I didn’t know if I could trust her. I’d been burned before.

“You look like you’re not from here,” I said instead.

She looked me up and down. “You look like you’re not from anywhere.”

We sat in silence for a while. I kept fidgeting with the guitar pick, trying to distract myself from the weight of everything I’d left behind.

“Taylor.”

She nodded. “I’m Jax.”

I didn’t know what to say. She looked like she might be dangerous, but she also looked like she might understand.

“So,” she said, “you’re running from something?”

I hesitated. “Yeah. Kind of.”

She leaned back, crossing her legs. “Me too. But I’m not sure if I’m running or just… waiting for something to happen.”

I looked at her. “What’s the difference?”

She smiled, but it didn’t reach her eyes. “You’ll figure it out when you’re older.”

records.response_text:
**Chapter 2: Gone South (Part Two)**

I’ve been in New Orleans for less than 24 hours, and already I feel like a ghost in a place that’s supposed to be alive with music and history. But all I hear is the clatter of traffic, the distant echo of laughter from a street vendor, and the low hum of a saxophone that doesn’t quite hit right. I’m standing outside a diner called *The Crescent Café*, clutching a crumpled bus ticket and a backpack that smells like old pizza and regret. My feet are sore, my jeans are torn at the knee, and I’m still reeling from the chaos of the past 48 hours.

I keep fidgeting with this guitar pick in my pocket. It’s one of those cheap plastic ones, picked up at a thrift store back home. It’s a nervous habit—something I did when I was scared, or when I didn’t know what to say. Today, it feels like both.

I was supposed to be on a bus to Atlanta, but I got kicked off in the middle of downtown. The driver said I was “too disruptive,” which is the kind of phrase that makes you want to punch someone. I had a sandwich in my hand, and I was trying to explain that I just needed a place to sleep. He didn’t care. Just said, “Get out.” So I did.

Now I’m here, in this city that’s famous for everything except kindness. I don’t know where to go, who to trust, or what to do with myself. My phone is dead. My money is mostly gone. And my family? They’re still back in that town I used to call home, probably arguing over whether I was “just being dramatic” or if they should call the cops.

I lean against the diner door, watching people go by. A group of tourists in matching sunglasses and matching shirts. A street musician playing a worn-out guitar. A man in a trench coat arguing with a woman who looks like she wants to cry. This place feels like a movie set. Too loud, too colorful, too full of people who don’t know me.

And then I see her.

She’s sitting on a bench outside the diner, legs crossed, eyes half-lidded, holding a cigarette between her fingers. She looks like she’s waiting for someone—or maybe waiting to be found. She’s wearing a black hoodie that’s too big, jeans that are too tight, and a look that says *I’ve been here before.*

I hesitate. I don’t know if I should approach her or run the other way. But something about her—her posture, the way she smokes like it’s the only thing keeping her grounded—feels familiar.

I take a deep breath and walk over.

“Hey,” I say, trying to sound casual. “You lost?”

She looks up. Her eyes are brown, but they look like they’ve seen too much. “Not lost,” she says. “Just waiting for the right moment to leave again.”

I sit down beside her. “Yeah. I’m kind of in the same boat.”

She smirks. “You? You look like you just escaped a cult.”

I roll my eyes. “I didn’t escape a cult. I *left* a family dinner.”

She laughs, low and dry. “That’s not much better.”

Her name is Jax. She’s 17, like me, and she used to live in Memphis. She ran away from a foster home, then ended up in a group home, then got kicked out for “acting out.” She’s got a history too, but she’s not one to talk about it. Not yet. She smokes a lot, has a scar on her wrist, and a tattoo on her ankle that says *”Don’t trust anyone.”*

I don’t know if that’s a warning or a mantra.

We talk. She tells me about her foster parents—how they didn’t understand her, how they called her “unmanageable.” I tell her about my family—how they didn’t understand me, how they called me “confused.” We laugh about how we’re both just kids who got tired of being misunderstood.

She offers me a cigarette. I say no, but I take a drag anyway, just to see what it’s like. It burns, but it’s also kind of freeing.

“Where are you headed?” she asks.

“I was supposed to be in Atlanta,” I say. “But I got kicked off a bus.”

She nods. “That happens. I’ve done it twice.”

“Do you ever stop running?” I ask.

She pauses. “Sometimes I think I’m running from something. Sometimes I think I’m running toward something. Most of the time, I’m just running.”

I think about that. About how I ran from my family, from my old life, from the fear that I was never going to be enough. And now I’m here, in a city that feels like it’s made of secrets and stories, and I don’t even know where I’m going.

Jax pulls out a phone and shows me a map. “There’s a place called *The Safe Haven*—it’s a shelter for runaways. It’s not the best, but it’s better than the streets.”

**Chapter Three: Gone South**

I’ve never been good at staying put. Not really. I mean, I’ve lived in the same town my whole life, but that doesn’t mean I’ve been *stable*. I’ve been bouncing between rooms, between moods, between trying to fit in and trying to be myself. And then there was the whole *trans* thing. That wasn’t just a phase or a joke — it was everything. And when I came out, it didn’t go well. Mom cried. Dad yelled. My little sister just stared at me like I’d grown a second head. And then I packed my stuff, grabbed my guitar, and ran.

I didn’t have a plan. Just a vague idea that New Orleans would be better than the place I was from. I thought the streets would be loud, the people wild, and maybe — maybe I’d find someone who understood. But I didn’t count on getting kicked out of a van in the middle of the night. Or waking up in a stranger’s alley, my backpack full of clothes, my guitar pick still in my pocket — a nervous habit I picked up back home.

I’m not sure how long I’ve been in New Orleans. Maybe five hours. Maybe more. My feet hurt. My stomach growls. And I’m surrounded by people who look like they know what they’re doing, and I’m just... me. A sixteen-year-old with a history of bad decisions and a heart that’s still trying to figure out what it wants.

I was walking along the riverfront, staring at the water, when I heard it — a voice. “Hey, you okay?”

I turned. A guy was standing there, leaning against a lamppost, wearing a leather jacket that looked like it had seen better days. He had a scar running from his eyebrow to his cheek, and his eyes were sharp, like he was always watching. He looked like someone who’d been through a lot, but not in a way that made him soft.

“I’m fine,” I said, not really meaning it.

He raised an eyebrow. “You look like you’re not. You lost?”

“I’m... not lost. Just... out.”

He smirked. “Well, that’s a start.” He stepped closer. “Name’s Jax. And you? You look like you’ve got a story.”

I hesitated. I didn’t want to open up. Not yet. But something about him — the way he looked at me like he knew what it was like to be alone — made me want to. “Taylor,” I said finally. “My name’s Taylor.”

He nodded. “Taylor. Cool name. You from around here?”

“Nah. Tennessee. Small town. Not really a place for people like me.”

He laughed, but it didn’t sound bitter. “You’d think New Orleans would be better. But yeah, it’s got its own kind of hell.”

I frowned. “What do you mean?”

He shrugged. “Family stuff. Money stuff. You know how it is.”

I nodded, because I *did* know. I had no idea what he meant, but I knew the feeling. The feeling of being stuck in a place that doesn’t want you.

“Wanna grab a bite?” he asked. “I know a place that serves decent food and doesn’t ask too many questions.”

I looked around. It was late, and I was hungry. I also didn’t have anywhere else to go. “Yeah. Sure.”

He led me down a side street, past a bodega and a closed-down jazz club. The air smelled like fried food and perfume. We walked in silence for a while, and I kept fidgeting with the guitar pick in my pocket. It was a habit from home — something I did when I was nervous, when I didn’t know what to say or do.

Jax noticed. “You always do that?”

I stopped fiddling. “Yeah. When I’m nervous.”

He smirked again. “You nervous?”

“Yeah. Like, *really* nervous.”

He chuckled. “Well, I’ve got a feeling we’re gonna be nervous together for a while.”

We ended up at a tiny diner with a neon sign that read *The Last Chance*. It was packed with people who looked like they belonged there — artists, drunks, old folks with stories. The place was loud, but somehow comforting.

“First time here?” Jax asked, sliding into a booth across from me.

“Yeah. I mean, I’ve seen pictures. But it’s not what I expected.”

“New Orleans is like that. You think you know it, and then you realize you’ve only seen the surface.”

I looked at him. “So... what’s your story, Jax?”

He hesitated. “Not sure I want to talk about it.”

I expected that. But I also expected more. “You’re not like the people I’ve met before. You don’t seem like a total stranger.”

He looked away. “I’ve been through a lot. Not all of it’s pretty.”

I didn’t push. I just waited.

Eventually, he sighed. “I ran too. From my family. From everything. I thought if I left, I’d be free. But it’s not that simple. You think you’re free

I’m just… floating. Like I’ve been thrown out of a moving train and landed in a place that doesn’t belong to me.

I found a bench under a streetlamp near the river. It’s cold, and the wind smells like rain. I pull out my phone, which is 90% full of dead messages. Mom’s last one said, *“We’re trying to figure things out. Please call.”* I don’t want to call. I don’t want to explain. I don’t even know what to say. I just want to be somewhere where no one knows me.

I keep fidgeting with the guitar pick in my pocket. It’s been a nervous habit since I was twelve, when I first started trying to play music and realized I couldn’t even hold a pick without dropping it. Now it’s like a comfort object. A voice cuts through the noise. “Hey, you okay?”

I jump. I didn’t hear anyone approaching. The person is sitting on a nearby bench, hunched over a backpack, their hoodie pulled up to their chin. They look about my age—maybe 17? 18? They have dark circles under their eyes and a hoodie that’s way too big for them. Their hands are folded in front of them, like they’re trying to keep something from falling out.

“Yeah,” I say, too quickly. “Just… thinking.”

They snort. “You’re not thinking. You’re *avoiding*.”

I roll my eyes. “Whatever.”

They don’t seem offended. In fact, they look like they’ve heard this before. “Name’s Jordan. And you?”

“Taylor.”

“Nice to meet you, Taylor. So, thinking about something important or just trying to escape the world?”

“I’m… just trying to figure things out.”

Jordan nods like they understand. “Yeah. I get that. I used to think I had it all figured out too. Then I realized I didn’t. And then I ran.”

I look at them, really look. There’s something familiar in their eyes—something that mirrors my own confusion, my own fear. Maybe I’m not as alone as I thought.

“So,” Jordan says, leaning back against the bench, “you’re a runaway?”

“I’m… not sure if I’m a runaway or just… gone.”

Jordan laughs, a little bitter. “You’re gone. That’s the first step. The second step is finding somewhere to sleep. And the third is figuring out what the hell you’re going to do next.”

I glance at the time. It’s 3:47 PM. “I guess I’m doing the first two.”

Jordan eyes my backpack. “You got a plan?”

I shake my head. “Not really. Just… not staying in Tennessee anymore.”

Jordan smirks. “Well, if you’re not staying in Tennessee, you might as well stay in New Orleans. It’s the closest thing to a place that *gets* you.”

I smile a little. “I like that.”

**Chapter Three: Gone South**

I’ve been in New Orleans for twenty-three hours and I still don’t know where I am. I’m standing in a park, watching people walk by with their heads down, their shoes clicking against the pavement like a metronome I can't sync with. The air smells like fried food and something sweet and cloying—like a bad memory. I keep fidgeting with the guitar pick in my pocket, a nervous habit from home. It’s been years since I last played an actual guitar, but the pick feels familiar, like a part of me I forgot I had.

I left Tennessee in a blur. My mom’s voice on the phone, all fake concern, “We need to talk.” Then my dad, who never talks, just stares at the ceiling like it’s holding the answers. Then my little brother, who I tried to tell *everything* to, but he just hugged me and said, “You’re still my brother, Tay.” Like that made it okay. Like that made it *safe*.

So I ran. I packed a duffel bag, grabbed my guitar case, and jumped in a car with a guy I barely knew from school—his name was Jace, and he was the kind of kid who wore band t-shirts and thought he was cool just because he knew how to use a computer. He didn’t ask questions. He just drove. And when we got to the highway, I said, “I’m going to New Orleans.” He said, “Cool.” And then I was gone.

Now I’m here, and it’s not cool. It’s loud. It’s confusing. And I’m *so* tired.

I found this park by accident. I was just walking, trying to figure out where the nearest bus stop was, when I saw someone sitting on a bench, staring at their phone. They looked like they were waiting for someone. I hesitated, then sat down next to them. It was a girl, maybe my age, with dark hair tied up in a messy bun and a hoodie that was too big. Her eyes were red, like she’d been crying.

“Lost?” I asked.

She didn’t look up. “Not really. Just... waiting.”

I shrugged. “Me too.”

She finally looked at me. “You’re not from around here.”

“No. I’m from Tennessee. Or was. Not sure which anymore.”

She smirked a little. “You’re not the first runaway I’ve met. Or the first trans one, either.”

I blinked. “You’re...?”

“Name’s Lila. And yeah, I’m trans. Or I was. Or maybe I still am. I don’t know. I used to be a girl, but I don’t think I am anymore.”

I felt a weird mix of things—relief, maybe? Like someone else gets it. Or maybe it’s just the exhaustion talking.

“Me neither,” I said. “I’m not sure what I am anymore. Or if I even want to be anything.”

She looked at me, really looked at me, and for a moment, I felt like she saw something in me that I couldn’t see in myself. “You’re not alone, Tay,” she said. “Not really.”

I almost laughed. “That’s what they all say.”

“Not all of them,” she countered. “Some of us actually mean it.”

We talked for a while—about Tennessee, about running away, about how it’s not easy, how it’s not fun, how it’s just... *everything*. She told me she left her family too, after they found out she was trans. They called her a “disappointment.” She said she tried to come back, but it was too much. So she ended up in Alabama for a while, then Georgia, then here.

“I ended up in a shelter for a month,” she said. “It wasn’t great. They didn’t understand me. I had to fight for every inch of space, every inch of dignity.”

I nodded. “I’ve been through that too.”

She looked at me, and for the first time, I saw something in her eyes that wasn’t just pain—it was *resilience*. Like she’d been through hell and was still standing.

“So what now?” I asked.

She shrugged. “I don’t know. I was supposed to meet someone today. A friend from Alabama. They said they’d help me get a job, find a place to stay. But I’m not sure I can trust anyone anymore.”

I thought about my own plans. I’d planned to hitchhike to Florida, maybe get a job at a diner or something. But now, sitting here with Lila, I wasn’t sure if I wanted to go it alone anymore.

“Do you want to come with me?” I asked. “I’m heading to the bus station. Maybe we can figure things out together.”

She hesitated. “You don’t even know me.”

“I know enough,” I said. “We’re both runaways. We’re both tired of being invisible.”

She smiled a little, like she was considering it. “Okay. But if this goes wrong, I’m not taking the blame.”

“Deal.”

**Chapter Three: Goin’ South**

I’ve been in New Orleans for less than 24 hours, and already I feel like I’ve been here my whole life. The air smells like fried food and regret. I’m standing in front of a bus stop that looks like it was built in the 1950s, my backpack slung over one shoulder, my boots scuffed from the drive down. I don’t know where I’m going, but I know I’m not going back. Not yet.

I’ve got a guitar pick in my pocket, one of those little metal things you use to pluck strings. I keep fidgeting with it, like it’s the only thing that makes sense right now. Mom said it was “concerning behavioural patterns.” She didn’t say anything about me being a runaway. I guess I should’ve called first. But I didn’t. I just packed a bag, got in a car with my best friend, Jake, and drove until the map said “New Orleans” and the road ended.

We got kicked out of the car in the middle of the street. The driver—some guy from a punk band—said he’d take us to a shelter, but when he realized we were *runaways*, he panicked and ditched us. I ended up on the sidewalk, holding a gas station map and a half-eaten hot dog. Jake was too busy crying to notice.

So now I’m here, in a city that’s supposed to be fun and full of music and everything that makes me want to live. But all I see is a bunch of people in sequins and sunglasses, pretending they don’t notice the kid in the thrift store hoodie looking lost.

I’m tired. I’m hungry. And I’m scared. Not of the city—no, I’ve seen worse. I’m scared of what happens when people find out who I am. I came out two days ago, and it was like the whole world turned off its lights. Mom screamed. Dad said he’d “pray for me.” My little brother just stared at me like I was some kind of alien.

I don’t know how to explain it. Being trans isn’t something you *do*. It’s something you *are*. And I’m tired of being forced to hide it.

A voice cuts through my thoughts. “You lost?”

I jump. A girl is standing a few feet away, leaning against a lamppost. She’s wearing a leather jacket that’s too big, black eyeliner that’s smudged, and a smirk that says she’s seen everything. She’s got a cigarette dangling from her lips, and she’s watching me like I’m the weird one.

“Kind of,” I say, shifting my weight. “Not lost, just… somewhere.”

She smirks again. “That’s a good start. Name’s Lila. You’re?”

“Taylor.”

“Nice to meet you, Taylor. You look like you’ve been through a lot.”

“I’ve been through a lot.”

She nods, like she gets it. “Come on. I know a place.”

I hesitate. I don’t trust people. Not after what happened back home. But something about her… there’s a kind of familiarity in the way she looks at me, like she’s been there too.

“Where are we going?”

“Somewhere safe. For now.”

I follow her down the street, past street performers and a guy in a top hat playing a saxophone that doesn’t seem to belong to him. Lila doesn’t look at anything but the road. She’s got a scar on her wrist, thin and pale, like it was made with a knife. I don’t ask.

We end up in a back alley behind a bar. The air is cooler here, and there’s a door that’s slightly ajar. Lila pushes it open and shines her lighter. “This is my place.”

It’s a tiny room with a couch, a table, and a TV that’s on mute. A few other people are there—mostly kids, some looking like they belong, others like they don’t. A guy in a hoodie is playing a video game on a laptop. A girl with dyed pink hair is typing on a phone. And then there’s Lila, who pulls up a chair and gestures for me to sit.

“Welcome to the underground,” she says, like it’s a joke. “You’re not the first runaway to crash here.”

I sit down, my backpack slipping off my shoulder. “So… what’s the deal with this place?”

“Just a safe space. Not a shelter, not a rehab center. Just a place where people who don’t fit in can fit in.”

I look around. The people here don’t look like they’re hiding. They look like they’ve made a choice. Like they’ve already decided who they are.

“Why are you here?” I ask.

She sighs. “Same reason you are. Didn’t fit in. Didn’t want to be fixed. Just… wanted to be free.”

I fiddle with the guitar pick in my pocket. “You know what it’s like?”

She nods. “I know. I’m not trans, but I’ve been in the closet. I’ve been scared. I’ve been kicked out.”

**Chapter Three: Gone South**

I’ve been in New Orleans for less than twelve hours, and already I feel like I’ve been here my whole life. The air smells like fried food, sweat, and something sweet and spicy I can’t name. It’s loud, alive, and completely overwhelming. I’m standing on the corner of Chartres and Royal, my backpack slung over one shoulder, my eyes scanning for anything—anything—that looks like a place to crash, a person who might not immediately assume I'm a drug-dealer or a tourist with a stolen passport.

Two days ago, I packed a backpack with clothes I didn’t want to wear anymore, a few snacks, and a mixtape that I hope will outlive me. I left behind a family that didn’t know how to love me, a town that didn’t know how to keep me safe, and a life that didn’t know how to stop breaking. Now I’m here, in the middle of a city that’s louder than any of that.

And then—*boom*—a parade hits me like a wave.

It’s the **Carnival of Shadows**, or whatever they call it. Colorful floats, people in costumes that look like they’ve been dragged through a dumpster, and a brass band that sounds like it’s trying to drown out the chaos. The crowd is moving, pushing, shouting, dancing. I freeze, caught between the noise and the beauty of it all. It’s the kind of chaos I used to crave, the kind of chaos I used to create. But now, it feels like a trap. I don’t know the rules here, and I’m already lost.

“Hey, you okay?” A voice cuts through the noise. I turn to see a kid around my age—maybe 17, maybe older—leaning against a lamppost, arms crossed, wearing a costume that looks like a cross between a pirate and a superhero. His hair is dyed electric blue, and he’s got a grin that doesn’t quite reach his eyes.

“I’m fine,” I say, too quickly. “Just… getting my bearings.”

He raises an eyebrow. “Getting your bearings in New Orleans? You’re gonna need more than that. You’re gonna need a guide.”

I hesitate. I’ve been alone for so long, I’ve forgotten how to trust people. But something about this kid—his confidence, his easy way with words—makes me want to listen.

“Name’s Jax,” he says, extending a hand. “And you? If you’re looking for a place to sleep, I know a guy who’ll take you in for the night. But you gotta be careful out here. This city’s got its own rules, and it don’t like strangers.”

I look him over again. He’s got a scar running from his eyebrow to his cheek, and a tattoo of a dove on his wrist. He looks like someone who’s seen a lot, and maybe been through some shit too.

“Taylor,” I say finally, shaking his hand. “And I’m not a stranger.”

He laughs. “Well, you’re not one, anyway. So, wanna come with me? I’ll show you the ropes.”

I don’t know why I say yes. Maybe it’s the way he doesn’t seem to judge me. Maybe it’s the fact that I’m tired of being alone. Or maybe it’s just that I need someone to talk to, even if it’s just for a little while.

We walk through the parade, Jax explaining things as we go—how the Mardi Gras season is more than just costumes and beads, how the city’s history is tangled with both beauty and brutality, how the streets are full of people who’ve run, just like me.

“I used to live in Memphis,” he says. “My mom… she wasn’t the best. She used to hit me. Not like, *every* day, but enough to make me want to disappear. So I did. Left when I was fifteen. Ended up in New Orleans. Found this guy, Marcus, who took me in. He’s a bartender, works at a dive bar called The Black Cat. He’s got a heart of gold, but he’s got a temper. You don’t mess with him.”

I nod, not sure what to say. We pass a street performer playing a saxophone that sounds like it's trying to scream. A group of kids in glittery masks laugh and throw beads at each other.

“So, what’s your story?” Jax asks, finally.

I hesitate. I don’t want to tell him everything. Not yet. But I also don’t want to lie.

“I came out to my family,” I say slowly. “And they… they didn’t take it well. It wasn’t just rejection. It was like they thought I was *asking* for it. Like I was being dramatic, or something. I tried to explain, but they just shut me out. So I left.”

Jax stops walking. He looks at me, really looks at me, and for a moment, I feel seen.

“That’s not drama,” he says. “That’s real. And you’re not the first person I’ve met who’s had to run from that.”

I feel something shift between us—like we’re both holding pieces of something broken,
