**Chapter Three: Gone South**

I’ve never been good at staying put. Not really. Even before I came out, I was always on the move—just not in the way people think. I didn’t run away from home. I ran from the *place* I called home. But two days ago, I finally did it. I packed my backpack, left a note, and walked out the door. No big goodbye, no dramatic exit—just me and my thoughts, and a few stolen clothes, and a plan that went sideways faster than I could say “I’m trans.”

Now I’m in New Orleans, which is everything I thought it would be and nothing I expected. The air smells like fried food and secrets. The streets are alive with music, and people are dancing like they’ve got something to prove. But I’m just another lost kid, hiding behind a hoodie and a heart full of questions.

I’d been walking for hours, trying to figure out what the hell to do next. I had a little money from my savings, a phone with a dead battery, and a list of things I needed: food, shelter, and maybe a way to get out of this town before I got caught. But I didn’t know where to go. The city was too big, and I was too tired to navigate it.

That’s when I saw the parade.

A colorful street parade was passing by, drums pounding, people in costumes twirling and shouting. It was everything I’d ever seen in movies—vibrant, loud, alive. But then, *bam*—a float blocked my path, and I was stuck, watching as the crowd surged around me. I didn’t want to be part of this. I didn’t want to be seen.

“Hey, kid, you okay?” A voice cut through the noise. I turned, startled. A guy with a Mohawk and a neon vest was holding up a sign that read “Lost? Found? Both?” He looked like he belonged in a music video, not a street parade.

“I’m fine,” I muttered, trying to shuffle past him.

“Nah, you’re not.” He grinned, eyes sharp. “You look like you’ve been running for a while.” He glanced at my backpack, then at my face. “You look like you’ve been running from something bigger than just a boring town.”

I didn’t want to talk. I didn’t want to trust anyone. But something about him—his confidence, his easy way with words—made me pause. “I’m not lost,” I said, voice low. “I’m just… trying to find my way.”

He studied me for a second, then nodded. “Name’s Jax. And you? I’m guessing you’re not from around here.”

I hesitated. “Taylor. And I’m not from here, either.”

He grinned again. “Well, Taylor, you’ve come to the right place. This city’s got more ghosts than a haunted house, and more lost kids than a shelter on a Saturday night.”

I didn’t know what to say. I was used to being invisible, not someone who was *interesting*.

“Come on,” he said, grabbing my wrist and pulling me into the crowd. “I’ve got a place to crash. And I need someone to talk to. You look like you could use a friend.”

I wanted to say no. I wanted to run. But something in his eyes—something real—made me think maybe I *did* need someone.

We wound through the streets, past jazz clubs and street vendors, until we reached a small, rundown apartment building. Jax had a key. He unlocked the door and gestured for me to go in. “It’s not much, but it’s safe. And it’s got a couch.”

I stepped inside. The place was messy but cozy, with mismatched furniture and a single lamp casting long shadows. A girl was sitting on the couch, scrolling through her phone. She looked up, surprised.

“Who’s this?” she asked.

“Taylor,” Jax said. “New to the neighborhood. And I think she’s got a story.”

The girl raised an eyebrow. “You always do. What’s your deal, Jax?”

“Her deal is that she’s not from around here,” Jax said, grinning. “And she’s not alone in being lost.”

I shifted uncomfortably. “I’m not lost. I’m just… trying to figure things out.”

The girl—her name was Maya—smirked. “Well, I’m not lost. I’m *fucked*.” She looked at me, then back at her phone. “But I guess I could use a new person to annoy.”

Jax laughed. “She’s got spunk. That’s a start.”

I didn’t know what to say. I’d expected hostility, not a reluctant ally.

Maya sighed. “Look, I’m not sure what your deal is, but I’m not going to kick you out. Not yet, anyway. But I’ve got rules. No stealing. No lying. And no running off again.”

“I won’t run,” I said, surprised by how honest I sounded.

She studied me for a second, then nodded. “Okay. But if you break any of those rules, I’m not

records.response_text:
**Chapter Three: Gone South**

I’ve been running for two days, and I still don’t know if I’m going anywhere. Or if I’m just running from something that’s already caught up with me.

New Orleans. Of all the places I could end up, it had to be this one. The kind of town where every corner smells like spices and secrets, and everyone’s got a story they’re too proud to tell. I didn’t come here to find a story—I came here to disappear. But apparently, the universe doesn’t like to let me have my way.

I was kicked out of the car in the middle of a street parade. Not the kind of parade you see on TV—no floats, no music, no glitter. This was a real one. People in costumes, beads, and boots dancing down the street like they were born for it. The air was thick with laughter and the smell of gumbo and jazz. I was standing there, frozen, watching it all go by, like I was an outsider in a world that didn’t know I existed.

I hadn’t planned on getting stuck in a parade. I’d been trying to get to Baton Rouge, then maybe Memphis, then… somewhere else. But my bus broke down, my phone died, and now I was in the middle of a city I didn’t know, with a bunch of strangers dancing around me like I was part of the show.

“Need a hand, kid?” A voice cuts through the noise. I turn and see a guy in a sequined shirt, beads hanging from his neck like a necklace. He’s got a camera slung over his shoulder, and he’s grinning like he’s seen this before.

I shake my head. “Nah, I’m fine.”

He shrugs and starts walking, gesturing for me to follow. “Come on, I’m heading to the Jazz Market. You look like you could use a little food and a little less chaos.”

I hesitate. I’ve been alone for two days—no one to trust, no one to talk to. But I’m hungry. And I’m tired of being invisible.

So I follow.

We walk through a street lined with vendors selling everything from jazz CDs to beaded necklaces. The guy introduces himself as Jax. He’s in his late twenties, with a laugh that’s too loud and a smile that doesn’t quite reach his eyes.

“First time in the city?” he asks, handing me a piece of gumbo.

I nod. “Sort of.”

He nods like he understands. “Yeah, I was like you. Running from something. Found this place, and it… changed me.”

I don’t know what to say. Everything I’ve ever known has been small, quiet, and full of judgment. New Orleans feels like a place that *wants* to be loud. I don’t know if I can handle it.

We end up at a little café tucked between two buildings. The owner, a woman named Ms. Lulu, looks like she’s seen it all. She doesn’t ask questions. She just hands me a plate of jambalaya and a glass of sweet tea.

“Eat,” she says. “You look like you haven’t eaten in a day.”

I do. I’ve been surviving on energy bars and whatever I could steal from the car. This is good.

Jax leans back in his chair, watching me. “So, what’s your story, kid?”

I look at him, unsure. “I don’t know.”

He raises an eyebrow. “That’s a funny answer.”

“I used to be a girl,” I say, too quickly. “My family didn’t get it. They didn’t want me to be anything but what they wanted me to be. So I left.”

He doesn’t flinch. Doesn’t laugh. Just nods. “Yeah. I get that.”

I’m surprised. “You’re a runaway too?”

He smirks. “Not exactly. I left a life I didn’t want. But I didn’t run. I just… stopped showing up.”

I study him. He’s got a calmness about him that’s strange. Like he’s been through a lot, but he’s still here.

“What happened to you?” I ask.

He looks away. “It’s a long story. But I think I’m trying to find my way back to something real.”

I don’t know what to say. I’ve never had anyone ask me that before. Not really. My family didn’t care. My friends didn’t understand. The school counselor called it “concerning behavioral patterns.” Like I was a problem to be fixed.

I feel the weight of everything I’ve carried for so long. The shame, the fear, the anger. And now, here I am, in a city that feels like it’s alive with possibility, and someone is asking me if I want to be real.

“Do you think I can be real?” I ask quietly.

Jax looks at me for a long time. Then he smiles. “Yeah. I think you can.”

We sit in silence for a while, eating and watching the parade pass by again—this time, from the window of the café. The music is loud, the colors are bright, and for the first time in a long time, I feel like I might belong

**Chapter 2: Lost in the Mardi Gras**

I didn’t plan on ending up in New Orleans. I mean, I planned on running. I planned on getting as far away as possible from Mom, from the house, from the silence that followed my coming out. But I didn’t plan on the cop. I didn’t plan on the car breaking down. I didn’t plan on being dumped in front of a street fair with a backpack full of stolen clothes and a heart full of nothing.

I was walking down a narrow street, trying not to look too lost, when the music hit me first — that chaotic, thunderous beat that vibrated through the pavement like it was alive. Then came the color, the noise, the bodies — people in glittery costumes, beads flying through the air, horns blaring, drums pounding. A parade. I had heard about Mardi Gras, but I didn’t think I’d *be* part of it. Not like this.

I ducked under a float, trying to get out of the way, but the crowd was too thick. Someone bumped into me, and I stumbled. My backpack hit the ground, and I heard a *thud* — like a heart stopping. I froze. Then I heard a laugh. A rough, sarcastic laugh.

“You okay, kid?” a voice asked.

I looked up. A guy, maybe early twenties, with a neon-green mask and a leather jacket that looked like it had seen better days. He had a cigarette in one hand and a backpack slung over the other. He was grinning, but there was something in his eyes — like he knew something I didn’t.

“I’m fine,” I muttered, picking up my bag. “Just… lost.”

He snorted. “You’re not lost. You’re *stuck*.”

I didn’t know what to say. I’d been lost for days. I’d run from my town, from my family, from the way things had gone down. I didn’t know if I was running from anything or everything. I just knew I couldn’t stay.

“I’m Taylor,” I said, trying to sound confident, even though I felt like a ghost.

“Name’s Jax,” he said, flicking his cigarette into a trash can. “You look like you’ve been running for a while. Want a ride?”

I hesitated. I didn’t know him. I didn’t know *anything* about him. But I also didn’t have anywhere else to go. And the parade was still going on, and I was still standing in the middle of it, like a fool.

“Yeah,” I said. “Sure.”

He smirked. “Great. Let’s go before I get caught in a parade and end up in a costume I don’t like.”

We walked through the crowd, and I watched as the parade moved on, the floats disappearing behind us like memories. Jax didn’t talk much at first, just kept walking, occasionally glancing at me like he was waiting for me to say something. I didn’t know what to say. Everything felt too loud, too fast, too real.

“You from around here?” I asked finally.

He shrugged. “Nope. Just passing through. You?”

“I’m… from Tennessee.”

He raised an eyebrow. “Tennessee? That’s where the ‘concerning behavioral patterns’ come from?”

I stiffened. “What?”

He laughed again, but it didn’t sound quite as confident this time. “You know, the school counselor called my mom. Said something about ‘concerning behavioral patterns.’” He paused. “Turns out, that was you.”

I felt my chest tighten. I hadn’t told *anyone* about that call. Not even my best friend, not even my sister. I had been too scared.

“Yeah,” I said quietly. “That was me.”

He looked at me for a second, then just nodded. “So, you’re a runaway. Got it. What’s the plan?”

I didn’t have a plan. I had a backpack, a few bucks, and a bunch of emotions I couldn’t name. “I… don’t know. Just running.”

He smirked again. “That’s not a plan. That’s a *reaction*.”

I frowned. “What do you mean?”

He looked at me like I was stupid. “You run because you’re scared. You run because you don’t know how to deal with things. But if you’re running from something, you have to know what it is. Otherwise, you’re just running in circles.”

I wanted to argue. I wanted to say I *did* know what I was running from. But the truth was, I didn’t. I had come out, and it had gone wrong. My family didn’t understand. They didn’t *want* to understand. And I didn’t know how to be anything else but me — a kid who was too much for them.

I looked down at my hands, curled around the strap of my backpack. “I just… wanted to be seen. I wanted them to *get it*.”

Jax was quiet for a second. Then he said, “You know, I get that. I used to run too.”

records.response_text:
**Chapter Three: Gone South**

I’ve been in New Orleans for less than twelve hours, and already I feel like a ghost in a place that’s alive with music and color and people who don’t seem to care if you’re invisible. I’m standing on a street corner, clutching a backpack that’s heavier than it should be, and trying not to cry. My feet are sore from walking for hours, my stomach is growling like a bear, and I’m still reeling from the fallout of leaving my family behind.

I had no plan, really. Just a stolen car, a half-baked idea, and a desperate hope that somewhere out there, beyond the Southern sprawl, there’d be something better. But here I am, in a city that smells like fried food and history, and I have no idea what to do next.

The sun is high, and the air is thick with humidity. I’m wearing the same clothes I wore when I left Tennessee—jeans that are too tight, a hoodie that’s too big, and a shirt that smells like regret. I kept my phone, but it’s dead. No signal. No money. Just me and my thoughts, which are loud and unrelenting.

And then—*boom*. A colorful street parade bursts through the street, a blur of beads, music, and people dancing like they’ve got nothing to lose. It’s the Mardi Gras Indians, I think. Their costumes are ridiculous, their chants are loud, and for a moment, I’m completely blocked from my destination—a tiny hostel I found online that looked like a place where people might actually *want* to stay.

I step back, trying to get a better look, but the parade is moving fast. People are throwing beads, laughing, singing. I feel out of place, like I’m watching a dream someone else is having. I try to remember the last time I felt like this—like I belonged. It was before I came out. Before everything went sideways.

I’m still trying to process the last 48 hours. My mom didn’t cry, she just said, “We love you, Taylor. We just… we don’t understand.” My dad tried to explain it like it was a phase. My sister said nothing. And my little brother… he just looked away. I think I broke something in that house. Maybe the air. Maybe my heart.

I had to leave. I couldn’t stay. I couldn’t explain. So I packed my things, stole a car, and drove until the map said “New Orleans.” I thought I’d be safe here. That maybe, somewhere in this big, loud city, I’d find something real.

But here I am, stuck in a crowd, trying not to be noticed.

The parade moves on, and I step forward again, only to bump into someone.

“Watch it, kid,” a voice says. I turn and see a girl about my age, maybe 17, with a messy braid and a leather jacket that looks like it's seen better days. She's holding a backpack that’s just as heavy as mine. Her eyes are sharp, like she’s been through a lot.

“I’m sorry,” I say, too quickly. “I wasn’t paying attention.”

She smirks. “Yeah, well, you're not the first person to get lost in this city. But you're probably the first one who looks like they've run away.”

I open my mouth to protest, but she cuts me off. “You're not the only one, you know. I’ve been running for a while too.”

I blink. “You too?”

She nods. “Name’s Jules. And you?”

“Taylor.”

She eyes me up and down. “You look like you’ve been through hell.”

I almost laugh. “That’s an understatement.”

She pulls her backpack closer. “I’ve got some food. Not much, but it’s something. You want to come?”

I hesitate. I’ve been so careful not to trust anyone. But I’m starving, and I don’t know anyone else here. “Yeah,” I say. “Sure.”

She leads me down a side street, past a jazz club and a vintage shop, and into an alley where there’s a small, run-down apartment with a sign that says *“The Last Stop.”* It’s a hostel, I think. Not fancy, but it’s clean.

“I’ve been crashing here for a few weeks,” Jules says as we walk. “It’s not much, but it’s safe. And the owner’s a bit of a weirdo, but he doesn’t ask questions.”

I follow her in, and for the first time in days, I feel like I’m not alone.

The room is small, with a single bed, a desk, and a TV that flickers. Jules drops her bag and pulls out a bag of chips and a bottle of water.

“You’re welcome to stay,” she says. “For now.”

I sit down, taking the chips. “Thanks. I don’t even know how to thank you.”

She shrugs. “You’re not the first runaway I’ve met. I’ve had my share of… drama too.”

I look at her, really look. There’s something in her eyes that I recognize—pain, but also resilience

**Chapter Two: Neon and Noise**

I’ve been running for two days, and already I’m starting to think I’ve seen everything. But then I walk into the heart of New Orleans and realize I’ve only just begun.

The city smells like fried food, sweat, and something sweet and spicy that I can’t place. It’s loud, too — not just the usual city noise, but a kind of living, breathing energy that vibrates through the pavement. People are dressed like they’re in a costume party, and I’m just a kid in a hoodie and worn-out jeans, trying not to look like a target.

I’d planned to hitch a ride to somewhere — anywhere — that wasn’t Tennessee. I thought I’d be smart, like I always do. I called a friend, a guy named Marcus who used to live in the same apartment complex but now lives in Memphis. He said he’d help. Then I waited. And waited. And waited. Finally, I just… left. I took a bus to Atlanta, then a train to New Orleans, and here I am, stuck in a park with no money, no plan, and a whole lot of anxiety.

I was going to find a shelter. That’s what I told myself. But then I saw it — a parade. Not just any parade, but a full-on Mardi Gras celebration, with floats, music, and people throwing beads like it’s the most normal thing in the world. I tried to cross the street, but a group of dancers in glittery costumes blocked my path. They were moving in sync, laughing, dancing like they owned the city. I felt like an outsider, like I’d stepped into a dream and forgotten the rules.

I was about to turn back when someone bumped into me. Hard.

“Whoa, sorry, man!” A voice, low and gruff, followed by a laugh. I looked up and saw a guy in a leather jacket, one ear pierced, the other missing. He was probably in his early twenties, with a messy haircut and a smirk that didn’t quite reach his eyes. He had a backpack slung over one shoulder and a duffel bag in his hand.

“Are you lost?” he asked, not unkindly.

“I’m… trying to find a shelter,” I said, trying to sound confident. But I knew I didn’t sound like it.

He snorted. “Shelters? You’re in New Orleans. We don’t have shelters. We have ‘safe spaces’ — but they’re mostly for people who don’t look like you.”

I didn’t like the way he said that. “I’m not looking for trouble,” I said, though I wasn’t sure if that was true.

He studied me for a second, then shrugged. “Name’s Jax. You’re not from around here, are you?”

“No,” I said. “I’m from Tennessee. And I’m… trans.”

He blinked. Then he smirked again. “Oh. So you’re the one who’s not from around here.”

I frowned. “What does that mean?”

“It means,” he said, stepping back, “you’re probably not the only one out here with secrets.”

I didn’t like that either. “What are you talking about?”

He looked around, then pulled me aside a bit. “Look, I’m not trying to be a jerk. I’ve been running from things too. I’m just… careful. You know? You don’t just show up in a place like this and expect to be safe.”

I wanted to argue, but something in his tone made me pause. I didn’t have much to lose.

“I’m not looking for a fight,” I said. “I just need a place to sleep tonight.”

He sighed. “Alright. But you gotta come with me. There’s a place I know — not a shelter, but it’s… okay. Just for tonight.”

I hesitated. I didn’t know if I could trust him. But I was tired. And I didn’t have anywhere else to go.

So I followed him.

We walked through the streets, past more parades, past people dancing and laughing and throwing beads. Jax didn’t seem to care about any of it. He just kept walking, like he knew the city better than anyone. When we got to a small building that looked like an old church, he knocked twice, then opened the door.

Inside, it was dim, with a few couches and a kitchenette. A few people were there — some kids, some older folks, all looking like they’d been through something. One of them, a girl with a dyed pink hair and a scar across her eyebrow, looked up from a book.

“Jax, who’s this?”

“Just a lost kid,” he said. “He’s trans. And he’s out of town.”

The girl raised an eyebrow. “You sure about that?”

I bristled. “I’m not a joke.”

She shrugged. “I’m not saying you are. Just… we don’t take chances here. We’ve had people come in with secrets, and some of them… well, they don’t come out the same.”

I looked around. There was something in the air here — a tension, a kind of quiet understanding. Like everyone had their own story, and no one

records.response_text:
**Chapter Three: Gone South**

I didn’t plan on New Orleans. Not really. I mean, I planned on running. I planned on getting as far away as possible from the lies, the silence, the shame. But I didn’t plan on ending up in a city that smells like fried food and regret. I was kicked out of the car in front of a street mural that looked like it had been painted by a drunk, angry angel. The driver—some guy I’d met in a gas station, who said he was “just helping a kid out”—had dumped me off with a bag of snacks and a warning: “Don’t get caught. You don’t wanna end up like me.”

So I walked. I walked for hours, past churches with stained glass that looked like they were crying, past people in feathered hats and beaded necklaces, past a street musician playing a saxophone so loud it made my ears bleed. I was hungry, tired, and more lost than I’d ever been in my life.

I didn’t know where I was going, but I knew I couldn’t stay in one place. Not yet. So I kept walking. And then, just as I was about to give up and sit down on a bench that looked like it had been used as a stepstool for a thousand pigeons, a colorful street parade rolled by—people in costumes, horns blaring, drums pounding like heartbeats. It was loud, it was loud, it was *so* loud. The noise blocked my path for a few minutes, and when it finally passed, I looked up and saw someone standing there, leaning against a lamppost, arms crossed, watching me like I was the weird one.

I blinked. Then I looked again.

She was maybe my age, with wild curly hair dyed something between green and brown, and a necklace made of tiny bells. She wore a dress that looked like it had been stitched together from old curtains, and she was holding a sign that read: *“Lost? Found? Or just lost and found?”*

I almost laughed. “I’m not lost,” I said. “I’m just… not sure where I’m going.”

She tilted her head. “That’s the first honest thing you’ve said.”

I raised an eyebrow. “You’re not exactly the type to make small talk, are you?”

She smirked. “I’m not the type to make *any* talk, unless it’s about how badly I need a shower and a decent meal. You?”

I shrugged. “I need both. And a better plan than ‘just walking.’”

She studied me for a second. “You’re not from here.”

“I’m not from *anywhere* anymore,” I said, and immediately regretted it. It sounded too heavy, too final.

She nodded. “I get that.”

Her name was Lila. She was 17, like me. She’d run away from her town in Alabama a few months ago, after her mom tried to “fix” her by enrolling her in some religious retreat that basically meant “no fun and no identity.” She’d been living out of a van, mostly, but had found a temporary place in a shelter downtown. She knew the city, the shortcuts, the places where the cops don’t look too closely.

“Come on,” she said, grabbing my wrist. “You’re not going to survive this city on your own. Not for long.”

I hesitated. Trust was a luxury I couldn’t afford. But I was too tired to argue. And honestly, I didn’t know anyone else.

---

We walked through the French Quarter, past jazz clubs and street vendors selling beignets that smelled like heaven. Lila showed me where to get a hot meal without getting arrested, and where to sleep without getting caught. She had a way of making everything seem less dangerous, less hopeless.

“I’ve been through this before,” she said, as we passed a mural of a woman holding a broken mirror. “You think you’re the first kid to run away because they’re tired of pretending?”

I looked at her. “You don’t look like you’ve been through much.”

She laughed. “That’s what they all say. But trust me, I’ve been through *everything*.”

We sat on a bench outside a café that smelled like coffee and rebellion. I told her about my family, about coming out, about the silence, the rejection, the way my parents had looked at me like I was some kind of mistake they couldn’t unmake.

She listened without interrupting, and when I finished, she just said, “You’re not a mistake.”

It was the first time someone had said that to me without sounding like they were trying to fix me. I almost cried.

“I’m just… so tired of being invisible,” I admitted.

She nodded. “I know. I’ve been invisible my whole life. But you know what? I learned to find my own light.”

I looked at her. “How?”

She smiled, but it didn’t reach her eyes. “I had to run. I had to leave everything behind. And when I did, I realized I wasn’t just running from something—I was running *toward* something. A better version of myself.”

I wanted to believe that. I wanted to believe that I could

records.response_text:
**Chapter Three: Gone South**

I’ve never been good at staying put. Not really. I mean, I’ve lived in the same town my whole life, but that doesn’t mean I’ve been *stable*. My mom calls it “concerning behavioural patterns.” I call it survival. You grow up in a place where everyone knows your business before you do, where your identity is something you’re supposed to keep quiet, and suddenly, you’re 16 and you’re done. Done with the lies, done with the stares, done with the quiet shame that followed me like a shadow. So I packed my bag, stole a bike, and left. Two states away, in a city that smells like fried food and rebellion.

I ended up in New Orleans at midnight. The city’s alive even then — neon lights, honking horns, people laughing like they don’t have a care in the world. I was hungry, tired, and more than a little lost. I had a few bucks, a backpack full of clothes I didn’t really need, and a head full of noise. The plan was to find a place to sleep, maybe a shelter, but I didn’t even know where to start.

Then I saw the parade.

It was a Mardi Gras parade — or something like it. Colorful floats, people in masks, music blaring from somewhere I couldn’t see. The streets were blocked off, and I was stuck between a line of people moving forward and a wall of color and noise. I tried to push through, but someone grabbed my arm.

“Hey, kid, you lost?”

I turned and saw a girl about my age, maybe a little older, with messy black hair and a hoodie that didn’t match her skin tone. She had a backpack slung over one shoulder, and a frown that said she’d seen too much.

“Not lost,” I muttered. “Just… getting out of town.”

She raised an eyebrow. “You’re not from here, are you?”

“No. Not really.”

She studied me for a second, like she was trying to figure out if I was a threat or a lost cause. Then she let go of my arm and stepped back. “You need a ride. Or at least a place to sleep.”

I hesitated. I didn’t trust people. Not after what happened back home. But I was also *so* tired. I hadn’t slept in 36 hours. “Where do you live?”

She smirked. “Nowhere. I’m a runaway too. Sort of. Not that it matters.”

“Okay,” I said, unsure. “What’s your name?”

“Jesse. And you’re…?”

“Taylor.”

We walked in silence for a few blocks, the parade still going on behind us, a blur of color and sound. Jesse didn’t talk much, but she didn’t push. I noticed she kept glancing at me, like she was trying to decide if I was worth the time. I didn’t know what to make of her. She looked like someone who’d been through a lot, but she carried herself like she didn’t care.

We ended up at a small, run-down hostel on Frenchmen Street. It was cheap, dirty, and smelled like old pizza, but it was *something*. Jesse had a key. She said she’d been staying there for a week. “I’m not a runaway,” she clarified. “I’m a… fugitive. From my parents. And the system. And my own stupid choices.”

I sat on the edge of my bed, the mattress sagging under me. “You don’t have to explain.”

She shrugged. “I don’t mind. You’re the first person I’ve talked to since I left.”

I looked at her. “You left? Like, *just* left?”

“Yeah. I didn’t plan it. I just… couldn’t take it anymore.”

I felt a weird pang in my chest. I didn’t know what it was — sympathy, maybe? Or just the fact that we were both running from something. “What did you run from?”

She looked away. “A lot of things. Mostly, I guess, myself.”

I didn’t push. Not yet. I was still trying to figure out if I could trust her. But I was tired, and I needed someone — even if they were a total stranger — to talk to.

Over the next few hours, we shared stories. Jesse had been in foster care, bounced around between places, and finally just decided to disappear. She had a history of mental health issues, and she didn’t want to go back to the system. She had a job — or at least, she used to — but she’d lost it when she started skipping out on shifts. “I’m not lazy,” she said, when I asked. “I just… don’t want to be anywhere that expects me to be anything.”

I felt something shift between us. Not friendship, not yet. But something like understanding. We were both broken in our own ways, and neither of us had a plan. But for now, we had each other.

We spent the night talking, eating stale cereal, and trying to figure out what the hell to do next. Jesse offered to help me find a job, a place to stay, maybe even a way to stay out of

records.response_text:
**Chapter Two: Gone South**

The humidity makes my t-shirt stick uncomfortably to my back, and I’m pretty sure I’m sweating through my jeans too. I’ve never been to New Orleans before, but I already feel like a tourist. The streets are loud, the air smells like fried food and regret, and my stomach is doing a weird dance between hunger and nausea. I caught a ride with a guy named Jax—some sort of gruff, half-drunk truck driver who didn’t ask questions and didn’t care about where I was going. He dropped me off in the French Quarter, like it was a gift. I guess it was. A gift wrapped in a middle finger.

I was supposed to meet someone at a diner called “The Gumbo Grind,” but it’s 9:30 and I haven’t seen a soul since I got off the bus. My phone is dead, my wallet is missing the cash, and my family’s number is blocked. I’m alone, in a city I don’t know, with nothing but a backpack full of clothes, a sketchbook, and a heart full of bruises.

I’m not used to being alone. Not like this. Not like *this*.

I remember the last time I tried to run—three years ago, when I told my mom I was a girl and she just… *stopped*. Like the conversation had reached the end of the road and there were no detours. I didn’t even get a chance to explain. She just stared at me like I’d grown a second head. I think I said something about wanting to live, and she said something about *not being a freak*. I left that night, too. And I didn’t come back.

But this time feels different. This time, I’m not just running. I’m *fleeing*. And I’m not sure I even know where I’m going.

I walk down Bourbon Street, eyes darting between the neon signs and the people who look like they’ve seen better days. I pass a street performer playing a saxophone that sounds like it’s crying. A group of tourists laugh at something I don’t understand. I feel invisible, like I’m just another face in the crowd—until I see her.

She’s sitting on a bench outside a bar, legs crossed, wearing a hoodie that’s too big for her. Her hair is a mess of dark curls, and she’s scribbling in a notebook. She looks like she’s been through hell and survived. I stop, unsure if I should approach her or just disappear back into the crowd.

“Lost?” she asks before I can.

I blink. “Yeah. Kind of.”

She smirks. “Welcome to New Orleans. We’re all lost here, one way or another.”

I sit down across from her. “You seem like you know what you’re doing.”

She laughs, low and rough. “I do. Or I did. Not sure which anymore.”

Her name is Lila. She’s 17, like me, and she’s got a scar running from her eyebrow to her jaw. I ask her how she got it, and she just shrugs. “It’s a long story. And I’m not sure I want to tell it.”

We talk. She’s a runaway too—though not for the same reasons. She left her town in Georgia after her mom got addicted to pills and started selling them to strangers. Lila’s dad left when she was nine, and she’s been bouncing between foster homes since. She’s got a talent for drawing, and she’s been selling art on the street to make ends meet. She’s smart, sharp, and way too cynical for someone her age.

“Why’d you run?” I ask, not really expecting an answer.

She looks away. “I didn’t run. I *fled*. And I’m not sure I ever want to go back.”

I don’t know what to say. I’ve been feeling the same way. Like I’ve been running for so long, I don’t know how to stop.

We split a sandwich from a food truck. It’s greasy and amazing. We talk about music, about the way the city feels like it’s alive, like it’s got secrets in every corner. She tells me about the time she got caught stealing bread from a bakery and ended up in jail for a day. I tell her about the time I tried to come out to my mom and she just… *stared*. She laughs, but it doesn’t sound like she’s happy.

By the time the sun starts to set, we’re sitting on a rooftop, watching the city lights flicker like fireflies. Lila pulls out her sketchbook and starts drawing. I watch her, curious.

“What’s it about?” I ask.

She hesitates. “A girl. She’s running too. Not from family, though. From herself.”

I don’t know what to say. I feel like I’ve been waiting for someone to understand me.

“I used to think running would fix everything,” I say quietly.

She looks up. “I used to think the same thing. But running doesn’t fix anything. It just… moves the problem.”

I think about my family, about the way they treated me like a mistake. I think about the way I felt when I came out

**Chapter Three: Gone South**

I’ve never been good at staying put. Not really. Even before I came out, I was always running—away from school, away from friends, away from the way things were. But this? This isn’t just running. This is *fleeing*. And I’m not even sure where I’m going.

The humidity here in New Orleans is suffocating. My t-shirt sticks to my back like a second skin, and I’m pretty sure I’ve already lost a sock somewhere in the backseat of the car that dumped me off. I’m standing in the middle of a street that smells like fried food and diesel, and I feel like a ghost. Like I don’t belong here. Like I never did.

I’ve been on the run for two days. Two days of driving, of arguing with a guy who was supposed to be my “guide,” of eating gas station snacks and listening to music that didn’t make sense anymore. I thought I’d feel lighter when I left Tennessee. I thought I’d feel free. Instead, I feel like I’ve just been handed a bigger, heavier backpack.

I’m not sure what I expected when I got off that bus. Maybe a hotel. Maybe a safe place. But instead, I found a street corner with a neon sign flickering in the dark and a guy leaning against a trash can, looking like he’s been waiting for me.

“Hey, kid,” he says, not unkindly. “You lost?”

I cross my arms and stare at him. “I’m not lost. I’m just… not in the best place right now.”

He smirks. “Well, I’m not in the best place either. But hey, you’re not the first runaway I’ve seen in this town.”

I raise an eyebrow. “You’re a runaway too?”

He nods, sitting down on the curb. “Used to be. Not anymore. I’m more of a… *vagabond* now. But hey, if you’re looking for a place to crash, I’ve got a couch. And a fridge full of beer. And a story or two.”

I hesitate. I’ve been taught to be careful. To trust no one. But I’m tired. And I don’t have anywhere else to go.

“Okay,” I say. “But I’m not staying long. Just until I figure things out.”

He grins. “Name’s Jax. And I’ll take that as a yes.”

But then we start to talk about things that don’t have to do with being runaways. We talk about music, about the weirdness of cities, about how both of us feel like we’ve been thrown out of our own lives.

Jax is older—maybe late teens or early twenties. He has a scar running down his cheek, and he smells like cigarettes and something else I can’t place. He’s got a quiet intensity about him, like he’s seen things I can’t even imagine. And yet, when he looks at me, there’s something soft in his eyes.

“I used to live in Texas,” he says. “My mom… she didn’t like me being gay. So I left. Then I got in with a gang. Then I got out. Now I just… float.”

I don’t know what to say. I’ve heard the stories. I’ve heard how easy it is to get lost in the wrong crowd. But I also know I’m not looking for a gang. I’m looking for something else—something real.

“You ever feel like you’re just… waiting to be found?” I ask.

Jax looks away. “Yeah. All the time.”

We sit in silence for a moment. The city hums around us, loud and alive. I feel the weight of everything I’ve left behind pressing down on me, but for the first time in days, I’m not alone.

Jax offers me a cigarette. I decline, but I do take a sip from his water bottle. “So, what’s your name?” he asks.

“Taylor.”

**Chapter 3: Gone South**

I’ve never been good at staying put. Not really. My life’s been a series of “I’m done” moments, each one more dramatic than the last. But this? This isn’t just a phase. This is survival. Two days ago, I told my family I was trans. They didn’t just reject me—they *disowned* me. Mom called me “disordered.” Dad said I was “confused.” My little brother, who used to call me “Tay-Tay,” just stared at me like I’d grown a second head. I didn’t cry. I didn’t even flinch. I just said, “Then I guess I’ll be gone.” And I was.

I left in 30 minutes. No goodbye, no explanation. Just a backpack, a hoodie, and a stolen set of keys. I didn’t think I’d make it out of Tennessee. But somehow, I did. I caught a bus to Memphis, then a train to New Orleans. Now I’m standing on the corner of Rampart and Chartres, sticky with sweat, my t-shirt clinging to my back like a second skin. The humidity is suffocating, and I’m pretty sure I’ve swallowed more mosquitoes than I’ve eaten.

I don’t know where I’m going. I just know I can’t go back.

A couple of blocks down, I see someone sitting on a bench, knees pulled to their chest, staring at the street like they’re waiting for something—or someone. They’re wearing a hoodie too, the same kind I had on, only it’s too big. Their hair is a mess, like they haven’t brushed it in days. They look about my age—maybe 17? 18? I can’t tell. They’re slumped low, like they’re trying to disappear.

I hesitate. I’ve been alone for a while now, and I’ve learned that people are either predators or prey. I don’t want to be either.

“Hey,” I say, voice shaky. “You okay?”

They look up slowly, eyes dark and tired. “Yeah,” they say. “I guess.”

I sit down next to them, careful not to touch anything. “You look like you could use a nap.”

They snort. “I tried. My brain won’t shut off.”

I smirk. “That’s the first thing I thought when I got here. My brain’s been on loop since I left Tennessee.”

They blink at me. “You left Tennessee?”

“I ran away,” I say. “From my family. They… didn’t take it well.”

They nod, like they understand. Or maybe they’re just trying to be polite. “Yeah. I ran too.”

I lean in. “From what?”

They hesitate. “A lot of things.”

I don’t push. Not yet. I’ve learned that some secrets are too heavy to carry. “You want to talk about it?”

They shake their head. “Not yet.”

I nod. “Fair enough.” I pause, then add, “But if you ever do, I’m here.”

They look at me, and for a second, I think they might cry. But then they just exhale and say, “Thanks. I’m… I’m not sure where I’m going either.”

I smirk again. “Well, that’s a start.”

We sit there in silence for a while, watching the world pass us by. The streets of New Orleans are alive with noise—music, chatter, the occasional honk of a car. It’s loud, chaotic, and somehow… free. I feel a weird mix of fear and excitement. I’ve never been anywhere this big before. And I’m not sure if I like it or not.

“Your name’s Taylor,” they say suddenly.

“Yeah. You?”

“Jamie.”

“Nice to meet you, Jamie.”

They smile a little. “You’re not like the others.”

I cross my arms. “And I’m the one with a plan?”

Jamie laughs, a hollow sound. “You have a direction. That’s something.”

I feel a flicker of hope. “I guess.”

We start walking, heading toward the river. The air is thick, but the water feels cool against my skin as I lean against the railing. Jamie is quiet for a while, then says, “I ran away from my mom. She… she kept me locked in a room for years. Said I was ‘broken’ if I didn’t fit into the mold she wanted me to be. I was a girl, but she wanted me to be a boy. She called me names. Told me I was cursed. I was so scared I wouldn’t even cry. I just… froze.”

Jamie shrugs. “It’s not like you can take it back.”

I nod. “Yeah. I guess I did.”

I smirk. “I’m not sure I do. But I’m trying.”

Jordan laughs again. “Fair enough. Follow me.”

I feel my chest tighten. “That’s… not easy.”

**Chapter 3: Gone South**

The humidity makes my t-shirt stick uncomfortably to my back, like it's trying to fuse itself to my skin. I’m standing in the middle of Bourbon Street, which is basically a giant, neon-lit party that doesn’t know I’m standing here with my backpack slung over one shoulder and a bruised heart in my chest. I’ve been on the run for two days, and I’m *still* not sure where I’m going. I just wanted to get out of Tennessee, out of the small town that’s been my whole life, out of the silence and the judgment and the way my family looked at me like I’d suddenly grown a second head when I told them I was trans.

I had no plan. No money. No friends to call. Just a backpack, a few clothes, and a desperate hope that somewhere out there, there was a place where I could be *me* without being shunned.

I wandered aimlessly, trying not to look too lost, trying not to give away the fact that I had no idea where I was. The streets here were loud and colorful and overwhelming. People were dancing, drinking, laughing—*living*. I felt like I was standing in a dream, watching someone else’s life play out in front of me. I wanted to be part of it. I wanted to belong. But I was just a kid with nowhere to go, and I didn’t even know if I could trust myself anymore.

That’s when I heard it—music, but not the kind you hear in a club. It was more like a voice, raw and a little broken, shouting something over a beat. I followed it, half hoping it was a prank, half hoping it wasn’t.

I found a small alley behind a shuttered bar, where a guy was standing in the middle of the sidewalk, shouting at a crowd of maybe three people. He had a shaved head, a leather jacket that looked like it had seen better days, and eyes that were too sharp for someone who looked like they hadn’t slept in days.

“You think you know what it means to be lost?” he yelled, gesturing wildly. “You think you know what it means to be *alone*? You don’t. You just think you do.”

A girl stood next to him, arms crossed, looking like she was about to laugh. “You’re not helping, Jax.”

“Helping? I’m *telling* the truth.”

“Yeah, and that’s why no one listens to you.”

I leaned against the wall, listening. It was weird, but I felt like I *understood* them. Like they were speaking a language I had only just started to learn.

“Hey,” I said, stepping forward, voice quiet but not timid. “You guys… you don’t look like you belong here either.”

Jax turned, eyes narrowing. “You’re not from around here, are you?”

“No,” I said. “I’m from Tennessee. And I just… ran away.”

He blinked. The girl—let’s call her Maya—snorted. “Of course you did.”

“I didn’t run away *for* anything,” I said, trying to sound more confident than I felt. “I just… needed to get out.”

Jax studied me for a second, then sighed. “Name?”

“Taylor.”

He nodded. “Taylor. Okay. You’re either brave or stupid. Probably both.”

Maya rolled her eyes. “He’s always like that.”

“Hey, I’ve been through a lot,” Jax said, defensively. “I’ve seen people make bad choices. Some of them turn out okay. Some of them don’t.”

I didn’t know what to say to that. I guess I just nodded.

“Look,” Jax said, stepping back. “You’re not gonna get far on your own. You need a place to crash. You need food. You need someone who *gets* it.”

“I don’t even know if I can trust anyone,” I admitted, my voice low.

He smirked. “Well, that’s a start.”

Maya crossed her arms again. “You’re not gonna be able to trust *anyone* in this city, Taylor. You’re gonna get taken advantage of. You’re gonna get hurt.”

“I know,” I said. “I just… I don’t know what else to do.”

Jax looked at me for a long moment, then nodded. “Alright. You’re coming with us.”

“Wait—what?!”

“You heard me. We’re not the best people, but we’re not jerks. We’re just… looking for our own way out, too. You’re welcome to come along.”

I hesitated. It felt like a trap. Like I was walking into something dangerous. But the alternative was just standing here, feeling like I was going to collapse under the weight of everything.

“Okay,” I said. “I’ll come.”

Maya raised an eyebrow. “You’re *sure* about this?”

“I don’t have a choice,” I said, and even I wasn’t sure if I believed that.

---

records.response_text:
**Chapter Three: Gone South**

I’ve never been good at staying put. Not really. Even before I came out, I was the kid who’d rather be somewhere else. Somewhere with more lights, more noise, more *life*. But Tennessee? That’s not life. That’s a slow burn, a constant itch you can’t scratch. And now I’m in New Orleans, which is *definitely* life. But not the kind I expected.

The humidity makes my t-shirt stick uncomfortably to my back. I should’ve worn something lighter. Or maybe not worn anything at all. I’m still trying to figure out how to survive this place. The city feels like it’s breathing, like it's got secrets in every corner and a heartbeat in every brick. I’m not sure if I like that or not.

I had a plan, sort of. I’d hitchhiked out of town, got a ride from some guy who looked like he was more interested in his phone than me. He dropped me off in the French Quarter, and now I’m standing in front of a street musician with a guitar and a voice that sounds like it’s been through a war. I don’t have money for a hotel. I don’t have a plan. I just have this t-shirt that’s stuck to my back and a whole lot of questions I don’t know how to answer.

I’m tired. I’m hungry. And I’m scared.

I remember the last 48 hours like a bad dream. My parents didn’t take it well. They didn’t even really *try*. My mom cried, but not for me. My dad just stared at me like I was some kind of mistake. And my little sister? She said nothing. Just looked away. That’s what kills me the most. Not the rejection, not the anger. The silence.

So I ran. And I didn’t stop running until I got here.

I’m not sure what I expected from New Orleans. Maybe a better chance at anonymity. Maybe a place where I could just… *be*. But this city is full of people, and I’m just another face in the crowd. And I know I’m not safe. I’ve heard stories about runaways getting into trouble here—some worse than others. I don’t want to end up in a shelter with a social worker who’ll ask me all the wrong questions.

I start walking, trying to figure out where to go. The streets are loud, the smells are overwhelming. There's music, laughter, and something that feels like danger lurking just out of sight.

That’s when I see her.

She’s sitting on the steps of a church, looking like she’s been through hell and back. She’s maybe my age, with a hoodie that’s too big and a backpack slung over one shoulder. Her hair is pulled back in a messy bun, and her eyes are tired but sharp. She looks like she’s waiting for someone—or maybe she’s just waiting to be found.

I hesitate. I don’t trust people easily. Especially not strangers. But something about her makes me stop. Maybe it’s the way she’s watching the street like she’s expecting something. Or maybe it’s the way she looks like she’s been through something too.

“Hey,” I say, trying to keep my voice light. “You lost?”

She doesn’t look up. “Nah. Just waiting.”

I sit down next to her, careful not to make it seem like I’m intruding. “You look like you could use a break.”

She finally looks at me. “So do you.”

I laugh, but it comes out more like a sigh. “Yeah. I guess I do.”

She nods, like she understands. “Name’s Mira.”

“Taylor.”

There’s a pause. Then she says, “You’re not from around here, are you?”

I shake my head. “Nope. From Tennessee. Backwater, mind you.”

She smirks. “I knew it. You’re the kind of kid who runs away from a town that’s too small for your soul.”

I raise an eyebrow. “You’re not wrong.”

She leans back against the step, crossing her legs. “I’m from Texas. But I’ve been running for longer than you have. So, tell me—what’s your story?”

I hesitate. I don’t want to open up. Not yet. But something about Mira makes me feel like I don’t have to be alone with this.

“I came out to my family two days ago,” I say slowly. “And it didn’t go well. They didn’t understand. They didn’t accept it. So I ran.”

She listens, not saying anything. Just nods. Then she says, “I came out to mine when I was 15. They threw me out. I didn’t have anywhere to go. So I started running.”

I look at her. “You’ve been running for a while.”

She nods. “Three years. I’ve been bouncing between cities, sometimes staying with people, sometimes just sleeping in alleys.”

I feel a pang of something—maybe sympathy? Maybe fear? “How do you do it?” I ask.

She shrugs. “You learn to survive. You learn to trust no one. You learn to keep moving.”

I want to argue with her.

**Chapter 3: Gone South**

I’ve never been good at staying put. Not really. I mean, I’ve lived in the same town my whole life—Tullahoma, Tennessee—so I guess I’m not exactly a stranger to being stuck. But this? This is different. This is *running*. And I’m not just running from my family, or my town, or the stupid, judgmental eyes that followed me around like I was some kind of cursed thing. I’m running from the past, from the hurt, from the lies, and from the fear that I’m not *enough*. 

I ended up in New Orleans around midnight, after a long, stupid ride in a van with a guy named Jax who thought he was some kind of rebel. He said he was a “free spirit,” which is code for “I don’t have a job and I’ve never had a plan.” We got pulled over by cops, and somehow I ended up in the middle of a downtown alley, my backpack on fire, my shoes stolen, and my dignity in shreds. 

I was halfway to the nearest bus stop when a cat showed up. Not just any cat—this one was all black, with one eye that looked like it had seen too much. It followed me for half a block, like it was trying to decide if I was worth saving or not. Then it disappeared down an alley, leaving me alone with my thoughts and a bruised ego. 

I didn’t sleep much that night. Just curled up in a doorway behind a laundromat, watching the neon signs flicker and wondering if I’d made a mistake. 

By morning, I was starving. My stomach growled like a beast, and I was starting to feel the weight of the world pressing down on me. I walked aimlessly through the French Quarter, taking in the noise, the smells, the chaos of a city that didn’t care about me. I wasn’t sure if I was looking for a place to stay or just something to do. 

Then I saw her.

She was sitting on a bench outside a coffee shop, looking like she’d been through hell and back. She was wearing a hoodie that was too big, and her hair was a mess of curls and dye jobs that didn’t match. Her eyes were tired, but there was something sharp in them—like she was always one step away from snapping.

I hesitated before sitting down. “Hey,” I said, trying to sound casual. “Mind if I join you?”

She looked up, and for a second, I thought she was going to yell at me. But then she just sighed and nodded. “Sure. Sit.”

I did.

“I’m Mira,” she said, handing me a napkin and a pen. “You’re the one with the cat, right?”

I blinked. “How do you know that?”

She smirked. “You didn’t run away from home, did you?”

I didn’t answer right away. I wasn’t sure if I wanted to tell her. But something about her made me feel… less alone. Maybe it was the way she didn’t look surprised, like she’d seen this before.

“I came out to my family two days ago,” I finally said. “And it didn’t go well.”

She nodded, like she’d expected that. “Yeah. You don’t come out to your family and expect a parade. Especially not in a place like Tennessee.”

I stared at her. “You… you’ve done this before?”

She shrugged. “Not exactly. But I’ve run away before. A few times.”

I leaned in. “You’re a runaway too?”

She smiled, but it didn’t reach her eyes. “I’m a runaway with issues and secrets. You’re a runaway with a lot of feelings and no plan.”

I laughed, but it came out more like a cough. “I don’t have a plan.”

“Good,” she said. “Because if you did, you’d be back home by now.”

We talked for hours—about everything and nothing. She told me she used to live in Florida, but her mom kicked her out when she started dating a girl. I told her about my dad calling me “a mistake” and my mom pretending she didn’t hear me when I said I was trans. 

We found a place to crash in an old apartment complex on the edge of the Quarter. It was run-down, but clean. Mira knew the place—she’d been there before. “It’s safe,” she said. “For now.”

I didn’t ask how she knew. I just followed her.

That night, I lay on a couch that smelled like old pizza and regret. Mira was on the floor, scrolling through her phone. I couldn’t sleep. My mind kept replaying the argument with my mom, the way she’d looked at me like I was a monster. 

I turned to Mira. “Do you ever think about going back?”

She paused. “Sometimes.”

“I don’t think I can.”

She looked up. “You don’t have to. Not yet.”

I wanted to believe her. I really did. But part of me still felt like I was just delaying the inevitable. What if I end up in another town, another family, another disaster? What if I’m still not enough

I just wanted to get out. Out of Tennessee, out of my family, out of the pitying looks and the “we’ll talk about this when you’re older” speeches. I wanted to breathe. To be seen. To not be a footnote in someone else's story.

So I hitchhiked. A guy in a pickup truck picked me up, said he was going to Baton Rouge, and I said, “Yeah, that’s fine.” I didn’t know how long it would take. I didn’t know if I’d be safe. I didn’t know if I’d even make it out of Tennessee. But I made it. And now I’m here, sitting on a bench outside a diner, eating a greasy burger like it’s the last meal I’ll ever have. She smirks. “You’re not from around here, huh?”

She nods. “Yeah. I know that look.”

“What happened?” she finally says.

**Chapter Three: Gone South**

I didn’t plan on running. Not really. But when the air in my house felt like it was slowly suffocating me, when every glance from my family felt like a judgment, I just… *snapped*. I packed a backpack, grabbed my phone, and took off before anyone could say “no.” Two states away, in a city I’d only seen in pictures, I thought I’d find freedom. Instead, I found a street full of strangers, a city that hummed with life I didn’t understand, and a stray cat that followed me for half a block before vanishing into an alley like it had a secret of its own.

I was exhausted. Not just from the long drive, but from the weight of everything I’d left behind. My parents’ silence, my sister’s pitying eyes, my mom’s tears when she found out I was gone. It all felt like a movie I couldn’t stop rewinding. I kept thinking about what the counselor had said on the phone—*“concerning behavioural patterns.”* Like I was some kind of broken experiment. I wanted to scream at them, to tell them I wasn’t “dramatic,” I was *real*. But I didn’t. I just ran.

Now, standing on the edge of Bourbon Street, I felt smaller than ever. The lights were too bright, the music too loud, and my stomach was growling like it knew I was lost. I had no money, no plan, and no idea what to do next. All I had was my phone—mostly dead, and mostly useless—and a backpack full of old clothes, a few snacks, and a tiny, invisible piece of me that still hoped someone would come looking for me.

The cat reappeared behind me, licking its paw like it was doing something important. I almost kicked it. It was annoying, and I was already stressed enough. But then it meowed—softly, almost like it was trying to comfort me. I rolled my eyes, but I followed it down the alley. It led me to a door that was slightly ajar, and I hesitated before pushing it open.

Inside was a small, dimly lit room with mismatched furniture and a TV playing something I couldn’t hear. A guy was sitting on the floor, hunched over a laptop, his eyes red and his hands shaking. He looked up as I entered, and for a moment, I thought he was going to yell at me. But instead, he just sighed and said, “You’re not the first one to crash here.”

“Uh… thanks,” I said, unsure if I should be insulted or not. “I’m not sure where I’m supposed to sleep.”

He looked me up and down, like he was trying to figure out if I was a threat. “Name’s Jax. And you’re not supposed to sleep anywhere. You’re supposed to *not* be here at all.”

“I’m not a criminal,” I said, defensive. “I just… ran away.”

He snorted. “Yeah, and I’m a certified genius. What’s your story?”

I hesitated. I didn’t want to tell him everything. Not yet. But something about Jax—his tired eyes, the way he kept glancing at the door like he was waiting for someone—made me feel like maybe I wasn’t alone in this.

“I’m… trans,” I said slowly, like it was a confession. “And my family… they didn’t take it well. I just needed to get out.”

Jax didn’t react immediately. He just stared at me, then slowly sat up straighter. “Okay,” he said. “So, you’re not the first runaway I’ve met. But you’re the first one who actually *tells* me why they ran.”

I frowned. “Why do you care?”

“Because I used to be you,” he said. “Back when I was 15, I left my town too. My dad was a real piece of work—beat me, called me names, told me I was a ‘disgrace’ because I liked girls. I didn’t come out until I was 17, and by then, it was too late. I ended up in foster care for a while. Then I met this guy who got me into the life of a street kid. We were a family, kind of. We survived together.”

I listened, fascinated and terrified all at once. “And you’re still out here?”

He smiled a little, but it didn’t reach his eyes. “I’m trying to be. I have a place to sleep, food, and a few people who don’t judge me. But it’s not easy. You have to be careful. People here—especially the cops—they don’t care about your story. They just want to lock you up.”

I felt a chill. “So… what do I do now?”

He looked at me for a long moment, then stood up. “First thing? You need a plan. Second thing? You need to stop running. Running only gets you so far.”

I just… I don’t know how to do this.”

I’ve seen this kind of cat before—torn between survival and curiosity, like it’s trying to decide if it wants to befriend me or eat me. I watch it go, feeling like I’ve been followed by a ghost all day.

I should’ve called someone. Mom, maybe. Or my brother, who’s always been the one who knew how to fix things. But I didn’t. I was too scared. Scared that if I told anyone, they’d just try to bring me back. Scared that I’d end up back in that house, that town, surrounded by people who didn’t understand me. 

So I ran. 

And now I’m here, in a city that’s famous for its music, its history, and its ghosts. I’m not sure which of those I’m more afraid of.

I walk down Frenchmen Street, trying to blend in, trying to look like I belong. The street is alive with music, with people, with a kind of energy that makes me want to scream or dance or something. I stop at a tiny bar called *The Golden Gator*, hoping for a free drink and a place to sleep. The bouncer is a guy with a beard and a tattoo of a snake coiled around a bottle. He doesn’t look impressed.

“Lost, kid?” he asks, leaning against the door.

“I’m just… passing through,” I say, trying to sound confident. It doesn’t work.

I almost laugh. “That’s an understatement.”

He shrugs and opens the door. “Come on in. We’ve got a couch. Just don’t break anything.”

“Free,” she says. “You look like you need it.”

“Thanks,” I say, taking it. It tastes like hops and hope.

Then there’s someone else—someone who looks like they’ve been through hell and back.

They’re sitting in the corner, wrapped in a blanket, their head in their hands. Their skin is pale, their clothes are worn, and there’s something about the way they’re holding themselves that makes me think they’re carrying more than just a heavy backpack.

I sit up, hesitating. “Hey. I’m Taylor.”

They look up, eyes red and tired. “Yeah. I’m… I’m Jordan.”

**Chapter 3: Gone South**

I’ve never been good at staying put. Not really. I mean, I’ve lived in the same town my whole life, but that doesn’t mean I’ve been *stable*. Some people grow up. I grew up pretending. And then—*boom*—I came out. And it didn’t go well. So here I am, two states away, with a busted-down sedan and a suitcase full of clothes that don’t fit me anymore. I got out of there faster than I thought I could. My mom’s voice still echoes in my head: *“You’re not even sure who you are, Taylor.”* Yeah, thanks, Mom. Real supportive.

New Orleans. I thought it’d be different. I thought the humidity would be worse, the streets louder, the people more… alive. But I’m just a kid with a backpack and a heart full of static. I didn’t even know where I was going, just that I had to get out. I figured I’d crash at a friend’s, or maybe find a shelter. But when I got off the bus, the city looked like a dream that had forgotten me. I was lost, and I didn’t even know if I wanted to be found.

That’s when the cat showed up.

It was a stray, all scruffy and wide-eyed, trailing me like it knew something I didn’t. I almost kicked it. I was too tired, too angry, too *everything* to care. But it followed me for half a block before vanishing down an alley. I rolled my eyes. Maybe it was just a coincidence. Or maybe it was a sign. I don’t know. I’ve been called a lot of things—delusional, dramatic, unstable—but *spirit animal*? That’s new.

I ended up at a park near the river, sitting on a bench that smelled like old beer and regret. I pulled out a sandwich I’d stolen from a vending machine and tried not to think about the fact that I’d left my phone in the car, and my money, and my hopes, and my *family* behind.

“Hey, you lost?”

I jumped. A girl stood a few feet away, leaning against a tree, arms crossed. She looked about my age—maybe 17?—with dark hair pulled into a messy bun and a hoodie that was too big for her. She had a scar running along her eyebrow, and something about her made me feel like I’d seen her somewhere before.

“Not lost,” I said, taking a bite of the sandwich. “Just… wandering.”

She raised an eyebrow. “Wandering? You look like you’ve been wandering for a while.”

I didn’t answer. What was there to say? That I ran away? That I’m trans? That I don’t know where I’m going? I didn’t think I could trust anyone—not my family, not the town, not even myself. And now there was this girl, like she knew something I didn’t.

“Name’s Lila,” she said, stepping closer. “You look like you could use a friend.”

I wanted to say no. I wanted to say I don’t need friends. But I also wanted to know what it felt like to not be alone. So I nodded.

“Taylor,” I said.

She grinned. “Cool name. You’re from around here?”

“Tennessee,” I said. “Small town. Backwater. You know the drill.”

She laughed. “Yeah, I get that. I’m from Texas. Small town too. And I ran away *months* ago. So I get it.”

I looked at her. “You’re not… like me, are you?”

**Chapter Two: The City That Never Sleeps (But Sometimes Judges)**

So, I’m in New Orleans. Or at least, I’m *near* New Orleans. I got dumped off in a sketchy part of the French Quarter, my backpack full of clothes that don’t fit me anymore, my phone dead, and my heart about to explode from the sheer amount of emotion I’ve been bottling up for weeks. I thought running would fix everything. I thought leaving Tennessee would make the silence in my head stop echoing. But here I am, standing in the middle of a street that smells like fried food and regret, with a stray cat following me like I’m some kind of lost tourist.

It’s a stupid cat. It’s got a collar with a tag that says “Mittens” in glittery font, like it thinks it’s a movie star. I ignore it. I don’t need a cat to remind me I’m lost. I already know that.

I’m not sure how long I stand there, just breathing in the humid air, trying to remember the last time I felt safe. The last time I didn’t have to lock the bathroom door when I peed. The last time I didn’t have to hide the way I look in the mirror. But those memories are too heavy. I can’t carry them. So I start walking, not knowing where I’m going, just hoping the city will take me somewhere.

I end up at a park, sitting on a bench under a tree that’s shedding leaves like it’s trying to get rid of me. The park is quiet, too quiet. I used to love parks. Used to think they were safe spaces. But now, every leaf looks like a warning. I take a deep breath and try to steady myself. I need to find shelter. I need food. I need someone who won’t judge me for being a kid who ran away from home.

Then I see her.

She’s sitting on a bench across from me, legs crossed, wearing a hoodie that’s too big for her, and a necklace with a cracked locket. She’s got messy hair and eyes that look like they’ve seen too much. She’s staring at her hands, like she’s waiting for something to happen.

I’m not sure why I start talking. Maybe it’s the cat, still following me like it’s got a mission. Or maybe it’s because I’ve been so alone for so long that I’ve forgotten how to be scared of people. I clear my throat and say, “Hey. You lost too?”

She looks up. For a second, I think she’s going to run. But then she smirks, like she’s been expecting this.

“Nah,” she says. “I’m just waiting for someone to ask me that.”

I sit down next to her. The bench is cold. I pull my knees up to my chest.

“I’m Taylor,” I say. “I ran away.”

She nods. “I’m Lila. I’ve been running for a lot longer.”

We don’t talk much at first. We just watch the people walk by—tourists, drunks, old people with tired eyes. The sun is starting to set, painting the sky in shades of orange and purple. I feel a little safer, sitting here with someone else who knows what it’s like to be untethered.

Lila pulls out a cigarette and offers me one. I shake my head. “No thanks. I don’t smoke.”

She shrugs. “Suit yourself. But you’re gonna need something to keep you warm tonight.”

She takes a drag and exhales slowly. “So, what’s your story?”

I hesitate. I don’t know how much to tell her. I don’t even know if I can trust her. But I also don’t have anyone else. So I start talking. I tell her about Tennessee, about the town, about my family not getting it, about coming out and being rejected. I tell her about the argument, the yelling, the slamming doors, the way I felt like I was just another problem to be solved.

She listens. And when I finish, she doesn’t say anything for a while. Then she says, “You’re not a problem. You’re just… complicated.”

I laugh, but it doesn’t sound like I mean it. “Yeah. Complicated.”

She looks at me. “I know that feeling.”

And then she tells me her story.

Lila’s not a runaway in the traditional sense. She’s a foster kid who got kicked out after trying to get help for her addiction. She’s got a history of trauma, of being bounced around, of trying to find a place where she fits. She’s got a chip on her shoulder and a heart that’s been bruised more than once.

But she also has a sense of humor. She’s sarcastic and sharp, but she’s not cruel. She’s just… damaged, like me.

**Chapter Three: Gone South**

I didn’t plan on ending up in New Orleans. Not really. I mean, I planned on running, sure — but not from Tennessee to the French Quarter. I thought I’d hitchhike to Memphis, maybe catch a bus to Atlanta, and figure it out from there. But somehow, I ended up here, stranded on the sidewalk outside a bistro that smells like crawfish and regret. The sun is blinding, and my backpack is heavy, and I’ve got a bruised heel from trying to run faster than a car would stop. I’m tired, hungry, and more lost than I’ve ever been.

A stray cat follows me for half a block before disappearing down an alley. I don’t know if it’s a sign or just a cat. Probably a cat. I keep walking, my eyes darting between the crowds and the buildings, trying to spot something — anything — that might help me survive this day.

I’m not used to cities. Back in Tennessee, the only people I ever saw were either my family or the ones who gave me the side-eye at school. Now, there’s too many people, too much noise, and I feel like I’m constantly being watched. I wrap my hoodie tighter around my neck, even though it’s already soaked through from the rain earlier.

I stop at a payphone — the last one I can find — and try to call my mom. She doesn’t answer. I try again. Still nothing. I’m not surprised. I know how this goes. She’ll call the cops. They’ll find me. And then I’ll be back home, trapped in a house that feels like a prison, with parents who don’t understand me and a brother who thinks I’m just being dramatic.

I hang up and start walking again, my feet dragging. I need a place to sleep, food, maybe some cash. I’ve got a little bit of money saved up — from my part-time job at the gas station — but I’m not sure how long it’ll last. I’m not even sure if I should trust myself anymore.

That’s when I see her.

She’s sitting on a bench near the river, legs crossed, wearing a leather jacket that’s too big for her, and a hoodie that’s the same color as my own. She’s got a sketchbook open on her lap, drawing something that looks like a person — but with too many eyes and too many arms. She looks up as I approach, like she already knows I’m here.

“Lost?” she asks, not unkindly.

I hesitate. “Kind of.”

She smirks. “Me too.”

I sit down across from her. “You’re a runaway too?”

She nods. “Used to be. Now I’m just… surviving.”

I don’t know what to say. I’ve never met anyone who’s been through what I’ve been through. Not really. My family didn’t exactly *support* me when I came out. They called it a phase. They prayed for me to change. They locked the doors at night. I didn’t have a choice but to leave.

“Name’s Jordan,” she says, holding out a hand. I shake it.

“Taylor.”

She flips through her sketchbook. “You’re trans?”

I freeze. “How’d you know?”

She shrugs. “You look like you’ve been running from something for a long time. And you don’t wear makeup. That’s a good sign. Most trans people I know do.”

I don’t know what to say. I want to tell her everything — how I came out, how my family reacted, how I felt like I had no one. But I also don’t want to burden her. She seems… different. Not like the people I’ve met before who just want to use me or take advantage of me.

“Do you… trust people?” I ask cautiously.

She laughs, but it doesn’t sound happy. “Not really. But I’ve learned that sometimes you have to.”

I look at her sketchbook again. The drawing is of a girl with too many arms, each one holding something — a key, a book, a broken mirror. “What’s that?”

“Just something I made up. A symbol. You know, for people like me — people who don’t fit into the world.”

I feel something in my chest tighten. “Yeah. I get that.”

We sit in silence for a while, watching the river flow. The sun starts to set, casting long shadows across the pavement. I feel a little safer, sitting here with someone who *gets* it.

“You hungry?” Jordan asks after a while.

“Starving.”

She stands up. “Come on. I know a place.”

I follow her, my feet still aching, but my head feeling a little lighter. She leads me through a narrow alley, past a graffiti-covered wall, and into a small courtyard with a tiny café. It’s tucked away, almost hidden. The lights are dim, the music is low, and there are people — mostly people who look like they don’t belong in the city. Or maybe they do.

“Taylor.”

**Chapter 2: Gone South**

I’ve never been good at tying my shoes. Not really. My hands are always too busy, too nervous, too distracted. But today, it’s not just the shoelaces. It’s everything.

I’m in New Orleans. Two states away from home, two states away from my family, two states away from the thing that made me run. I got out of Tennessee in thirty minutes—ran out the front door, packed a backpack, stole a jacket from my sister’s closet, and hopped in a car with a guy I barely knew who said he’d take me to “the coast” and “a place where people understand.” He dropped me off in the middle of the French Quarter, where I’m now standing in front of a street musician playing a saxophone that sounds like it’s about to fall apart. My shoelace is untied again. I kick at a pebble, and it rolls into a puddle. I don’t even know where I am.

I’m supposed to be somewhere else. Somewhere safe. But I don’t even know what that means anymore. I used to think “safe” meant home. Now I think it means something else entirely—something I don’t have the words for yet.

The first thing I notice about New Orleans is the noise. It’s not just the music. It’s the people, the cars, the chatter, the laughter, the *life* in the air. It’s overwhelming. I feel like I’m walking through a dream. My mom would have called it “vibrant.” My dad would have called it “too loud.” I don’t know what to call it. I just know I’m here, and I’m trying not to cry.

I’m wearing my old hoodie—stained, faded, and slightly too big. My jeans are too short. I look like a kid who’s lost their way. And I *am*. I just don’t know how to find my way back.

I stop at a corner store to buy water and a sandwich. The clerk is a woman in her fifties with a clipboard and a suspicious look. She doesn’t ask questions. That’s a good sign. I hand her a twenty and get change for a dollar. I’m still not used to being treated like a normal person. Not anymore.

As I walk back to the street, I notice someone sitting on a bench nearby. They’re wearing a hoodie too, but it’s black, with a logo I don’t recognize. Their face is hidden beneath a beanie, but I can tell they’re a teenager. Maybe 17. Maybe older. They’re staring at their phone, but not really looking at it. They look like they’re waiting for something—or someone.

I decide to sit down next to them. I don’t know why. Maybe it’s the loneliness. Maybe it’s the fact that I’ve been alone for so long, I’ve forgotten how to be around people. Or maybe I just want someone to talk to, even if it’s a stranger.

“Hey,” I say. It comes out louder than I expected.

The person doesn’t look up immediately. When they do, their eyes are tired, like they’ve seen too much. They take off their beanie, revealing a shaved head and a scar running along the side of their face. They look like they’ve been through hell and back.

“Hey,” they say back. Their voice is low, rough, like they’ve been smoking something they shouldn’t have. “You lost?”

I laugh, but it doesn’t sound like a real laugh. “I think I’m more like… misplaced.”

They smirk. “Well, welcome to New Orleans. We’ve got a lot of misplaced people here.”

I glance at my shoelaces again. “I keep losing them.”

They laugh. “That’s a sign. You’re not from here.”

I don’t know what to say to that. I guess I don’t have to. We sit in silence for a while, just watching the world pass us by. Then they finally speak again.

“I’m Kai,” they say. “You’re… not from around here, either.”

I nod. “Taylor.”

“Nice to meet you, Taylor.” They pause. “You look like you’ve been running.”
