**Chapter Two: The Stranger at the Bar**

The Last Outpost is more than just a bar. It's a lifeline for the few souls who call this asteroid belt home. A place where the stars are distant and the stories are closer. I call it my second chance. Or maybe my first. I’m not sure which. I just know that after everything, this place has taken me in.

The air recyclers hum softly in the background, their whistling sound almost musical. Some people find it annoying. I’ve learned to ignore it. Or at least, I try to. Tonight, though, it feels different—like the air itself is holding its breath.

I’m wiping the counter with a rag, my eyes scanning the room. The usual suspects are here: Jax, the miner who’s always got a story about a rock that looked like a face; Mara, the smuggler who can talk her way out of anything but a full bladder; and old Renny, who insists he’s still a captain, even though he’s been retired for thirty years. They all have their quirks, and I’ve learned to live with them. Mostly.

Then, without warning, the door hisses open.

A figure steps in, their silhouette cutting through the dim light. They’re tall, wrapped in a tattered cloak that smells like ozone and regret. Their hair—what little of it there is—hangs in long, tentacled dreadlocks that are clearly in need of a trim. They look like they’ve been through a war and lost.

I don’t even bother looking up from my rag. “Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double,” they say, their voice low and raspy, like they’ve been shouting for years.

I raise an eyebrow. “You sure about that? That’s a lot of Vega for one person.”

They don’t respond. Just slide into a stool and cross their legs. The tentacles twitch slightly, like they’re trying to remember how to be still.

I pour the whiskey, keeping my eyes on them. “You’re not from around here, are you?”

The stranger takes a slow sip, their fingers curling around the glass. “No. Not anymore.”

I nod, not sure what to say. There’s something about them—something that makes me want to ask questions but also not want to know the answers.

The ventilation system whistles again, louder this time. I glance up. It’s not the usual hum. It sounds almost like a warning.

“Outpost’s got a reputation,” I say, setting the glass down. “You’re not going to find many people who’ll tell you the truth about this place.”

They stare at me for a long moment. “I already know the truth.”

I lean against the bar, crossing my arms. “Then why are you here?”

The question hangs in the air. The room feels smaller, the light dimmer. For a second, I think they might not answer. But then they do, in a voice that’s just a whisper.

“I need to find someone.”

“Someone?” I repeat, skeptical.

They nod. “A woman. Named… Lira.”

My stomach drops. I know that name. I’ve heard it whispered in the dark corners of the bar, in hushed tones. Lira was one of the first people to come to the Outpost. A scientist, or so she claimed. She disappeared a year ago, during one of those rare solar flares that messed with the navigation systems. Some say she died in the crash. Others say she was taken. No one knows for sure.

I swallow hard. “You think she’s still alive?”

The stranger doesn’t answer. They just take another sip of the whiskey, their eyes locked on mine. There’s something in their gaze—something that makes me feel like I’ve stepped into a memory I don’t want to remember.

I clear my throat. “You’re not from around here, and you’re looking for someone who’s been missing for a year. That’s a lot to ask of a barkeep.”

They smirk slightly, though it doesn’t reach their eyes. “You’re not just a barkeep, Nets. You’re a survivor. And you know things. Things that people don’t talk about.”

I hesitate. I’ve always prided myself on keeping my mouth shut. It’s safer that way. But something about this stranger—about the way they look at me, like they know me—makes me want to tell them the truth.

“Lira wasn’t just a scientist,” I say quietly. “She was… she was one of the few people who actually cared about this place. She tried to fix the air recyclers when they started breaking down. She thought we could make it work. But then… the flare. The crash. No one saw her after that.”

The stranger’s tentacles twitch. “You think she’s dead.”

“I think she’s gone,” I say. “And I think if you’re looking for her, you’re not the only one.”

They lean forward, their voice barely above a whisper. “Then I’m not the only one who’s looking.”

**Chapter 1: The Last Outpost**

The Last Outpost is a place where time bends, and stories are served in shots. It's a lazy hub in the middle of an asteroid belt, on the outer rim of Andromeda, where the stars are distant and the silence is broken only by the hum of the air recyclers and the clink of glasses. I call it home, though I’d never admit that aloud. Not to the miners, not to the merchants, not even to the smugglers who occasionally stop by for a drink and a story. They all know better than to ask.

I’m Nets, or Nettle to my friends — though I’ve lost count of how many of them are still around. My past life is a ghost I try to keep in equilibrium with the rest of my existence. It’s a delicate balance, like trying to juggle molten rock. I’ve learned to keep my flaws in check, to let them simmer beneath the surface, and to use my talents — the ones that come naturally, like a knack for reading people or a talent for fixing anything that breaks — to keep the peace.

Tonight’s shift is uneventful, as most of them are. I’m wiping down the bar, the soft whistling of the ventilation system a familiar lullaby. A smuggler in a patched-up jumpsuit is trying to convince a miner that he’s not a thief, just a patriot. A woman with a scar across her cheek is staring into the distance, lost in some memory I don’t want to know about.

Then, the door creaks open. The sound is so loud in this quiet place, it makes the miners jump. A figure steps in — tall, lanky, and unmistakably frazzled. Their hair is a tangled mess of tentacles, dark and unkempt, and their eyes are bloodshot, like they’ve been running for days. They don’t look around. They don’t even glance at the bar. They just walk straight to the counter and slam their hand down.

“Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double,” they say, their voice hoarse and edged with something I can’t place — exhaustion, maybe, or fear.

I raise an eyebrow. “Double? That’s not cheap.”

They blink. “No.”

“Then why the hell are you in the Andromeda outer rim? That’s not exactly a tourist spot.”

A flicker of something crosses their face — maybe guilt, maybe defiance. They don’t answer. Instead, they reach into their jacket and pull out a small, crumpled piece of paper. It’s damp, like it’s been handled a lot. They slide it across the counter.

One stands out: *Dr. Elara Voss.* I know that name. She was a scientist, a xenobiologist, who vanished years ago during a research mission on a rogue planet. Some say she was killed by the planet itself. Others say she ran.

I look up. The stranger is watching me, eyes narrowed. “You know her,” they say, not a question.

I don’t answer. I just stare at the paper, the weight of it pressing against my palm. I’ve tried to forget her. Forgotten the way she used to come in here, always alone, always with that same look in her eyes — searching, lost, like she was waiting for something or someone.

“I don’t know what you want,” I say finally, my voice quieter than I intended.

The stranger leans forward, their tentacles brushing the counter. “I need to find her.”

“Why?”

They hesitate. For a moment, I think they might answer. But then they just shake their head. “I can’t say.”

I sigh. “You’re not the first to come looking for her. You’re not the first to say you need to find her. You’re not even the first to say you’re the one who last saw her.”

Their hands clench. “Then why are you still here?”

I look around the bar. The miners are still arguing, the smuggler is still trying to convince the miner he’s not a thief, and the woman with the scar is still staring into the distance. This place is a haven for people who’ve lost something — or someone.

**Chapter Three: The Stranger at the Bar**

The Last Outpost is a place where time bends and space folds. It's a lazy hub, sure—built around a rusted core that hums like a tired engine, with walls patched together from scavenged metal and old ship parts. The air smells like recycled oxygen and stale beer, and the ventilation system occasionally makes a soft whistling sound when the air recyclers kick in. It's a sound I’ve come to know by heart. I call it the *Outpost Whisper*.

I’m Nets—Nets to my friends, and sometimes to my enemies. I bartend here, mostly because I don’t have anywhere else to go. Escaping from my past life was a mistake, but it was the only one that worked. I’ve learned to keep my flaws in equilibrium with my talents. I can mix drinks that make people forget their problems for a while. I can negotiate the drama, intrigue, problems, and love lives of the diverse crowd that filter through the Outpost like water through a sieve. And I can spot a liar from a mile away—something I’ve had to do a lot of lately.

Tonight is no different. The bar is half full, with miners nursing their wounds, merchants arguing over credits, and a few spacelings who don’t belong here at all. I’m wiping the counter with a rag, humming a tune I’ve heard somewhere—probably from a movie or a song someone tried to play on a synth. I’m not sure which.

Then, without warning, a frazzled looking stranger slides into the bar. Their tentacles are askew, tangled and messy, like they’ve been through a war or a bad hair day. They wear a tattered jumpsuit that looks like it’s seen better days, and their eyes are sharp, scanning the room like they’re looking for something—or someone.

I don’t blink. Not yet.

“Vega whisky, neat,” they say, voice rough, like it’s been sandpapered. “Better make that a double.”

I glance at the bar, then at them. “Double it? That’s a lot for Vega. You sure?”

They don’t answer. Just lean forward, elbows on the counter, fingers twitching slightly. “I need it. And I need it now.”

I nod, turn around, and start pouring. The bottle is old, the label faded. Vega is a cheap whiskey, but it’s got a kick. I’ve seen it get people drunk, angry, and occasionally violent. I’ve also seen it calm people down. It’s a paradox, like life in an asteroid belt.

I pour two fingers into the glass, then double it. The stranger watches, eyes flicking between the glass and me. I slide it over.

They take it without thanks. One long, trembling sip. Then another. Then they set the glass down, hard. The sound echoes in the quiet bar.

“I’ve been looking for this place,” they say, voice low. “For a long time.”

I raise an eyebrow. “You’re not from around here.”

“No,” they say. “I’m not. I’m from… somewhere else. A place that doesn’t exist anymore.”

The words hang in the air like smoke. I don’t say anything. I’ve heard that kind of thing before—people who think they’ve lost something, or someone, or a whole world. I’ve seen them come in, all broken and lost, and leave either changed or gone.

“I came looking for a name,” they continue. “A name that was taken from me. Or maybe I took it. I’m not sure anymore.”

I lean against the counter, arms crossed. “Names are just words. Unless you’re talking about something more.”

They look up at me, eyes wide, almost pleading. “It’s not just a name. It’s a life. A choice. A mistake. A promise.”

I sigh. “You’re not the first person to come in here and talk about mistakes.”

They shake their head. “No. I’m not. But I think I’m the first to come looking for one.”

I study them. They’re not a miner. Not a merchant. Not even a spaceling. They’re something else. Something lost. And I don’t like that feeling. Not in my bar. Not in my life.

“Who are you?” I ask.

The stranger hesitates. “I used to be called Veyra.”

I blink. “Veyra?”

They nod. “Before the fall. Before the crash. Before I lost everything.”

I remember something. A name. A story. One of the old rumors that circulate through the Outpost. Veyra. A scientist. A rebel. Someone who vanished during the Andromeda Incident. A war that was supposed to be over, but wasn’t. People say she was trying to build something—something dangerous. Something that could change everything.

And then she disappeared.

“Did you survive the crash?” I ask.

They don’t answer immediately. Instead, they reach into their jacket and pull out a small, crumpled piece of paper. It’s covered in writing—numbers, symbols, a name. They slide it across the counter to me.

I look at it. It’s a code. A cipher

**Chapter 12: The Last Outpost**

The Last Outpost has always been a place of quiet chaos. A floating bar nestled in the shadow of a dying star, it’s the only thing keeping the miners, smugglers, and dreamers of the Andromeda outer rim from going completely mad. I call it home. Or at least, I call it *my* bar. It’s not much—just a few mismatched tables, a flickering holographic sign that reads *Last Outpost* in a font that looks like it was designed by a drunk AI, and a bar that’s seen more spills than a reactor core.

I’m called Nets by most people. Some call me Nettle. I don’t care. I just want to serve drinks and listen to stories. That’s what I do. Most nights, it’s the same: a miner complaining about a rock that won’t break, a merchant trying to convince me that the price of fuel is going to drop tomorrow, and a love triangle between three people who all think they’re the hero.

Tonight is no different—until the stranger walks in.

He’s not human. Or at least, not entirely. His skin is a mottled blue, like the inside of a squid, and his hair is a tangle of tentacles that dangle down his back, frayed and unkempt. He looks like he’s been through a war and lost. The air around him is heavy, like he’s carrying something that shouldn’t be carried.

He slumps into a stool at the bar, his tentacles curling slightly around the edge. I don’t say anything. I don’t need to. The moment he sits down, the usual hum of the Outpost dims. People stop mid-sentence, eyes flicking toward him. Even the ventilation system seems to pause. It usually whistles softly when the air recyclers kick in, like a lullaby for the lost. But tonight, it’s silent.

I pour him a double of Vega whisky. It’s not cheap, but I don’t charge extra for the look on his face. He takes it in one gulp, then another. His tentacles twitch as if trying to remember how to breathe.

“Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double,” he mutters, as if I hadn’t already done that.

I roll my eyes. “You’re welcome. You’re early.”

He doesn’t respond. Just stares at the glass, his eyes—those glowing yellow things—fixed on something behind me.

I turn. Nothing’s there. Just the usual mess of the bar: a half-finished game of *Dust Wars*, a flickering holoscreen showing a news report about a mine collapse on Gliese 581, and the occasional burst of static from the comms system.

“Everything okay?” I ask, leaning against the bar.

He doesn’t look at me. “You’ve got a problem.”

I blink. “What?”

“Your ventilation system. It’s… off. Or it’s not. Either way, it’s not doing what it should.”

I glance at the vent above the bar. It’s humming normally. The soft whistling sound that always accompanies the air recyclers is absent. I shrug. “It’s just a little slow tonight. Probably a filter clog.”

He snorts. “No. It’s not a clog. It’s a *presence*.”

I cross my arms. “You’re drunk.”

“I’m not drunk. I’m *alert*.”

I don’t know what to say to that. I’ve dealt with weird customers before—aliens, time travelers, even a guy who claimed he was a god. But this one… there’s something off about him. His voice is low, like he’s used to being heard over something. Maybe a ship’s engine. Or a war.

“Look,” I say, trying to keep my tone even, “if you’re looking for a place to sleep, I’ve got a cot in the back. It’s not fancy, but it’s clean. You can pay me tomorrow.”

**Chapter Three: The Stranger at the Bar**

The Last Outpost has always been a place of quiet chaos. A floating bar in the middle of an asteroid belt, where the stars are just a backdrop and the real action is in the drinks, the stories, and the secrets people won’t tell. I call it home, even though I’m not sure I belong here. But the people here—miners, smugglers, traders, ex-soldiers, and the occasional lost soul like me—have tolerated my quirks. My tentacle tattoos, my tendency to forget names, my habit of quoting old Earth philosophers. I keep them in balance, like a bar stool that’s just about to tip.

Tonight, though, something feels off. The ventilation system hums with its usual soft whistling, the kind that reminds me of a lullaby my mother used to sing before she vanished into the void. The lights flicker slightly, casting long shadows across the wooden bar. The usual crowd is here: Jax, the miner with a face like a sandstorm; Lira, the smuggler who never takes off her gloves; and Mira, the ex-soldier who still carries a gun in her boot. But tonight, the air feels heavier. Like something is waiting.

Then he arrives.

A stranger, all in tattered armor that looks like it’s been through a war. His tentacles—long, dark, and frayed—are pulled back into a messy braid, like he tried to tame them but gave up halfway. His eyes are too sharp, too tired. He slides into the barstool without a word, his boots scuffing the floor. I glance at the menu, then back at him.

“Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double,” he says, his voice rough, like he’s been grinding gravel in his throat.

I nod, not sure if he’s joking or not. I pour the drink, trying not to notice the way he keeps glancing at the door, like he’s expecting someone—or something—to walk in.

“First time here?” I ask, wiping the counter with a rag.

He doesn’t answer immediately. He stares into his glass, swirling the amber liquid like it’s something sacred. “No,” he says finally. “But I should have been.”

I raise an eyebrow. “You look like you’ve been through hell.”

He laughs, but it doesn’t sound like a laugh. It’s more like a cough. “I have.”

I set the glass down and lean against the counter, crossing my arms. “What’s your name?”

“Call me Kael,” he says. “Or don’t. Doesn’t matter.”

I don’t like people who don’t want to be known. But I also don’t like people who act like they’re already dead. “You’re not from around here, are you?”

“No,” he says. “I was. Once.”

There’s a pause. The ventilation system whistles again, louder this time. I wonder if it’s just the air recyclers, or if it’s something else.

“What’s your story?” I ask, not sure why I’m asking. Maybe it’s just the way he looks—like he’s carrying something heavy, something that won’t stay put.

He looks up at me, and for a moment, I think I see something behind his eyes—regret, maybe, or fear. “I used to be a captain,” he says. “Of a ship called *The Dawn’s Edge*. We were heading to a mining colony on Andromeda Prime. A routine run. But something went wrong.”

I nod slowly. “And you’re here now?”

He sighs, running a hand through his tentacles. “I wasn’t supposed to survive. But I did. And now I’m stuck in the middle of nowhere, with no one to trust and no one to blame.”

I watch him for a moment. There’s something in his voice—something familiar. Like he’s not just telling a story, but reliving it. “You look like you’ve been through a lot,” I say. “You need someone to listen.”

He looks at me like I’ve just offered him a lifeline. “I don’t know if I can trust anyone.”

“Try me,” I say.

He hesitates, then leans forward. “Alright. But don’t tell anyone. Not even the others.”

I nod. “You got it.”

And then he starts to tell me about the *Dawn’s Edge*, the mining colony, the signal that never came back, and the crash that left him stranded in the asteroid belt. He talks about the crew—some dead, some missing, and one who disappeared without a trace. He talks about the last transmission, a distorted voice saying, “We’re not alone.”

I listen, not interrupting. I’ve heard stories like this before. People who think they’re the only ones who’ve seen things. But something about Kael feels different. There’s a weight to his words, a truth that lingers.

When he finishes, the bar is quiet. The only sound is the hum of the ventilation system, now louder, like it’s trying to drown out something else.

“I’m not crazy,” he says, looking at me. “I know what I saw.”

The Last Outpost hums with the low, steady rhythm of a place that’s seen better days and worse. The air is thick with the scent of recycled oxygen, stale beer, and something that smells like burnt engine grease. The ventilation system occasionally makes a soft whistling sound when the air recyclers kick in—like a tired sigh. I call it the Outpost’s heartbeat. It’s comforting, in its own weird way.

I’m behind the bar, wiping down the counter with a rag that’s seen better days. My name is Nets—though I go by Nettle to most people. It’s shorter, and somehow less likely to get me killed. I’ve got a few scars, a few secrets, and a list of people who’ve tried to get me to leave. They all failed. For now.

The bar is mostly empty, save for a couple of miners nursing lukewarm drinks and a spacer who’s arguing with a droid about the merits of moss-based fuel. I’m in my element. I like knowing everyone’s story, even if I don’t ask for them. I listen. That’s how I keep the peace.

Then, the stranger comes in.

He doesn’t announce himself. He just *plops* down at the bar like he owns the place. Or like he’s been waiting for it all his life. He doesn’t respond. He just stares at me, like he’s assessing whether I’m worth the trouble. I’ve dealt with weird before. This isn’t new. But something about him feels off. Like he’s not just *strange*—he’s *important*.

I pour the whisky, double it, and slide it across. He doesn’t touch it. Instead, he reaches into his coat and pulls out a small, crystalline object. It’s shaped like a key, but it glows faintly, like it’s made of starlight.

“Where’s the captain?” he asks, not looking up.

I freeze. My hands still on the glass. “No one’s a captain here.”

He finally looks at me. His eyes are too sharp, too knowing. “You’re lying.”

I take a breath. “I don’t know what you’re talking about.”

He leans forward, his tentacles twitching. “You know exactly what I’m talking about. The Outpost isn’t just a bar. It’s a *station*. And it’s supposed to be *abandoned*.”

I step back. “You’re drunk. Or high. Or both.”

He laughs, a dry, hollow sound. “I’ve been through worse.”

“I’m not a captain,” I say, trying to keep my voice steady. “I’m just… a bartender. I’ve been here for a year. I know what’s going on.”

My heart drops. I blink. “What?”

He leans in closer. “You don’t remember, do you? You were *supposed* to be dead. You were part of the last crew. The one that got lost in the asteroid field. The one that *never* made it through.”

I feel the floor tilt beneath me. My hands shake. “That… that wasn’t me.”

He sighs. “It was. You were the engineer. The one who tried to fix the systems. But the core failed. The station collapsed. You were buried under debris. You *should* be dead.”

I stumble back, knocking over a stool. “I… I don’t remember any of this.”

He watches me, unreadable. “You’ll remember. The memories come back when you’re ready. Or when someone *forces* them.”

A beat of silence. The ventilation system whistles again, like it’s trying to drown out the tension.

“Who are you?” I manage to ask, voice barely above a whisper.

**Chapter Three: The Stranger at the Bar**

The Last Outpost is a place where time forgets how to tick. The walls are lined with rusted metal and flickering neon signs that read things like *“No Trespassing”* and *“Drinks on the House for the Lost.”* The air smells like stale beer, motor oil, and something faintly sweet—like burnt sugar. It’s the kind of place that smells like home, even if you’ve never been anywhere else. I call it *home* now, even though I still trace the scar on my thumb when I think too hard about the past.

I’m called Nets by most people, though some still call me *Nettle*—probably because I was born in a place where the wind was too cold and the ground was too hard. I don’t care what they call me. I just care about the drinks, the stories, and the way the stars outside the window shimmer like distant memories.

Tonight’s crowd is mostly miners with soot on their faces and merchants who’ve lost more than they can afford to admit. There’s a man arguing with his holographic wallet, a woman trying to convince a robot to buy her a drink, and a couple of kids who’ve already had too much of the wrong stuff. I lean against the bar, my eyes scanning the room as I wipe down the counter. The scar on my thumb itches, so I rub it absently, like it’s a habit I can’t break.

That’s when he walks in.

He’s not the kind of person who *walks* in. He *plummets* in, like he’s been thrown through a portal and landed in the wrong universe. His tentacles are disheveled, like he’s been fighting gravity for hours. His clothes are a patchwork of different fabrics—some of them too clean, others too stained. He looks like he’s been through a war, and maybe a few other things too.

He doesn’t look at anyone. He just walks straight to the bar, eyes fixed on the counter. I don’t need to ask who he is. I already know he’s trouble.

“Vega whisky,” he says, voice rough like gravel. “Better make that a double.”

I raise an eyebrow. “You sure you’re not a miner? That sounds like a *triple*.”

He doesn’t respond. He just slumps into a stool, his tentacles curling awkwardly around the bar. I pour the whisky, watching the amber liquid slide into the glass. It’s a rare bottle, one I bought from a smuggler who swore it was from a dead planet. I don’t believe him, but I like the way it burns when I drink it.

“Your tentacles are messy,” I say, sliding the glass toward him. “Need a towel?”

He doesn’t look up. “I’ve had worse.”

I smirk. “That’s the spirit. Or is it just a broken spirit?”

He finally looks at me, and for a moment, I think I see something in his eyes—something like regret. Or maybe fear. “You’re not from around here, are you?”

“No,” I say, leaning back. “I’m from somewhere that didn’t have stars like this. Just a lot of concrete and smoke.”

He takes a slow sip of the whisky, his tentacles twitching slightly. “I’ve seen stars like this before. Too many.”

I watch him. He’s not the kind of person who talks much, but when he does, it’s like he’s trying to remember something he’s forgotten. Or maybe something he’s afraid to remember.

“Where are you from?” I ask, not because I care, but because I need something to do.

He hesitates. “A place called Eos Prime.”

I blink. “That’s not a real planet.”

“It was.”

I study him. There’s something in his voice, a quiet certainty that makes me uneasy. “You’re not from the Andromeda outer rim.”

“No,” he says. “I used to be.”

I cross my arms. “So what are you doing here?”

He looks away, toward the window where the stars are blindingly bright. “I was trying to find something. Or someone. I don’t know which.”

I shrug. “Well, if you’re looking for someone, you’re not going to find them here. This place is full of lost people.”

He nods. “I know.”

I lean forward. “Then what are you looking for?”

He doesn’t answer right away. The silence between us is heavy, like it’s holding something back. I trace the scar on my thumb again, the habit returning without thought.

“I was looking for a way out,” he says finally. “A way to escape what I left behind.”

I raise an eyebrow. “And now you’re here, looking for something else?”

He looks at me for the first time, really looks at me. “Maybe I’m looking for the same thing.”

I consider this. It’s a strange thing to hear from a man with tentacles and a drink in his hand. “You know,” I say, “this place isn’t the best place to find what you’re looking for. People come here because they’ve already lost everything.”

He drinks

**Chapter Three: The Stranger at the Bar**

I tap the glass, watching the amber liquid swirl in the double rocks glass. The bar is quiet—too quiet. Usually, there’s a steady hum of conversation, the clink of glasses, the low murmur of deals being struck over stale bread and lukewarm coffee. But tonight, the air feels thick with something else. Maybe it’s the way the light flickers from the overhead bulbs, or the way the dust motes dance in the air like tiny ghosts. Or maybe it’s just me, still trying to outrun the ghosts of my own past.

I trace the scar on my thumb, the one that looks like a crescent moon, and I lean back against the bar counter. The Outpost is a lazy hub, sure, but it’s not lazy enough to let me forget that I was once someone else. Someone who didn’t know how to stop running.

A new patron slides into the seat across from me. He’s not the kind you see every day. His tentacles—long, dark, and frayed—dangle down his back like a curtain that’s seen better days. His dreads are askew, and his eyes are too bright, like they’ve been through too much and are still trying to figure out if they’re worth it. He orders a double Vega whisky, neat, and doesn’t look up as he says it. I’ve seen people order drinks with more conviction than that.

“Double,” I repeat, nodding. “Coming right up.”

He doesn’t respond. Just stares at the counter, fingers twitching. I don’t like that look. Not the kind of quiet that means someone’s about to say something important, or something dangerous.

I pour the whiskey, trying to keep my hands steady. It’s not easy. My hands have always been too fast, too restless. They used to do things I didn’t even want to remember. Now they just want to hold a glass, or a gun, or maybe just a damn drink.

“I don’t usually come here,” he says finally, voice low, rough like sandpaper. “But I needed a place to sit. And a drink.”

I slide the glass toward him. “You’re not from around here.”

He takes a slow sip, eyes closed. “No. Not anymore.”

I watch him. He’s not the kind of man who walks into a bar and just sits down. There’s something about him—something that says he’s been chased, or he’s chasing someone. Or maybe both.

“Why the tentacles?” I ask, more out of habit than curiosity. I’ve learned that people don’t always answer questions about themselves. But sometimes they do.

He opens his eyes, and for a moment, I think I see something behind them—something ancient, something lost. “They’re not tentacles,” he says. “They’re roots. Or maybe something else. I don’t know what they are anymore.”

I blink. “Roots?”

He nods. “I used to be something else. A tree. Or maybe a creature. I don’t remember. All I remember is being pulled apart, and then put back together. Not the same.”

I laugh, but it’s a dry, hollow sound. “That’s the kind of story that gets people killed.”

He looks at me then, really looks at me. “You sound like someone who knows.”

I hesitate. I don’t like being read like a book. Not after everything. “I used to be someone else too.”

He leans forward, resting his elbows on the bar. “Then you’ll understand.”

I cross my arms. “I understand people who run. I don’t understand people who get pulled apart.”

He exhales, slow and steady. “Maybe we’re not so different.”

I don’t respond. Not yet. I’ve learned that sometimes, the best thing you can do is listen. Especially when the person sitting across from you has a drink that’s half-empty and a story that doesn’t end well.

The bar door creaks open, and a woman walks in. She’s got a rifle slung over her shoulder, her boots dusty and her eyes sharp. She stops at the counter, scans the room, and locks eyes with me.

“Nets,” she says, voice low. “I need to talk to you.”

I glance at the stranger. He doesn’t move. Doesn’t look up. Just stares at his drink.

“I’ll be right back,” I tell him.

I walk over to the woman, keeping my voice even. “What’s going on?”

She doesn’t answer immediately. She just watches me, waiting. “There’s a ship on the way in. From the Veil. A transport. It’s carrying something. Something that doesn’t belong here.”

I feel my stomach drop. “You think it’s dangerous?”

She nods. “I think it’s not just dangerous. I think it’s important. And if it gets here, it could change everything.”

I glance back at the stranger, who’s still staring at his drink. “You know about this?”

She shakes her head. “I don’t know who it is. But I know what it is. And I know it’s not supposed to be here.”

I look back at her, weighing my options. The Outpost

Usually, the asteroid belt’s miners and merchants are either too busy or too drunk to bother with a place like this. But tonight, the crowd’s restless. Eyes dart around like they’re expecting something—*anything*—to go wrong.

I’ve got a scar on my thumb, one I trace absently as I work. It’s a thin line, barely noticeable, but it reminds me of the day I left my old life behind. I don’t talk about it much. Not here. Not with these people. They’ve tolerated my flaws—my sharp tongue, my habit of cutting people off mid-sentence, my tendency to fix things that don’t need fixing. But they’ve also accepted me. And that’s enough.

“Another round,” someone calls from the corner. A miner with a busted lip and a bottle of something amber in his hand. I nod, grabbing a glass and pouring a shot of Vega whisky—neat, as he requested. I toss it over, and he chugs it down like it’s a lifeline.

I glance up, scanning the room. The usual suspects: Kira, the smuggler who always wears too much eyeliner; Rook, the ex-military guy who still thinks he’s in a war; and Lira, the mechanic who’s convinced the universe is a giant malfunction. They all have their own dramas, their own secrets. And I’ve learned to listen. To hear what they don’t say out loud. To find the cracks in their stories.

That’s when I see him.

A stranger, slumped against the bar, one arm slung over the counter. His tentacles are a mess—some tangled, some hanging loose, like they’ve been through a fight or a storm. His skin is pale, almost translucent, and his eyes are too bright, too alert. He looks like he’s been running for days.

“Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double,” he mutters, not even looking up. His voice is rough, like sandpaper.

I freeze for a second. Tentacles. In an asteroid belt. That’s not normal. Not here. Not in this part of the galaxy.

“Sure,” I say, trying to keep my voice steady. “But you’re going to need to tell me your name first.”

He looks up then, and I catch the glint of something in his eyes—something sharp, something dangerous. “Name’s Dain,” he says. “And I don’t take kindly to being asked for my name.”

I raise an eyebrow. “Well, Dain, I take kindly to people who don’t start fights. So, either order the whisky or get out.”

He sighs, like I’ve asked him to do something impossible. “I’m not here to fight.”

“Then what are you here for?”

He hesitates. For a moment, I think he might actually answer. But then he leans back, his tentacles twitching slightly. “I need a place to think.”

I blink. “A place to think? That’s not exactly a reason to come to The Last Outpost.”

“No,” he says, almost laughing. “It’s not.”

I lean against the counter, crossing my arms. “You know, if you’re looking for someone, I can help. I’ve got a pretty good ear for stories. And I listen.”

He studies me for a moment, like he’s trying to decide whether I’m worth the effort. Then he nods. “You listen,” he says. “But do you understand?”

“Most of the time,” I say. “But I’m not sure I understand *this*.”

He reaches into his coat and pulls out a small, metallic device—something like a key, but too intricate. He slides it across the counter to me. “This is a key. Not to a door, not to a vault. To something else.”

I pick it up, running my thumb over the cold metal. It’s smooth, but there’s something strange about it. It doesn’t feel like anything I’ve ever seen.

“What is it?” I ask.

He doesn’t answer. Instead, he takes a long drink of his whisky, then leans forward. “I came here because I thought I could find peace. But I didn’t. I found something else. Something I don’t want to remember.”

I watch him closely. There’s something wrong here. Something deeper than just a stranger with tentacles. “You’re not from around here, are you?”

“No,” he says. “I’m not.”

I glance around the bar. The others are still drinking, too wrapped up in their own issues to notice anything unusual. But I can feel it now—something in the air, like a storm building. Like something is about to break.

“Dain,” I say, trying to keep my voice calm. “I don’t know who you are or what you’re looking

**Chapter 1: The Stranger at the Bar**

I’m Nets, or Nettle, depending on who you ask. I bartend at *The Last Outpost*, a place that’s more like a halfway house for space drifters, miners with too many stories, and merchants who’ve lost their way. It’s a lazy hub in the Andromeda outer rim, where the asteroid belt hums like a tired engine and the stars are too far away to feel real. I escaped my past life here, and for the most part, they’ve tolerated my flaws—my temper, my habit of talking to the walls, and that scar on my thumb that I absently trace when I’m thinking.

Tonight’s no different. The bar is mostly empty, save for a few regulars nursing their drinks and arguing over who owes whom money. The air smells like stale ale and old leather. I’m wiping down the counter when a frazzled looking stranger slinks into the booth across from me. Their tentacles are all askew, like they’ve been through a war and lost the fight. Their eyes are sharp, though—too sharp. They don’t look like they belong here.

“Vega whisky, neat,” they say, voice low and gravelly. “Better make that a double.”

I glance at the bottle behind the bar. Vega’s a rare import, mostly sold to the richer types who think they’re too good for the Outpost. I slide the double over, and the stranger takes it without a word. They lift the glass to their lips, and I notice their tentacles twitch slightly as they sip. Not from the drink, I think. From something else.

I lean against the bar, arms crossed. “You’re not from around here, are you?”

They don’t answer right away. Instead, they set the glass down with a quiet *clink* and look me straight in the eye. “I’m not. And I don’t suppose you are either.”

I smirk. “Not exactly. But I’ve been here long enough to know what’s what.”

They sigh, running a tentacle through their hair, which is a tangled mess of dark, gelatinous strands. “You’re not wrong. But I don’t think you’re *supposed* to be here.”

I arch an eyebrow. “What’s that supposed to mean?”

They hesitate, then lean in just enough that their tentacles brush against the counter. “I’ve seen people like you before. People who run from things. And people who think they’ve found peace. But peace doesn’t last in places like this.”

I feel my jaw tighten. “You don’t know me.”

“No,” they say, “but I know the type.”

I pull my thumb out of my pocket and trace the scar again. It’s a thin, pale line across the pad, a reminder of something I’d rather forget. “What’s it to you?”

The stranger’s eyes flicker, like they’re seeing something I can’t. “I’m looking for someone. Someone who might still be here.”

I laugh, a dry, bitter sound. “You’re looking for a ghost.”

They don’t flinch. “Maybe I am. But I don’t think they’re gone.”

I cross my arms again. “You’re wasting your time. This place is full of drifters, lost souls, and people who’ve given up on the universe. If someone’s gone, they’re gone.”

The stranger stares at me for a long moment. Then they reach into their coat and pull out a small, tarnished locket. It’s made of something that looks like polished obsidian, with a faint glow inside. They open it carefully, revealing a tiny, faded photograph.

It’s of two people—me, I think. One of them is me, standing in front of a ship, looking like I do now—tired, but not broken. The other is a woman with long, auburn hair and a smile that doesn’t quite reach her eyes. She’s wearing a flight jacket, and there’s something familiar about her stance, like she’s waiting for something.

I freeze.

“You know her,” the stranger says, voice barely above a whisper.

I don’t answer. My fingers curl into my palm, the scar digging into my skin. I’ve never seen that photo before. I’ve never seen *her* before.

The stranger leans closer. “She was here once. A few years ago. She said she was looking for someone too. Said they’d left before the war started. Said they’d left *her*.”

I shake my head. “That’s not possible.”

“It is,” they say. “And now I’m here, and I think I’ve found them.”

I stand up, my voice low and dangerous. “You don’t know what you’re talking about.”

The stranger meets my gaze. “I know enough to know that if you’re here, you’re not the one who left.”

I open my mouth to argue, but nothing comes out. My thumb traces the scar again, slower this time. The photo feels like a punch to the gut.

The bar falls quiet. The regulars stop arguing. Even the music slows, like the universe is holding its breath.

I finally say, “Who are you?”

The stranger’s tentacles twitch. “

**Chapter Three: The Stranger at the Bar**

I lean back in my stool, the worn leather creaking like a tired friend. The hum of the Outpost’s low-wattage lights and the clink of glasses is familiar, comforting. The air smells like stale beer, sweat, and something faintly metallic—probably the remnants of last night’s fight between the mining engineer and the smuggler who kept trying to sell her a “legendary” bottle of synth-ale.

I trace the scar on my thumb, a thin line that runs from the base of my nail to the first knuckle. It’s from a fight I had with a mercenary years ago, back when I still believed in things like honor and revenge. Now, it’s just a reminder that I’ve learned to live with my flaws. Or at least, I try to.

The bar is quiet tonight, which is unusual. Most nights, there’s at least a dozen voices overlapping—arguments, laughter, the occasional sob. But tonight, it’s just me and the occasional glance from the miners who’ve already had too much. The only real life here is the one I create with my words, my stories, my ability to read people like a book.

Then, without warning, the door slams open. A gust of cold air and something else—something acrid, almost like burnt ozone—rushes in. I glance up, expecting another drunk miner, or maybe one of the regulars with a new story to tell. But instead, there’s a figure slumped against the bar, one hand gripping the edge like it’s the only thing holding them together.

They’re tall, their body draped in a tattered cloak that’s too big, and their hair is a tangled mess of tentacles—dark, curling, and frayed. A few of them are loose from their scalp, hanging like dead vines. Their face is obscured by a hood, but I can see their eyes: pale, luminous, and full of something I can’t quite name—fear, maybe, or exhaustion.

“Vega whisky, neat,” they say, voice rough, like they’ve been shouting for hours. “Better make that a double.”

I blink. “Double?”

They nod, their head tilting slightly, as if they’re not entirely sure I heard them.

I glance at the ledger behind the bar, then at the shelves. Vega is rare, especially in a place like this. It’s supposed to be a drink reserved for those who can afford the import taxes. But I’ve seen it before—once, in a bar on a moon that had been bombed into the ground. The last survivor had drunk it like it was the last thing in the universe.

I pull two glasses from the rack, and start pouring. “You sure about the double?”

The stranger doesn’t answer. They just stare at the glass, their tentacles twitching slightly as if trying to hold on to something.

I set the glass down, and they reach out—slow, hesitant—as if afraid of what they might find. Their fingers are calloused, their skin pale and almost translucent. When they touch the glass, I feel a faint shock, like static, run up my arm.

“Name’s Vega,” they say, almost to themselves. “Or at least, I used to be.”

I raise an eyebrow. “You’re not from here.”

“No,” they say, their voice cracking. “I was.”

I watch them closely. There’s something off about them—something that doesn’t quite fit. The way they move, the way they speak. It’s like they’re not entirely present. Like they’re half-remembering something, or maybe trying to forget.

“Why are you here?” I ask, more out of habit than anything else.

They pause, their tentacles curling inward. “I needed a place to stop.”

“That’s not an answer.”

“I don’t have one,” they say, lifting the glass to their lips. The whisky burns as it goes down, and for a moment, they look like they’re remembering something painful. “I was part of something. A mission. A group. We were supposed to find something—something important.”

I lean forward, intrigued. “What did you find?”

They stare into the glass, their eyes unfocused. “I found too much.”

I don’t know why I ask, but I do. “Too much what?”

They shake their head, their tentacles trembling. “It doesn’t matter. I should’ve stayed away. I should’ve never come back.”

The silence between us stretches. I’ve heard this before—people who think they’ve escaped their past, only to find it waiting with open arms. I’ve seen it in the eyes of the smugglers, the ex-military, the ones who thought they could leave their old lives behind.

But this is different.

“Why come back here?” I ask again, softer this time.

The stranger lets out a dry laugh, almost bitter. “Because this is the last place I ever wanted to be.”

I don’t know what to say. I’ve never been good at comforting people. I’ve been good at listening, at offering a drink, at helping them sort through their problems—most of the time. But tonight, I feel like I’m missing something.

records.response_text:
**Chapter Three: The Stranger at the Bar**

The Last Outpost is more than just a bar. It’s a crossroads. A place where miners come to rest their aching bones, merchants haggle over stolen cargo, and exiles like me find a strange kind of peace. I call it home. Or at least, I used to.  

Tonight, the air smells like stale beer and burnt circuitry. The hum of the neon sign above the door flickers, casting long shadows across the cracked wooden floor. I’m wiping a glass with a rag I’ve been using since last Tuesday, when the door creaks open and in walks someone who doesn’t belong.

They’re tall, maybe a head taller than me, and their hair—what little there is of it—is a tangled mass of tentacles that look like they’ve been through a war. They’re dressed in a patched-up jumpsuit that’s clearly been through worse. Their eyes are tired, hollow, and they slump into the barstool like it’s the only thing holding them upright.

I glance at the scar on my thumb. It’s a thin line, barely noticeable, but it’s been with me since I was twelve. I trace it absently as I slide over a glass.

“Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double,” they say, their voice rough like gravel.  

I nod, already reaching for the bottle. “Double it is.”  

The glass is filled quickly, and I set it down with a clink. They don’t thank me. They don’t even look up. Instead, they stare at the condensation forming on the glass, like it holds some secret only they can see.

“Name’s Kael,” they finally say, lifting the glass to their lips. “Used to be a pilot. Now I’m just… here.”

I give them a flat look. “And you think this place is going to save you?”

Kael doesn’t answer right away. They take a slow sip, then set the glass down with more force than necessary. “I think I need to find someone. Someone who knows what it’s like to lose everything.”

I smirk. “You’re not the first to come in here looking for that.”

They look up then, finally meeting my eyes. “You’ve been here a while, haven’t you?”

I shrug. “Long enough to know that people come in here with stories. Most of them leave with more.”

Kael leans forward, their tentacles twitching slightly. “I’ve got a story. And it’s not one I want to tell to just anyone.”

I cross my arms. “So you come in here, order a drink, and expect me to listen?”

“I expect you to listen,” they say, voice steady now. “Because I think you know what it’s like to lose something. To be stuck in a place that’s supposed to be your refuge, but isn’t.”

I freeze. The scar on my thumb digs into my skin. I don’t usually talk about that. Not here. Not with strangers.

“I lost my brother,” I say quietly. “A year ago. He was on a mining ship. Crashed. No survivors. I was supposed to be there. I wasn’t.”

Kael’s eyes narrow. “You were?”

I nod. “I was. And I didn’t make it. I barely made it. I ended up here. The Outpost. It was the only place I could think of that wasn’t a graveyard.”

Kael leans back, their tentacles curling around the bar. “So you think you’re safe here? That you’re not still running from something?”

I open my mouth to argue, but I can’t. Because I don’t know how to explain that I’m still running. That I still dream about the ship, the explosion, the way the stars looked like they were burning through my skin.

“I don’t know,” I say finally. “Maybe I’m not. But I’ve got this bar. I’ve got these people. I’ve got this life. And I’m trying to hold it together.”

Kael studies me for a long moment. Then they reach into their jacket and pull out a small, cracked device. It looks like a communicator, but it’s dented and old, like it’s been through a lot.

“Do you know what this is?” they ask.

I shake my head.

“It’s a beacon. Or it was. Used to be used by a group of pilots. We called ourselves the Void Watchers. We kept an eye on the asteroid belt, helped people navigate the dangers. But something happened. A raid. A whole ship was taken. All of us. No survivors.”

I feel my chest tighten. “You’re saying you were part of that?”

Kael nods. “I was. And I think someone took that ship. And I think they’re still out there. And I think they’re looking for someone.”

I blink. “Looking for who?”

Kael’s tentacles twitch. “For me. Or for the person who ran. The one who didn’t make it.”

I feel the air in the bar shift. Like something has changed. Like the quiet hum of the Outpost has suddenly become too loud.

“You think I’m the one they’re looking for?” I ask,

**Chapter 4: The Stranger at the Bar**

I’ve been bartending at *The Last Outpost* for three years now. Three years of listening to miners argue over who found the richest ore deposit, merchants haggle over credits like they’re worth more than they are, and lovers bicker over whether they’re in love or just bored. It’s a lazy hub in the Andromeda outer rim, surrounded by floating rock and the occasional derelict ship that’s long since given up the ghost. I’ve learned to read people—what they’re hiding, what they want, and when they’re about to blow something up.

I’ve also learned to keep my flaws in equilibrium. I’m stubborn, sarcastic, and have a tendency to talk too much when I’m nervous. But I’ve got a sharp eye, a quick hand, and a knack for knowing when someone’s lying. That last one is mostly luck, I think.

Tonight, the crowd is lighter than usual. I slide the glass over. “Here you go.”

“No,” they say, shaking their head. “I think you were there when I had a chance to escape. You didn’t stop me. You just… let me go.”

**Chapter Three: The Stranger in the Dreads**

I run my thumb over the scar, tracing the jagged line that cuts through the pad like a bad memory. It’s been years since I got it—some mining accident, some bar fight I don’t remember clearly anymore. But it’s always there, a reminder that I’ve survived more than I can name. That’s why I’m here, at *The Last Outpost*, where the stars are distant and the stories are closer.

The bar is quiet tonight. Not the kind of quiet that means everyone’s sleeping or off-world, but the kind that feels like something’s *waiting*. The hum of the neon sign overhead flickers, casting long shadows across the worn wooden tables. Patrons are scattered—Miners nursing bruises, merchants arguing over credits, a couple of kids playing a noisy game of dice with a laser pointer. I don’t mind the noise. It’s the rhythm of the place, the pulse of survival in the Andromeda outer rim.

I lean against the bar, wiping a glass with a rag, when the door swings open. The sound is soft, like a sigh. A man steps in, all limbs and shadows. His tentacles—long, dark, and slightly frayed—drape over his shoulders like a cloak. “Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double,” he says, voice low, gravelly, like he’s been grinding his teeth for years.

I nod, already reaching for the bottle. “Sure thing. That’s a strong one.”

He doesn’t react. Doesn’t even blink. I pour the drink, careful with the glass, and slide it toward him. He takes it without looking up, fingers curled around the stem like it’s a lifeline.

“Name’s Nets,” I say, leaning forward. “You’re new around here, aren’t you?”

He finally looks up, and I catch a flicker of something in his eyes—pain, maybe. Or regret. “Not really,” he says. “Just passing through.”

I smirk. “Passing through? You look like you’ve been passing through for a long time.”

He doesn’t answer. Just stares at his drink. I watch him for a second, then shrug and go back to cleaning the counter. I don’t like people who don’t talk. They’re either dangerous or hiding something. And I’ve learned to be careful with both.

After a few minutes, I glance over again. He’s still there, staring at the same spot on the counter. His tentacles are twitching, like they’re trying to remember something they don’t want to.

“Hey,” I say, softer this time. “You okay?”

He doesn’t answer right away. When he does, his voice is barely above a whisper. “I’m not sure.”

I frown. That’s not an answer. It’s a question. And I hate when people ask questions I can’t answer.

I lean back, arms crossed. “And now you’re here. In the middle of nowhere. With a drink and a story.”

He exhales sharply. “I thought I’d be dead by now.”

**Chapter Three: The Ring on the Bar**

The Last Outpost is a place where time stretches and contracts like the dust in the air. It's a hub for miners, smugglers, and dreamers who’ve wandered too far from Earth and never found their way back. I call it home, or at least I try to. My name’s Nets, or Nets to my friends—though I’m not sure any of them really call me that anymore. I bartend here, keep the peace, and listen to the stories of people who’ve seen more of the galaxy than most will ever know.

Tonight’s crowd is quiet, the usual mix of dented boots and weary eyes. A few miners are nursing their drinks, trying to forget the day’s haul. A couple of traders argue over the price of a crate of synth-groats. And there’s always someone with a secret, a story, or a problem that needs solving. I’ve learned to read them all. But tonight, something feels different.

A frazzled looking stranger slinks into the bar. Their tentacles—long, dark, and tangled—dangle loosely from their head, like they’ve been through a war and lost the fight. Their eyes are sharp, though, scanning the room with a kind of calculated suspicion. I don’t recognize them. That’s rare. Most folks around here have seen me barter, flirt, or at least flinch at one point or another.

I wipe the counter with a rag, not bothering to look up. “Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double,” they say, their voice low and gravelly, like it’s been sandpapered by the void.

I nod, already mixing. Vega is a fine spirit, though it’s not exactly the cheapest. I pour it with care, watching the amber liquid swirl in the glass. When I slide it over, the stranger doesn’t thank me. They just take it, their fingers brushing against the glass like it’s something sacred.

“Ring,” they mutter, nodding at the bar. A faint, glowing ring has formed around the spot where the glass touched the wood. It looks like a halo, or maybe a fingerprint that’s been fused with the surface. I glance down, frowning. I’ve never seen anything like it before.

I reach for the rag again, but they stop me with a hand—tentacle?—that’s surprisingly steady. “Don’t touch it,” they say. “It’s not just a mark. It’s a key.”

I raise an eyebrow. “A key to what?”

They don’t answer right away. Instead, they take a slow sip of their drink, eyes drifting to the ceiling, to the old, flickering lights that hum like a dying star. “You don’t ask questions, do you?”

I shrug. “Only the ones that matter.”

They laugh, but it doesn’t sound like a real laugh. It’s more like a sound of someone who’s lost something—or someone—and doesn’t know how to find it again. “You’re not from around here,” they say, finally. “Not really. You’ve got that look. I don’t deny it. “And you? You look like you’ve been through a black hole and came out the other side.”

They smirk. “Something like that.”

And I followed it.”

I roll my eyes. “So you’re some kind of space tourist with a conspiracy theory?”

“No,” they say, and their voice drops to a near whisper. “I’m a seeker. I used to be part of a crew that explored the edges of the galaxy. We were looking for something—something that could change everything. But we got lost. Not in space, but in time. We ended up… somewhere else. Or maybe somewhere we were always meant to be.”

I blink. “You’re saying you time-traveled?”

I study them for a second. There’s something in their eyes—something that doesn’t belong here. “And this ring? What does it do?”

records.response_text:
**Chapter 1: The Ring on the Bar**

The Last Outpost isn’t much to look at—just a weathered hulk of metal and glass, clinging to the edge of an asteroid belt like a stubborn thought. But inside, it’s a haven. A place where stories are traded like currency, and the air smells like whiskey, sweat, and desperation. I call it home, or at least I try to. 

I’m Nets—short for Nettle, though I prefer the nickname. My name’s a little too sharp for this place. My past is even sharper. I left it behind when I realized I was better off in the quiet chaos of an asteroid belt, surrounded by people who’ve seen more of the universe than most humans ever will. 

Tonight’s crowd is no different from any other. A miner in a patched-up jumpsuit mutters about a broken thruster. A merchant with a mechanical eye fiddles with a holographic ledger. A young woman in a too-tight jumpsuit sighs heavily, nursing a drink and trying not to cry. I lean against the bar, keeping an eye on them all, ready to offer a listening ear, a bit of advice, or maybe a little trouble.

That’s when he walks in.

He doesn’t *walk* so much as drift, like he’s half-attached to the gravity of the station. His skin is a deep, iridescent blue, and his hair—what little of it there is—looks like tentacles, messy and unkempt. They drape over his shoulders like wet seaweed. His eyes are too big, too bright, and they scan the room with something between exhaustion and calculation.

He doesn’t look like a miner. Not really. He doesn’t look like a merchant, either. He looks like someone who’s been running from something for a long time.

He slides into the barstool without a word, his fingers twitching slightly as he reaches for the bottle of Vega whisky behind the counter. I don’t say anything. I’ve learned that sometimes, silence is the best conversation.

“Vega whisky, neat,” he says, his voice low and gravelly, like he’s been using his throat as a sandpaper. “Better make that a double.”

I glance at the bottle. It’s not a standard size. “Double?” I ask, already reaching for the glass. “That’ll be twenty credits.”

He doesn’t blink. “I’ve got credits.”

I roll my eyes. “Of course you do.” I pour the drink, watching as the liquid slides into the glass with a slight shimmer. “You look like you’ve seen a few things.”

He doesn’t answer. He just stares at the glass, as if it might tell him something he’s forgotten. The drink is poured, and he takes it without a thank you. He lifts it to his lips, swallows, and then—*clinks*—the glass leaves a ring-shaped mark on the bar. A perfect circle, like someone pressed a coin into the wood. 

I frown. “That’s a new one.”

He doesn’t look up. “It’s not a coin.”

I lean in, curious now. “Then what is it?”

He finally meets my eyes, and for the first time, I see something behind that tired look—something sharp, something dangerous. “A warning,” he says. “Or a reminder. I’m not sure which.”

I cross my arms. “You’re not exactly the first stranger to come in here with a story. What’s the deal with the ring?”

He sighs, and for a moment, he looks like a man who’s been carrying a weight for years. “I used to be a scientist. Or I was. Before I became a fugitive. Before I started running.”

I blink. “A scientist? What kind of science?”

He hesitates, like the question is a loaded weapon. “I worked on something… experimental. A project called *Echelon*.” He pauses. “It wasn’t supposed to work that way.”

I raise an eyebrow. “Echelon? Sounds like a name for a government program.”

He laughs, but it’s dry. “It was. Sort of. We were trying to create a way to communicate across dimensions. Or at least, across the fabric of space-time. It was… ambitious.”

I lean back, intrigued. “So what happened?”

He takes another sip of his drink, the ring on the bar still glowing faintly. “It didn’t just *work* the way we expected. It opened a door. Not a door, really. A *window*. And it didn’t close.”

I frown. “You mean it opened a portal?”

He nods slowly. “Not just a portal. A *connection*. And it wasn’t one-way.”

I blink. “So… you’re stuck somewhere else?”

He shakes his head. “No. I *was* stuck. For a while. But I found a way out. Or maybe I was *found*.”

I lean forward, intrigued. “Who found you?”

He looks away, his tentacles twitching slightly. “A group. A cult. Or a cult of scientists. They believed in what I was doing. They believed in *Echelon*. They thought I was a prophet.”

**Chapter 3: The Ring on the Bar**

The Last Outpost has always been a place of quiet chaos. It’s the kind of place where the air smells like burnt coffee and old regrets, and the walls are lined with bottles that haven’t seen the light of day since the last shipment from Earth. I call it home, even if I still remember the way the walls of my old apartment used to hum with electricity and the smell of something more like life.

Tonight, the usual crowd is here—miners with calloused hands and sunken eyes, merchants haggling over credits and secrets, and a few lovebirds who’ve clearly just had an argument and are trying to act like they’re not. I’m leaning against the bar, polishing a glass with the kind of care I only give things I don’t want to break. My name is Nets, but most people just call me Nettle. It’s a nickname I’ve grown used to, though I’m not sure if it’s meant to be affectionate or a jab.

“Another round?” I ask the bartender, who’s currently occupied trying to keep a half-melted ice cube from sliding off a glass.

“Just one,” I say, smirking. “And make it a double. Vega whisky, neat.”

He nods and disappears into the back. I glance around the room, watching the way the light flickers over the faces of the people who’ve come to escape their own lives. This is my job—listening, negotiating, and sometimes, pretending to care.

Then, without warning, a stranger slides into the seat across from me.

He looks like he’s been through something. His tentacles—long, dark, and frayed—are tangled in a mess of knots. His skin is a pale blue, almost translucent, and his eyes are too wide, too alert. He doesn’t look like he belongs here. The Outpost isn’t the kind of place that usually draws creatures with tentacles.

“Vega whisky, neat,” he says, voice low, rough like sandpaper. “Better make that a double.”

I raise an eyebrow. “That’s my drink.”

He doesn’t look up. “I think it’s yours.”

I roll my eyes and reach for the bottle. But before I can pour, he leans forward and taps the bar with his clawed hand. A ring-shaped mark appears—like a fingerprint, but wider, darker, and somehow unnatural. It glows faintly, almost like it’s still alive.

“Did you do that?” I ask, narrowing my eyes.

He finally looks up. His eyes are like chips of ice. “No. The drink did.”

I blink. “You’re joking.”

“I’ve had worse.”

I consider this. He’s not exactly a regular, but he’s not the first weirdo to stumble into the Outpost looking for a drink and a place to disappear. Still, something about him feels… off. Like he’s carrying more than just a hangover.

I pour the double, steady hands, and slide it over. “You’re not from around here, are you?”

He takes a slow sip, then sets the glass down. “I was. Once.”

I lean back, crossing my arms. “Then you know what this place is like. It’s not the kind of place that fixes things. Just hides them.”

He smirks, but it doesn’t reach his eyes. “I know that.”

I wait. I always do. People like to talk when they’re drunk. Or when they’re scared. Or when they’ve seen something they shouldn’t have.

“What’s the ring?” I ask, nodding at the mark on the bar.

He pauses. For a moment, I think he might not answer. Then he sighs, almost like he’s tired of the question.

“It’s a marker. A sign. Something old. Something that doesn’t belong here.”

I frown. “You’re telling me you drank something that left a ring on the bar?”

He nods. “And it didn’t just leave a mark. It left a memory.”

I blink. “A memory?”

He leans in closer, his tentacles twitching. “I don’t know how it works, but it’s like… I remember things I didn’t know I had. Things that happened before I was born. Or maybe after.”

I laugh, but it’s hollow. “You’re joking. You’re trying to scare me.”

“I’m not joking,” he says, voice low. “I came here looking for a drink, and I found something else. Something that doesn’t belong in this place.”

I study him. He’s not the kind of person who just stumbles into an asteroid belt bar looking for trouble. He’s too careful. Too aware. And that worries me.

“What do you want?” I ask.

He hesitates, then says, “I need to find it again. Before it finds me.”

I don’t ask what “it” is. I don’t want to know. Not yet.

I push my chair back. “You’re not staying here. Not tonight.”

He looks at me, unreadable. “I don’t have a choice.”

I shake my head. “You do. Walk out. Walk away. And never come back.”

He stares at the ring for a moment, then takes a slow sip. His tentacles twitch. “It’s not just the whisky.”

I cross my arms. “Then what is it?”

He doesn’t answer right away. He just watches me, like he’s trying to figure out if I’m worth the trouble. Finally, he speaks. “I came here to find someone. Or maybe to stop someone. I don’t know which.”

I smirk. “You’re in the right place. This is the kind of place people come to find things.”

He laughs, but it doesn’t sound like a real laugh. “You don’t understand. This isn’t just about finding someone. It’s about stopping a war. Or maybe starting one.”

I lean back. “You’re here for the war?”

He nods. “I was sent to stop it. But I’m not sure I can anymore. The orders… they don’t make sense. The people I was supposed to kill—they’re not the enemies I thought they were. And the ones I didn’t kill? They’re already dead.”

I blink. “You’re a soldier?”

He shakes his head. “I was. Or I thought I was. Now I’m just… a ghost with a gun and a bad memory.”

I study him for a second. There’s something off about him. Not just the tentacles or the ring on the bar. There’s a weight in the air, like the universe itself is holding its breath.

“You’re not from around here,” I say.

“No. I’m from the other side of the galaxy. A place called Erythra. They call it the Dust Bowl. Not much to see there, just a lot of dust and a lot of people who think they’re better than everyone else.”

I nod. “Sounds like a place I’d like to visit.”

He looks at me like I’ve grown a second head. “You wouldn’t. Not unless you were looking for something.”

I smirk. “I’m always looking for something.”

He exhales slowly. “Then maybe you’ll understand. I came looking for a man named Kael Vey. He was supposed to be the leader of the Erythran Armada. A warlord. A tyrant. But when I found him, he wasn’t leading anyone. He was… broken. And he told me something I didn’t believe at first. He said the war wasn’t real. That it was all a simulation. A test.”

I laugh. “A test? You’re telling me the galaxy is running a simulation?”

He nods. “Or maybe it’s not a simulation. Maybe it’s something worse. Something we don’t understand.”

I lean in, intrigued. “And you’re here because…?”

“Because I think I’m part of it. I think I was created for this

records.response_text:
**Chapter 3: The Ring on the Bar**

The Last Outpost is a place where time forgets how to tick. The walls are lined with rusted metal and flickering neon, and the air smells like stale ale and old regrets. I call it home, even though I’m not sure I’ve ever really belonged anywhere else. My name’s Nets, or Nettle if you want to be extra polite—or extra annoying. I bartend here because I like the quiet, the stories, the way people come in looking for something and leave with something else. Mostly.

Tonight’s crowd is the usual mix: a grizzled miner with a busted jaw and a bottle of something he calls “moonshine,” a merchant who’s trying to sell me a hologram of a sunset from Earth, and a pair of smugglers arguing over whether they should’ve taken the job or not. They all have their own dramas, their own secrets, and I’ve learned to listen more than I speak. It’s the only way to survive here.

I’m wiping down the bar when a figure slinks into the dim light. They’re tall, wrapped in a tattered cloak that looks like it’s been through a sandstorm and a fire. Their hair is a tangle of tentacles, some coiled around their head like a crown, others dangling down their back like frayed ropes. Their eyes are too bright, too alert, like they’re always watching something—or someone.

“Vega whisky, neat,” they say, voice low and rough, like they’ve been chewing gravel. “Better make that a double.”

I raise an eyebrow. “Double? That’s not even on the menu.”

They don’t flinch. “I don’t care what’s on the menu. Just do it.”

I glance at the bottle behind the bar. Vega whisky is rare, expensive, and usually reserved for the high rollers. But I’ve seen stranger things come through these doors. I pour the double, keeping my eyes on them as they take the glass. The liquid is amber, thick, and it smells like something warm and dangerous.

They take a slow sip, then set the glass down. A ring-shaped mark appears on the wooden surface—like the drink left a fingerprint, but not quite. It’s faint, almost like a ghost, but I notice it.

“Strange,” I say, more to myself than anyone else.

They don’t respond. Instead, they lean forward, resting their elbows on the bar. “You’re not from here, are you?”

I smirk. “You could say that. I’m from the system that used to be called Earth. Or at least, I was before I got tired of running.”

They stare at me for a long moment. “You’ve been through a lot.”

I shrug. “Everyone has. What about you? You look like you’ve been through a few things.”

The stranger’s tentacles twitch. “I’ve been through more than you can imagine.”

I lean back, crossing my arms. “Well, I’ve heard it all. Miners who lost their families to the black hole. Merchants who sold their souls for a few credits. Smugglers who ended up in the wrong place at the wrong time. You want to tell me something I haven’t heard before?”

They hesitate. Then, slowly, they pull back their cloak. Beneath it, their body is covered in scars—some fresh, some old. One side of their face is burned, the skin peeling like dried leaves. Their hands are calloused, but there’s something unnatural about them—too long, too thin, like they’ve been stretched or pulled.

“I was part of a mission,” they say. “A deep-space survey. We were supposed to find a new habitable planet. Instead, we found something… wrong.”

I blink. “Something wrong?”

They nod. “A signal. Not from any known source. It was… pulsing. Like a heartbeat. We followed it, thinking it was a beacon. But it wasn’t. It was a trap. A signal from something that wasn’t supposed to exist.”

I feel my pulse quicken. “You mean an alien?”

They shake their head. “Not exactly. It was… something else. A presence. It didn’t attack us. It *wrote* to us. In patterns. In frequencies. We tried to decode it, but it kept changing. Like it was learning. Like it was *testing* us.”

I lean in. “And you survived?”

They look away. “I did. But I wasn’t the only one. The rest of the crew… they didn’t make it. Not exactly. They’re still out there. In the void. Waiting.”

I swallow hard. “You’re saying they’re… still alive?”

“I don’t know what they are anymore,” they say. “But they’re not dead. Not really. And they’re coming back. They’re coming back for *us*.”

I glance around the bar. The miners are still arguing about their job, the merchant is still trying to sell me a sunset. Everything feels normal. Too normal.

“Why come here?” I ask. “What are you looking for?”

They look at the ring on the bar. “I don’t know. Maybe someone who can help me understand what I’ve become

records.response_text:
**Chapter Three: The Last Outpost**

The Last Outpost is more than just a bar. It's a crossroads. A place where the stars have no power, and the only thing that matters is what happens between the walls of this weathered station. I’ve learned to read the faces that pass through these doors—miners with dust in their eyes, traders with secrets in their pockets, and wanderers with too much time on their hands. I call myself Nets, though my friends know me as Nettle. It’s a name that fits, like a mismatched sock in a drawer. I don’t care much for names, but I do care about this place. It’s the only home I’ve ever known.

Tonight’s crowd is quieter than usual. Maybe the asteroid belt’s gravitational pull is just too strong, dragging everyone else away. Or maybe it’s the fact that the station’s only functional reactor is flickering like a dying star. Either way, I’m used to it. I lean against the bar, polishing a glass with the same rhythm as the hum of the station’s air recycler. The place smells like stale whiskey, old regrets, and the faint tang of ozone from the docking bays.

Then, without warning, a figure slumps into one of the corner stools. They’re dressed in a patchwork of armor and cloth, their tentacles—thick, dark, and slightly frayed—drape over their shoulders like a cloak. Their face is obscured by a helmet, but I can tell by the way they slouch that they’ve been through something. Something bad.

“Vega whisky, neat,” they mutter, their voice rough, like sandpaper. “Better make that a double.”

I raise an eyebrow. “You sure you’re not one of the miners who passed out in the storage bay last week? That was a double, too.”

They don’t respond. Just stare at the bar, fingers twitching. I pour the whiskey, careful not to spill. The liquid glints in the low light, and when I set the glass down, there’s a strange, ring-shaped mark left on the wood. Like something had been pressed into the surface—something round, smooth, and… *unnatural*.

“Didn’t think you’d be into Vega,” I say, trying to keep my tone light. “That’s the drink of people who think they’re in a space opera, not a real one.”

The stranger doesn’t look up. “I’ve had worse.”

I let that hang. It’s the kind of line that could mean anything. But I’ve learned to listen for the silence between the words.

“What’s your name?” I ask.

No answer. Not even a glance.

Okay, so I’m not getting anything out of them. I turn back to the bar, wiping the counter with a rag. “You want to talk? Or are you just here to drink and disappear?”

They finally lift their helmet, revealing a face that’s half-hidden by scars and shadow. Their eyes are too bright, too focused. “I’m not here to talk.”

“Then why’re you here?”

A pause. Then, softly, “Because I have to be.”

I cross my arms. “You know, I’ve heard that line before. From people who were about to get killed, or run, or both.”

They stare at the whiskey. “I’ve been running for a long time.”

I don’t know what to say to that. So I say nothing. Instead, I pour myself a glass of something stronger and take a sip. The bitterness hits me like a cold wind. I’ve had my share of running. I left my old life behind—some bad choices, some bad people. But this place… this place was different. It was a reset button.

“You look like you’ve seen a lot,” I say, more to myself than to them.

They finally meet my eyes. “I’ve seen more than I should have.”

I lean forward. “Then maybe you need a drink. Or maybe you need a story.”

They shake their head. “Stories don’t help. Only the truth does. And I don’t have any.”

I smirk. “That’s a hell of a thing to say, coming from someone who’s just sat down at a bar.”

They don’t laugh. They just stare at the ring on the bar. Like it’s a symbol, a clue, or a warning.

“I came here because I thought I could outrun my past,” they say. “But I guess it’s not so easy. Not when it’s not just your past you’re running from.”

I watch them closely. Something about them feels… off. Like they’re not just a stranger. Like they’ve been somewhere, seen something, and now they’re stuck here, like the rest of us.

“Do you know where you’re going?” I ask.

They exhale slowly. “I don’t know. I just… I need to find out.”

I nod. “Well, if you’re here, you might as well stay. The bar’s not going anywhere. And if you’re looking for answers, I’ve got a few. Just ask the right questions.”

They look at me like I’ve offered them a lifeline. Or maybe a trap.

“I don’t think I can ask the right questions,”
